topics.title: Continuous time and discrete time systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEOT522 - INTRODUCTION TO SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Continuous time and discrete time systems # 📘 SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition of a System In **Signals and Systems** terminology, a **system** is a mathematical model or physical device that responds to an input signal by producing an output signal. Formally, a system is an operator or transformation that maps an input signal $x(t)$ (or $x[n]$) to an output signal $y(t)$ (or $y[n]$). Mathematically, a continuous-time (CT) system can be represented as: $$y(t) = T\{x(t)\}$$ and a discrete-time (DT) system as: $$y[n] = T\{x[n]\}$$ where $T\{\cdot\}$ denotes the system transformation operator. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** A system is essentially a **function-of-a-function** — it takes a signal as input and processes it to produce another signal. Everything from an electrical circuit to a software filter is a "system." ## 1.2 Continuous-Time (CT) vs Discrete-Time (DT) Systems | Feature | Continuous-Time System | Discrete-Time System | |---|---|---| | **Input/Output Domain** | $x(t), y(t)$ defined for all real $t$ | $x[n], y[n]$ defined only at integer $n$ | | **Independent Variable** | Continuous $t \in \mathbb{R}$ | Discrete $n \in \mathbb{Z}$ | | **Examples** | RC circuit, analog filter, motor | Digital filter, DSP processor, microcontroller | | **Representation** | Differential equations | Difference equations | > [!NOTE] > **Continuous-Time System:** A system whose inputs and outputs are continuous-time signals, defined for every instant of time. > > **Discrete-Time System:** A system whose inputs and outputs are discrete-time signals, defined only at discrete instants (typically integer values of $n$). ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Vending Machine" Model Imagine a **vending machine** as a system: - **Input signal:** The coins you insert + button pressed. - **Output signal:** The snack that drops. - **System transformation ($T\{\cdot\}$):** The internal mechanism of the machine. - **Continuous-time** is like a **water tap** — water flows continuously, and you can sample it at *any* instant. - **Discrete-time** is like a **photocopier printing pages** — pages are produced only at discrete moments (page 1, page 2, ...), not continuously. ## 1.4 Block Diagram Representation The standard representation of any system is a **block** with an input arrow and output arrow: $$x(t) \longrightarrow \boxed{T\{\cdot\}} \longrightarrow y(t)$$ > [!TIP] > This convention is universal in KTU board exams. Always draw the block with the system operator name inside (e.g., $T$, $H$, $L$). # 📘 SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Systems (The KTU Big 6) In the **KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1**, every system is classified along **six canonical properties**. Board exam questions frequently ask you to identify which properties a given system satisfies. ### 2.1.1 Static vs Dynamic (Memoryless vs With Memory) - **Static System (Memoryless):** Output at any instant depends *only* on the input at the *same* instant. - **Dynamic System (With Memory):** Output depends on past or future values of input. **Examples:** - $y(t) = 2x(t)$ → **Static** - $y(t) = x(t-1)$ → **Dynamic** (1-second delay) - $y[n] = x[n] + x[n-1]$ → **Dynamic** ### 2.1.2 Linear vs Non-Linear A system is **linear** if it satisfies the **Superposition Principle**, which combines: 1. **Additivity:** $T\{x_1 + x_2\} = T\{x_1\} + T\{x_2\}$ 2. **Homogeneity (Scaling):** $T\{a \cdot x\} = a \cdot T\{x\}$ Combined as: $T\{a_1 x_1 + a_2 x_2\} = a_1 T\{x_1\} + a_2 T\{x_2\}$ for any constants $a_1, a_2$. > [!IMPORTANT] > If a system contains any **nonlinear operation** (square root, square, logarithm, absolute value, signum, multiplication of two inputs, trigonometric functions), it is **automatically nonlinear**. ### 2.1.3 Time-Invariant vs Time-Variant - **Time-Invariant (TI):** A shift in the input signal causes an identical shift in the output signal. - **Time-Variant (TV):** System parameters change with time. **Test:** Shift input by $t_0$, get $y_1(t) = T\{x(t-t_0)\}$. Then shift the output $y(t)$ by $t_0$, get $y_2(t) = y(t-t_0)$. - If $y_1(t) = y_2(t)$ for all $t_0$ → **Time-Invariant** - If $y_1(t) \neq y_2(t)$ → **Time-Variant** ### 2.1.4 Causal vs Non-Causal - **Causal System:** Output depends only on **present and past** inputs (never future). - **Non-Causal System:** Output depends on **future** inputs. > [!NOTE] > All **real-time physical systems** must be causal. Non-causal systems can only be implemented offline (with stored data, e.g., image processing). ### 2.1.5 Stable vs Unstable (BIBO Stability) - **Stable (Bounded-Input Bounded-Output):** Every bounded input produces a bounded output. - **Unstable:** Some bounded input produces an unbounded output. Mathematically: If $\vert x(t) \vert < M_x < \infty$, then $\vert y(t) \vert < M_y < \infty$ for all $t$. ### 2.1.6 Invertible vs Non-Invertible - **Invertible:** Distinct inputs produce distinct outputs (system has a unique inverse). - **Non-Invertible:** Two or more different inputs produce the same output. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Property | Test Condition | Static/Memoryless | Dynamic/With Memory | |---|---|---|---| | **Linearity** | Superposition: $T\{a_1 x_1 + a_2 x_2\} = a_1 y_1 + a_2 y_2$ | — | — | | **Time-Invariance** | $T\{x(t-t_0)\} = y(t-t_0)$ | — | — | | **Causality** | Output uses only $x(\tau), \tau \leq t$ | Usually causal | May be causal/non-causal | | **Stability (BIBO)** | Bounded $x$ $\Rightarrow$ Bounded $y$ | — | — | | **Static** | $y(t)$ depends on $x(t)$ only | ✓ | — | | **Invertibility** | $\exists$ unique $T^{-1}$ such that $T^{-1}\{y\}=x$ | — | — | ## 2.3 Interconnection of Systems KTU often tests **interconnected systems** — the block diagram below shows three fundamental interconnections: $$H_1 \xrightarrow{\text{cascade}} H_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad H_{eq} = H_1 \cdot H_2$$ For **parallel** interconnection: $$H_{eq} = H_1 + H_2$$ For **feedback** interconnection (negative feedback): $$H_{eq} = \frac{H_1}{1 + H_1 H_2}$$ ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility | System Property | Engineering Application | |---|---| | **Linear + TI (LTI)** | Foundation of all digital filter design (FIR, IIR) | | **Causal** | Real-time audio/video processing, control systems | | **Stable** | Every commercial amplifier, controller, and filter | | **Memoryless** | Resistors in circuits (instantaneous voltage-current) | > [!TIP] > In **KTU board exams**, LTI systems are the most heavily tested because they allow elegant analysis using convolution, Fourier, and Z-transforms. # 📘 SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Problems ## 3.1 Problem A: Complete Classification of $y[n] = 2x[n] + 3x[n-1]$ **Step 1: Static or Dynamic?** The output $y[n]$ depends on $x[n]$ and $x[n-1]$ (a past input). Therefore, the system has **memory** and is **Dynamic (with memory)**. **Step 2: Linear or Non-Linear?** Apply the superposition test. Let input be $x_1[n]$, output be: $$y_1[n] = 2x_1[n] + 3x_1[n-1]$$ Let input be $x_2[n]$, output be: $$y_2[n] = 2x_2[n] + 3x_2[n-1]$$ For combined input $x_3[n] = a_1 x_1[n] + a_2 x_2[n]$, the actual output is: $$ \begin{aligned} y_3[n] &= 2(a_1 x_1[n] + a_2 x_2[n]) + 3(a_1 x_1[n-1] + a_2 x_2[n-1]) \\ &= a_1(2x_1[n] + 3x_1[n-1]) + a_2(2x_2[n] + 3x_2[n-1]) \\ &= a_1 y_1[n] + a_2 y_2[n] \end{aligned} $$ Since superposition holds, the system is **Linear**. ✓ **Step 3: Time-Invariant or Time-Variant?** Apply a shift by $n_0$ to the input: $x[n - n_0]$. The output becomes: $$y_1[n] = 2x[n-n_0] + 3x[n-1-n_0]$$ Now shift the original output $y[n]$ by $n_0$: $$y_2[n] = y[n-n_0] = 2x[n-n_0] + 3x[n-1-n_0]$$ Since $y_1[n] = y_2[n]$, the system is **Time-Invariant**. ✓ **Step 4: Causal or Non-Causal?** The output $y[n]$ uses $x[n]$ (present) and $x[n-1]$ (past). No future value of input is used. The system is **Causal**. ✓ **Step 5: Stable (BIBO)?** Assume input is bounded: $\vert x[n] \vert \leq M < \infty$. Then: $$ \begin{aligned} \vert y[n] \vert &= \vert 2x[n] + 3x[n-1] \vert \\ &\leq 2\vert x[n] \vert + 3\vert x[n-1] \vert \\ &\leq 2M + 3M = 5M \end{aligned} $$ The output is bounded. The system is **Stable (BIBO)**. ✓ **Step 6: Invertible?** The system is a 2-tap linear filter. Given $y[n]$, we can recover $x[n] = \frac{y[n] - 3x[n-1]}{2}$ recursively (knowing initial conditions). The system is **Invertible**. ✓ **Final Classification:** Linear, Time-Invariant, Causal, Stable, Dynamic, Invertible. (**LTI-CSI**) --- ## 3.2 Problem B: Classification of $y(t) = \sin(x(t))$ **Step 1: Static or Dynamic?** $y(t)$ depends only on $x(t)$ at the same instant — **Static (Memoryless)**. **Step 2: Linear or Non-Linear?** Test with $x(t) = c \cdot x_1(t)$: $$T\{c \cdot x_1(t)\} = \sin(c \cdot x_1(t)) \neq c \cdot \sin(x_1(t)) = c \cdot T\{x_1(t)\}$$ Homogeneity fails. System is **Non-Linear**. ✗ **Step 3: Time-Invariant or Time-Variant?** Since no explicit time $t$ appears in the system operator, the operation is shift-invariant. Apply shift $t_0$: $$T\{x(t-t_0)\} = \sin(x(t-t_0)) = y(t-t_0)$$ System is **Time-Invariant**. ✓ **Step 4: Causal?** Depends only on present input — **Causal**. ✓ **Step 5: Stable (BIBO)?** Since $\vert \sin(\cdot) \vert \leq 1$, the output is always bounded for any input. System is **Stable**. ✓ **Final Classification:** Non-Linear, Time-Invariant, Causal, Stable, Static. --- ## 3.3 Problem C: Classification of $y(t) = t \cdot x(t)$ **Step 1: Static or Dynamic?** Only depends on $x(t)$ at the same instant — **Static**. **Step 2: Linear?** $$T\{a_1 x_1 + a_2 x_2\} = t(a_1 x_1 + a_2 x_2) = a_1(t x_1) + a_2(t x_2) = a_1 y_1 + a_2 y_2$$ **Linear**. ✓ **Step 3: Time-Invariant or Time-Variant?** Apply input shift $x(t-t_0)$: $$y_1(t) = T\{x(t-t_0)\} = t \cdot x(t-t_0)$$ Now shift the output: $$y_2(t) = y(t-t_0) = (t-t_0) \cdot x(t-t_0)$$ Clearly, $y_1(t) = t \cdot x(t-t_0) \neq (t-t_0) \cdot x(t-t_0) = y_2(t)$. **Time-Variant**. ✗ **Step 4: Causal?** Depends on present input only — **Causal**. ✓ **Step 5: Stable (BIBO)?** For bounded input $\vert x(t) \vert \leq M$, the output magnitude is $\vert t \cdot x(t) \vert$, which grows without bound as $t \to \infty$. **Unstable**. ✗ **Final Classification:** Linear, Time-Variant, Causal, Unstable, Static. > [!TIP] > The explicit appearance of the independent variable ($t$ or $n$) as a multiplicative factor in the system equation is the classic signature of a **time-variant** system. # 📘 SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Classification Hierarchy of Systems ```mermaid flowchart TD A["SYSTEM CLASSIFICATION"] --> B["BY DOMAIN"] A --> C["BY PROPERTIES"] B --> B1["CONTINUOUS TIME SYSTEM
Input x of t Output y of t"] B --> B2["DISCRETE TIME SYSTEM
Input x of n Output y of n"] B --> B3["HYBRID SYSTEM
Mixed CT and DT blocks"] C --> P1["STATIC
Memoryless"] C --> P2["DYNAMIC
With Memory"] C --> P3["LINEAR
Superposition Holds"] C --> P4["NON LINEAR
Superposition Fails"] C --> P5["TIME INVARIANT
Parameters Constant"] C --> P6["TIME VARIANT
Parameters Change"] C --> P7["CAUSAL
No Future Input"] C --> P8["NON CAUSAL
Uses Future Input"] C --> P9["BIBO STABLE
Bounded In Bounded Out"] C --> P10["UNSTABLE
Bounded In Unbounded Out"] C --> P11["INVERTIBLE
Unique Inverse Exists"] C --> P12["NON INVERTIBLE
Multiple Inputs Same Output"] B1 --> EX1["Examples
RC Circuit
Analog Filter"] B2 --> EX2["Examples
Digital Filter
Microcontroller"] P3 --> LTI["LTI SYSTEM
Linear AND Time Invariant
Most Important Class"] P5 --> LTI ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Flow — System Interconnection Topologies ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CASCADE["CASCADE INTERCONNECTION"] X1["x"] --> H1A["H1 block"] H1A --> H2A["H2 block"] H2A --> Y1["y"] end subgraph PARALLEL["PARALLEL INTERCONNECTION"] X2["x"] --> H1B["H1 block"] X2 --> H2B["H2 block"] H1B --> SUM["Adder"] H2B --> SUM SUM --> Y2["y"] end subgraph FEEDBACK["FEEDBACK INTERCONNECTION"] X3["x"] --> SUM3["Summing Junction"] SUM3 --> HF["Forward H"] HF --> Y3["y"] Y3 --> HB["Feedback H"] HB --> NEG["Negative Sign"] NEG --> SUM3 end ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — CT vs DT System Processing Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph CT_PIPELINE["CONTINUOUS TIME PIPELINE"] CT_IN["Analog Source Signal"] --> CT_FILTER["Analog Filter
RC or RLC Circuit"] CT_FILTER --> CT_AMP["Amplifier Stage"] CT_AMP --> CT_OUT["Analog Output
Speaker or Motor"] end subgraph DT_PIPELINE["DISCRETE TIME PIPELINE"] DT_IN["Analog Input"] --> ADC["ADC
Sampler and Quantizer"] ADC --> DT_PROC["Digital Processor
DSP or Microcontroller"] DT_PROC --> DAC["DAC
Reconstruction Filter"] DAC --> DT_OUT["Analog Output"] end DT_PROC --> MATH["MATHEMATICAL CORE
Difference Equation
y of n equals f of x of n and past x"] ``` # 📘 SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1:** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define a continuous-time system and a discrete-time system with one example each.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A **continuous-time system** is one in which the input and output signals are defined for all real values of time $t$. The system maps $x(t) \to y(t)$ through an operator $T\{\cdot\}$. **Example:** An RC low-pass filter circuit. A **discrete-time system** is one in which the input and output signals are defined only at discrete instants, typically integer values of $n$. The system maps $x[n] \to y[n]$. **Example:** A digital signal processor implementing an FIR filter. --- ### **Question 2:** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` **State the condition for a system to be BIBO stable.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** A system is **BIBO (Bounded-Input Bounded-Output) stable** if every bounded input produces a bounded output. Mathematically: If $\vert x(t) \vert < \infty$ for all $t$, then $\vert y(t) \vert < \infty$ for all $t$. Equivalently, if there exists a finite constant $M_x$ such that $\vert x(t) \vert \leq M_x$, then there must exist a finite constant $M_y$ such that $\vert y(t) \vert \leq M_y$ for all $t$. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) ### ✍️ **Question A (Choice 1):** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]` **Check whether the following systems are (i) Linear/Non-linear, (ii) Time-invariant/Time-variant, (iii) Causal/Non-causal, (iv) Stable/Unstable:** **(a)** $y(t) = x(t) \cdot \cos(\omega_0 t)$ where $\omega_0$ is a constant. **(b)** $y[n] = n \cdot x[n] + x[n-1]$. --- ### 🔹 Model Solution for Part (a) — $y(t) = x(t) \cos(\omega_0 t)$ **(i) Linearity:** Let $x(t) = a_1 x_1(t) + a_2 x_2(t)$: $$ \begin{aligned} y(t) &= [a_1 x_1(t) + a_2 x_2(t)] \cos(\omega_0 t) \\ &= a_1 x_1(t)\cos(\omega_0 t) + a_2 x_2(t)\cos(\omega_0 t) \\ &= a_1 y_1(t) + a_2 y_2(t) \end{aligned} $$ **Linear.** ✓ **(ii) Time-Invariance:** Shift input by $t_0$: $$y_1(t) = x(t-t_0)\cos(\omega_0 t)$$ Shift output by $t_0$: $$y_2(t) = y(t-t_0) = x(t-t_0)\cos(\omega_0(t-t_0))$$ Since $\cos(\omega_0 t) \neq \cos(\omega_0(t-t_0))$, we have $y_1(t) \neq y_2(t)$. **Time-Variant.** ✗ **(iii) Causality:** Output depends on $x(t)$ at the present time only. **Causal.** ✓ **(iv) Stability:** If $\vert x(t) \vert \leq M$, then $\vert y(t) \vert = \vert x(t) \vert \cdot \vert \cos(\omega_0 t) \vert \leq M \cdot 1 = M$. **Stable.** ✓ --- ### 🔹 Model Solution for Part (b) — $y[n] = n \cdot x[n] + x[n-1]$ **(i) Linearity:** $$ \begin{aligned} T\{a_1 x_1[n] + a_2 x_2[n]\} &= n(a_1 x_1[n] + a_2 x_2[n]) + a_1 x_1[n-1] + a_2 x_2[n-1] \\ &= a_1(n x_1[n] + x_1[n-1]) + a_2(n x_2[n] + x_2[n-1]) \\ &= a_1 y_1[n] + a_2 y_2[n] \end{aligned} $$ **Linear.** ✓ **(ii) Time-Invariance:** $$y_1[n] = T\{x[n-n_0]\} = n \cdot x[n-n_0] + x[n-1-n_0]$$ $$y_2[n] = y[n-n_0] = (n-n_0) x[n-n_0] + x[n-1-n_0]$$ $y_1[n] \neq y_2[n]$. **Time-Variant.** ✗ **(iii) Causality:** Depends on $x[n]$ (present) and $x[n-1]$ (past). **Causal.** ✓ **(iv) Stability:** For bounded input $\vert x[n] \vert \leq M$: $$\vert y[n] \vert = \vert n \cdot x[n] + x[n-1] \vert \leq \vert n \vert M + M$$ As $n \to \infty$, this becomes unbounded. **Unstable.** ✗ --- ### ✍️ **Question B (Choice 2):** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **For each of the systems below, determine whether the system is (a) Static/Dynamic, (b) Linear/Non-linear, (c) Causal/Non-causal, (d) Time-invariant/Time-variant.** **(a)** $y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau) \, d\tau$ **(b)** $y[n] = x[-n]$ --- ### 🔹 Model Solution for Part (a) — $y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau) d\tau$ (Accumulator/Integrator) **(a) Static/Dynamic:** The output at time $t$ depends on the entire past history of $x(\tau)$ from $-\infty$ to $t$. **Dynamic.** **(b) Linear/Non-Linear:** For input $a_1 x_1 + a_2 x_2$: $$ \begin{aligned} y(t) &= \int_{-\infty}^{t} [a_1 x_1(\tau) + a_2 x_2(\tau)] d\tau \\ &= a_1 \int_{-\infty}^{t} x_1(\tau) d\tau + a_2 \int_{-\infty}^{t} x_2(\tau) d\tau \\ &= a_1 y_1(t) + a_2 y_2(t) \end{aligned} $$ **Linear.** ✓ **(c) Causal/Non-Causal:** Output depends only on past values of input ($\tau \leq t$). **Causal.** ✓ **(d) Time-Invariant:** $$T\{x(t-t_0)\} = \int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau-t_0) d\tau = \int_{-\infty}^{t-t_0} x(u) du = y(t-t_0)$$ **Time-Invariant.** ✓ --- ### 🔹 Model Solution for Part (b) — $y[n] = x[-n]$ (Time Reversal) **(a) Static/Dynamic:** Output depends on input at time $-n$. **Dynamic** (with memory). **(b) Linear/Non-Linear:** $$T\{a_1 x_1[n] + a_2 x_2[n]\} = a_1 x_1[-n] + a_2 x_2[-n] = a_1 y_1[n] + a_2 y_2[n]$$ **Linear.** ✓ **(c) Causal/Non-Causal:** For $n = -1$: $y[-1] = x[1]$ — depends on **future** input. **Non-Causal.** ✗ **(d) Time-Invariant:** $$T\{x[n-n_0]\} = x[-n-n_0]$$ $$y[n-n_0] = x[-(n-n_0)] = x[-n+n_0]$$ These are not equal in general. **Time-Variant.** ✗ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing Linearity with Time-Invariance:** Students often declare a system "linear and time-invariant" without testing both conditions separately. Each must be rigorously tested. > 2. **Forgetting the Boundedness Test in Stability:** Showing stability requires explicit bounding (use triangle inequality: $\vert a+b \vert \leq \vert a \vert + \vert b \vert$). > 3. **Treating explicit time ($t$ or $n$) as a constant:** Any explicit appearance of the independent variable as a multiplicative factor or coefficient makes the system **time-variant**. > 4. **Static ≠ Linear:** A system being memoryless does not guarantee linearity (e.g., $y(t) = x^2(t)$ is static but non-linear). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) - ✅ A **system** maps input signals to output signals via an operator $T\{\cdot\}$. - ✅ **CT systems** use $x(t), y(t)$; **DT systems** use $x[n], y[n]$. - ✅ **Static (memoryless):** $y(t)$ depends only on $x(t)$; **Dynamic:** depends on past/future values. - ✅ **Linear:** obeys superposition ($T\{a_1 x_1 + a_2 x_2\} = a_1 y_1 + a_2 y_2$). - ✅ **Time-Invariant:** $T\{x(t-t_0)\} = y(t-t_0)$; otherwise time-variant. - ✅ **Causal:** output uses only present and past inputs; never future. - ✅ **BIBO Stable:** bounded input ⇒ bounded output (test using triangle inequality). - ✅ **Invertible:** distinct inputs produce distinct outputs. - ✅ **LTI Systems** = Linear + Time-Invariant (most important class in DSP). - ✅ **Interconnections:** Cascade → multiply; Parallel → add; Feedback → $\frac{H_1}{1+H_1 H_2}$ (negative feedback). - ✅ System property mnemonics: **S**uperposition for **L**inearity; **S**hift test for **T**ime-invariance; **F**uture check for **C**ausality.

topics.title: Properties of systems: time invariance, linearity, causality, systems with and without memory, and stability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEOT522 - INTRODUCTION TO SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Properties of systems: time invariance, linearity, causality, systems with and without memory, and stability # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What is a System? In the context of **Signals and Systems**, a **system** is any physical, mathematical, or computational process that takes an input signal $x(t)$ (or $x[n]$) and transforms it into an output signal $y(t)$ (or $y[n]$). Formally, a system is an operator $T\{\cdot\}$ that maps an input signal from its domain to an output signal in its range. Mathematically, this is expressed as: $$y(t) = T\{x(t)\} \quad \text{(Continuous-Time System)}$$ $$y[n] = T\{x[n]\} \quad \text{(Discrete-Time System)}$$ The operator $T\{\cdot\}$ is a transformation rule, and the **properties of systems** (time invariance, linearity, causality, memory, and stability) are the fundamental classifications that govern how $T\{\cdot\}$ behaves for different inputs. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** > Students must master **FIVE** core system properties: > 1. **Time Invariance** > 2. **Linearity** (Superposition + Homogeneity) > 3. **Causality** > 4. **Memory** (with memory vs. memoryless) > 5. **Stability** (BIBO Stability) ## 1.2 Real-World Intuition (The "Factory" Analogy) Imagine a system as a **black-box factory** processing raw material: - **Input $x(t)$** → Raw material entering the factory - **System $T\{\cdot\}$** → The factory machinery and processing rules - **Output $y(t)$** → Finished product leaving the factory | System Property | Factory Analogy | Plain English Meaning | |----------------|-----------------|----------------------| | **Time Invariance** | Factory rules don't change with shift time | A delay or shift in input produces the same shift in output | | **Linearity** | Production scales linearly with input | Doubling input doubles output; adding inputs adds outputs | | **Causality** | Output never depends on tomorrow's deliveries | Output at any time depends only on present/past inputs | | **Memory** | Some factories need history; others don't | Output may depend on past/future values of input | | **Stability** | Factory never explodes regardless of input size | Bounded input always produces bounded output | > [!NOTE] > **Why Study These Properties?** > Understanding system properties allows engineers to **predict behavior**, **simplify analysis**, and **design reliable circuits, filters, and control systems**. For example, an unstable amplifier in a feedback loop can destroy a speaker; a non-causal filter cannot be implemented in real-time DSP. ## 1.3 Formal Definitions Snapshot > [!IMPORTANT] > **Definition Box — The Five Properties at a Glance** - **Time-Invariant System:** If $y(t) = T\{x(t)\}$, then $T\{x(t-t_0)\} = y(t-t_0)$ for all $t_0 \in \mathbb{R}$. - **Linear System:** Satisfies $T\{a x_1(t) + b x_2(t)\} = a\,T\{x_1(t)\} + b\,T\{x_2(t)\}$ (Additivity + Scaling). - **Causal System:** Output $y(t_0)$ depends only on $x(t)$ for $t \leq t_0$. - **Memoryless System:** Output $y(t)$ depends only on the current input $x(t)$ (no past/future values). - **Stable System (BIBO):** If $\vert x(t) \vert \leq M < \infty$, then $\vert y(t) \vert < \infty$ for all $t$. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Time Invariance (TI) A system is **time-invariant** if a time shift in the input signal results in an identical time shift in the output signal. The system's behavior does **not** depend on the absolute time at which it operates. ### Test Procedure: 1. Delay the input by $t_0$: get $x(t-t_0)$. 2. Find the output to the delayed input: $T\{x(t-t_0)\}$. 3. Find the output to the original input: $y(t) = T\{x(t)\}$. 4. Delay the output by $t_0$: get $y(t-t_0)$. 5. **If $T\{x(t-t_0)\} = y(t-t_0)$ for ALL $t_0$** → System is Time-Invariant. 6. **If equality fails for ANY $t_0$** → System is Time-Variant. **Why it matters:** Time-invariant systems can be characterized by their **impulse response** alone (LTI systems). This dramatically simplifies analysis using convolution. ## 2.2 Linearity A system is **linear** if and only if it satisfies the **Principle of Superposition**, which has two parts: - **Additivity:** $T\{x_1(t) + x_2(t)\} = T\{x_1(t)\} + T\{x_2(t)\}$ - **Homogeneity (Scaling):** $T\{a \cdot x(t)\} = a \cdot T\{x(t)\}$ for any scalar $a$ These combine to the **Superposition Principle**: $$T\{a_1 x_1(t) + a_2 x_2(t)\} = a_1 T\{x_1(t)\} + a_2 T\{x_2(t)\}$$ ### Zero-In Response Test (Necessary Condition): For a linear system, the response to a **zero input** must be **zero output**: $$x(t) = 0 \implies y(t) = 0$$ If the system has a non-zero output for zero input, the system is **non-linear** (e.g., $y(t) = x(t) + 1$). > [!NOTE] > **Common Mistake in KTU Exams:** Students often confuse "linear" with "linear-in-math" (a straight-line graph). In signals and systems, **linear means superposition holds**, NOT that the input-output graph is a straight line. The system $y(t) = x^2(t)$ is **non-linear** even though it is a smooth function. ## 2.3 Causality A system is **causal** if the output at any time $t_0$ depends **only** on input values for $t \leq t_0$ (past and present), and **never** on future inputs ($t > t_0$). ### Test Procedure: Examine the system equation. If the output $y(t)$ involves $x(t+1)$, $x(t-3)$ is allowed (past), but $x(t+1)$ is **not allowed** (future). | System Equation | Causal? | Reason | |----------------|---------|--------| | $y(t) = x(t-1)$ | ✅ Yes | Depends on past input | | $y(t) = x(t+1)$ | ❌ No | Depends on future input | | $y(t) = x(t) \cdot \cos(t)$ | ✅ Yes | Only current input (time-varying coefficient is allowed) | | $y[n] = x[-n]$ | ❌ No | $n=1$ requires $x[-1]$ (future for $n<0$ is past — depends on time origin) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Rule:** All **real-time** physical systems (circuits, filters, controllers) **must be causal**. Non-causal systems are used in **offline applications** like image processing, where the entire signal is available in a buffer. ## 2.4 Memory A system is **memoryless** if the output at any time $t$ depends **only** on the input at the **same time** $t$. - **Memoryless:** $y(t) = 2x(t)$, $y(t) = x^2(t)$, $y(t) = \sin(x(t))$ - **With Memory:** $y(t) = x(t-1)$, $y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} x[k]$ (accumulator), $y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau)\,d\tau$ ## 2.5 Stability (BIBO) A system is **Bounded-Input Bounded-Output (BIBO) stable** if **every bounded input** produces a **bounded output**. **Formal definition:** If $\vert x(t) \vert \leq M_x < \infty$ for all $t$, then $\vert y(t) \vert \leq M_y < \infty$ for all $t$. **Physical constants/metrics used in stability testing:** - $M_x$: Bound on input magnitude - $M_y$: Bound on output magnitude - **Impulse response integral condition** for LTI systems: $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h(t) \vert \, dt < \infty$ ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Property | Test Condition (CT) | Test Condition (DT) | |---|----------|--------------------|--------------------| | 1 | **Time Invariance** | $T\{x(t-t_0)\} = y(t-t_0)$ | $T\{x[n-n_0]\} = y[n-n_0]$ | | 2 | **Linearity** | $T\{a_1 x_1 + a_2 x_2\} = a_1 y_1 + a_2 y_2$ | Same form with $n$ | | 3 | **Causality** | $y(t)$ depends only on $x(\tau), \tau \leq t$ | $y[n]$ depends only on $x[k], k \leq n$ | | 4 | **Memoryless** | $y(t)$ depends only on $x(t)$ | $y[n]$ depends only on $x[n]$ | | 5 | **BIBO Stability** | $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h(t) \vert \, dt < \infty$ | $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[n] \vert < \infty$ | | 6 | **Zero-in / Zero-out (Linear check)** | $x(t) = 0 \implies y(t) = 0$ | $x[n] = 0 \implies y[n] = 0$ | | 7 | **Superposition** | $T\{x_1 + x_2\} = T\{x_1\} + T\{x_2\}$ | Same | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** > - **LTI systems** (Linear + Time-Invariant) are the **backbone of DSP** because they can be fully characterized by their impulse response $h(t)$ and analyzed via convolution: $y(t) = x(t) * h(t)$. > - **BIBO stability** is critical in **feedback control systems**; an unstable system can lead to physical destruction of equipment. > - **Causality** is mandatory for **real-time radar, audio processing, and control**. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Test Linearity and Time Invariance of $y(t) = t \cdot x(t)$ > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** Test the system $y(t) = t \cdot x(t)$ for **linearity** and **time invariance**. ### Step 1: Test Linearity Apply two arbitrary inputs $x_1(t)$ and $x_2(t)$ with scalars $a$ and $b$. **Input-output relations:** $$y_1(t) = T\{x_1(t)\} = t \cdot x_1(t)$$ $$y_2(t) = T\{x_2(t)\} = t \cdot x_2(t)$$ **Apply weighted sum to input:** $$T\{a\,x_1(t) + b\,x_2(t)\} = t \cdot \big[a\,x_1(t) + b\,x_2(t)\big]$$ **Distribute the multiplication:** $$= a\,t\,x_1(t) + b\,t\,x_2(t)$$ **Apply weighted sum to outputs:** $$a\,y_1(t) + b\,y_2(t) = a\,[t\,x_1(t)] + b\,[t\,x_2(t)]$$ $$= a\,t\,x_1(t) + b\,t\,x_2(t)$$ **Compare:** Both expressions are identical. **System is LINEAR.** ✓ ### Step 2: Test Time Invariance **Step 2a:** Apply delayed input $x(t-t_0)$: $$T\{x(t-t_0)\} = t \cdot x(t-t_0)$$ **Step 2b:** Compute delayed output $y(t-t_0)$: $$y(t-t_0) = (t-t_0) \cdot x(t-t_0)$$ **Step 2c:** Compare the two results: $$T\{x(t-t_0)\} = t \cdot x(t-t_0) \quad \text{(coefficient is } t\text{)}$$ $$y(t-t_0) = (t-t_0) \cdot x(t-t_0) \quad \text{(coefficient is } t-t_0\text{)}$$ **Conclusion:** The two expressions are **not equal** for $t_0 \neq 0$. **System is TIME-VARIANT.** ✗ ## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Test Causality, Memory, and Stability of $y(t) = x(2t)$ > [!NOTE] > **Problem:** Test the system $y(t) = x(2t)$ for **causality**, **memory**, and **BIBO stability**. ### Step 1: Test Causality To compute $y(0)$, we need $x(2 \cdot 0) = x(0)$ → present value, OK. To compute $y(1)$, we need $x(2 \cdot 1) = x(2)$ → **future value** of input (since $2 > 1$). Since the output at time $t$ depends on input at time $2t$, which can be in the future, **the system is NON-CAUSAL.** ✗ ### Step 2: Test Memory The output $y(t) = x(2t)$ depends on the input at time $2t$, not at time $t$. Therefore, the output is **not** a function of the present input alone. **The system has MEMORY.** ✗ (Not memoryless) ### Step 3: Test BIBO Stability Assume input is bounded: $\vert x(t) \vert \leq M_x < \infty$ for all $t$. The output is: $$y(t) = x(2t)$$ Take the absolute value: $$\vert y(t) \vert = \vert x(2t) \vert \leq M_x$$ Since $2t$ is just a different time index, $\vert x(2t) \vert$ is still bounded by $M_x$. Therefore: $$\vert y(t) \vert \leq M_x < \infty$$ **The system is BIBO STABLE.** ✓ ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Classification of Common Systems | System | Linear? | Time-Invariant? | Causal? | Memoryless? | BIBO Stable? | |--------|---------|-----------------|---------|-------------|--------------| | $y(t) = x(t)$ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | | $y(t) = x(t-2)$ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | | $y(t) = x(t+1)$ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ | | $y(t) = t \cdot x(t)$ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | | $y(t) = x^2(t)$ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | | $y(t) = e^{x(t)}$ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | | $y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau)\,d\tau$ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ (integrator diverges) | | $y[n] = x[n] + 2x[n-1]$ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | | $y[n] = n\,x[n]$ | ✅ | ❌ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ (if $x$ bounded) | | $y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} x[n-k]$ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ❌ | ❌ (accumulator) | ## 3.4 Proof: BIBO Stability of LTI System via Impulse Response > [!NOTE] > **Theorem:** An LTI system is BIBO stable if and only if its impulse response $h(t)$ is absolutely integrable. ### Derivation For an LTI system, the output is given by the **convolution integral**: $$y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} h(\tau)\,x(t-\tau)\,d\tau$$ Take the absolute value: $$\vert y(t) \vert = \left\vert \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} h(\tau)\,x(t-\tau)\,d\tau \right\vert$$ Apply the triangle inequality: $$\vert y(t) \vert \leq \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h(\tau) \vert \cdot \vert x(t-\tau) \vert \, d\tau$$ Assume input is bounded: $\vert x(t) \vert \leq M_x$ for all $t$. Then $\vert x(t-\tau) \vert \leq M_x$: $$\vert y(t) \vert \leq M_x \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h(\tau) \vert \, d\tau$$ If the impulse response is **absolutely integrable**: $$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h(\tau) \vert \, d\tau = M_h < \infty$$ Then: $$\vert y(t) \vert \leq M_x \cdot M_h < \infty$$ **Conclusion:** Output is bounded. Hence, the LTI system is BIBO stable. $\blacksquare$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Counter-example:** For the integrator $y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau)\,d\tau$, the impulse response is $h(t) = u(t)$ (unit step). Then $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert u(t) \vert \, dt = \infty$. So the integrator is **not BIBO stable**. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram: Generic System Representation ```mermaid flowchart LR A["Input Signal x(t)"] --> B["System T{ }"] B --> C["Output Signal y(t)"] B -.-> D["Impulse Response h(t)"] D -.-> E["LTI Convolution: y(t) = x(t) * h(t)"] A -.-> F["Properties Tested"] F --> G["Time Invariance"] F --> H["Linearity"] F --> I["Causality"] F --> J["Memory"] F --> K["BIBO Stability"] ``` ## 4.2 Flowchart: Systematic Test Procedure for a Given System ```mermaid flowchart TD Start["Start: Given System y = T{x}"] --> Q1{"Is system linear?
Check superposition"} Q1 -- "Yes" --> L1["LINEAR ✓"] Q1 -- "No" --> L2["NON-LINEAR ✗"] L1 --> Q2{"Is system time-invariant?
Compare T{x(t-t0)} vs y(t-t0)"} Q2 -- "Yes" --> T1["TIME-INVARIANT ✓"] Q2 -- "No" --> T2["TIME-VARIANT ✗"] T1 --> Q3{"Does output depend on
future inputs?"} T3 -- "No" --> C1["CAUSAL ✓"] T3 -- "Yes" --> C2["NON-CAUSAL ✗"] C1 --> Q4{"Output depends on
current input only?"} Q4 -- "Yes" --> M1["MEMORYLESS ✓"] Q4 -- "No" --> M2["HAS MEMORY ✗"] M1 --> Q5{"Bounded input gives
bounded output?"} Q2 -- "Already tested" --> Q3 M2 --> Q5 C2 --> Q5 T2 --> Q5 L2 --> Q5 Q5 -- "Yes" --> S1["BIBO STABLE ✓"] Q5 -- "No" --> S2["UNSTABLE ✗"] ``` ## 4.3 Block-Level Architecture: Property Testing for LTI Systems ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph INPUT_BLOCK["INPUT DOMAIN"] I1["x1(t)"] --> ADD["Adder/Scaler: a*x1 + b*x2"] I2["x2(t)"] --> ADD end ADD --> SYS["LTI System T{ }"] subgraph OUTPUT_BLOCK["OUTPUT DOMAIN"] SYS --> O1["y1(t) = T{x1}"] SYS --> O2["y2(t) = T{x2}"] SYS --> O3["y(t) = T{a*x1 + b*x2}"] end O1 --> COMB["Linear Combiner: a*y1 + b*y2"] O2 --> COMB O3 --> CHECK["Equality Check:
y(t) = a*y1 + b*y2 ?"] COMB --> CHECK CHECK -- "Equal" --> RES_L["LINEAR ✓"] CHECK -- "Not Equal" --> RES_NL["NON-LINEAR ✗"] ``` ## 4.4 Decision Tree: Causal vs. Non-Causal System Identification ```mermaid flowchart TD A["System Equation: y = T{x}"] --> B{"Does y(t) or y[n]
contain x with argument
greater than t or n?"} B -- "Yes (e.g., x(t+1), x(t-5) is OK)" --> C["NON-CAUSAL ✗"] B -- "No" --> D{"Does output depend
on values from
other time instants?"} D -- "No (only same t or n)" --> E["CAUSAL & MEMORYLESS ✓"] D -- "Yes (only past: x(t-1), x[n-2])" --> F["CAUSAL with MEMORY ✓"] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define BIBO stability. Check whether the system $y(t) = \cos(\omega_0 t)\,x(t)$ is BIBO stable.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (1.5 Marks):** A system is **Bounded-Input Bounded-Output (BIBO) stable** if every bounded input produces a bounded output. Formally, if $\vert x(t) \vert \leq M_x < \infty$ for all $t$, then $\vert y(t) \vert \leq M_y < \infty$ for all $t$. **Stability Test (1.5 Marks):** Let $\vert x(t) \vert \leq M_x$ (bounded input). The output is: $$y(t) = \cos(\omega_0 t)\,x(t)$$ Take the absolute value: $$\vert y(t) \vert = \vert \cos(\omega_0 t) \vert \cdot \vert x(t) \vert$$ Since $\vert \cos(\omega_0 t) \vert \leq 1$ for all $t$: $$\vert y(t) \vert \leq 1 \cdot M_x = M_x < \infty$$ **Conclusion:** The system is **BIBO STABLE.** ✓ --- ### Question 2 **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **What is a memoryless system? Give two examples of memoryless and two examples of systems with memory.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** **Definition (1 Mark):** A system is **memoryless** if the output at any time $t$ depends only on the input at the same time $t$, and not on any past or future values of the input. **Memoryless Examples (1 Mark):** 1. $y(t) = 3x(t)$ (Resistor: $v = iR$) 2. $y(t) = \sin(x(t))$ (Instantaneous nonlinear function) **Systems with Memory Examples (1 Mark):** 1. $y(t) = x(t-2)$ (Time delay) 2. $y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} x[k]$ (Accumulator) --- ## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 / Model Paper]** **(a)** Test the system $y(t) = x(t)\cos(\omega_0 t)$ for **linearity** and **time invariance**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Test the system $y[n] = n \cdot x[n] + x[n-1]$ for **causality**, **memory**, and **stability**. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for (a): Linearity and Time Invariance of $y(t) = x(t)\cos(\omega_0 t)$ **Test Linearity (3.5 Marks):** Let $x_1(t) \to y_1(t) = x_1(t)\cos(\omega_0 t)$ and $x_2(t) \to y_2(t) = x_2(t)\cos(\omega_0 t)$. Apply weighted sum input: $$T\{a\,x_1(t) + b\,x_2(t)\} = [a\,x_1(t) + b\,x_2(t)]\cos(\omega_0 t)$$ $$= a\,x_1(t)\cos(\omega_0 t) + b\,x_2(t)\cos(\omega_0 t)$$ $$= a\,y_1(t) + b\,y_2(t)$$ **System is LINEAR.** ✓ **Test Time Invariance (3.5 Marks):** **Step 1:** Delay the input and find output: $$T\{x(t-t_0)\} = x(t-t_0)\cos(\omega_0 t)$$ **Step 2:** Delay the output $y(t) = x(t)\cos(\omega_0 t)$: $$y(t-t_0) = x(t-t_0)\cos(\omega_0 (t-t_0))$$ **Step 3:** Compare the two expressions. They differ because the multiplier $\cos(\omega_0 t)$ is not shifted. **System is TIME-VARIANT.** ✗ #### Model Solution for (b): Causality, Memory, Stability of $y[n] = n \cdot x[n] + x[n-1]$ **Test Causality (2 Marks):** The output at index $n$ depends on $x[n]$ (present) and $x[n-1]$ (past). There is **no dependence** on $x[n+1], x[n+2], \ldots$ (future). **System is CAUSAL.** ✓ **Test Memory (2 Marks):** The output depends on both $x[n]$ and $x[n-1]$ (two different time instants). Therefore, the system **has MEMORY** (not memoryless). **System has MEMORY.** ✗ (Not memoryless) **Test Stability (3 Marks):** Assume $\vert x[n] \vert \leq M_x$ for all $n$. $$\vert y[n] \vert = \vert n \cdot x[n] + x[n-1] \vert$$ By the triangle inequality: $$\vert y[n] \vert \leq \vert n \vert \cdot \vert x[n] \vert + \vert x[n-1] \vert$$ $$\vert y[n] \vert \leq \vert n \vert \cdot M_x + M_x = M_x (\vert n \vert + 1)$$ As $n \to \infty$, $\vert y[n] \vert \to \infty$. The output is **unbounded**. **System is UNSTABLE (not BIBO stable).** ✗ --- ### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) **(a)** Define linearity. Test the system $y(t) = x^2(t) + 3$ for linearity. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Define causality. Check whether the systems $y(t) = x(-t)$ and $y[n] = x[-n]$ are causal. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for (a): Linearity Test of $y(t) = x^2(t) + 3$ **Definition (2 Marks):** A system is **linear** if it satisfies the principle of superposition: $$T\{a_1 x_1(t) + a_2 x_2(t)\} = a_1 T\{x_1(t)\} + a_2 T\{x_2(t)\}$$ **Test (5 Marks):** **Zero-Input Test:** Apply $x(t) = 0$: $$y(t) = 0^2 + 3 = 3 \neq 0$$ Since the zero input produces a **non-zero output**, the system **fails the linearity condition** at the most basic level. **Superposition Test (confirmation):** For $x_1(t)$ and $x_2(t)$: $$T\{x_1(t) + x_2(t)\} = [x_1(t) + x_2(t)]^2 + 3$$ $$= x_1^2(t) + 2x_1(t)x_2(t) + x_2^2(t) + 3$$ But: $$T\{x_1(t)\} + T\{x_2(t)\} = [x_1^2(t) + 3] + [x_2^2(t) + 3] = x_1^2(t) + x_2^2(t) + 6$$ The two expressions are **not equal** (the cross-term $2x_1 x_2$ and the constant $3$ differ). **System is NON-LINEAR.** ✗ #### Model Solution for (b): Causality Test of $y(t) = x(-t)$ and $y[n] = x[-n]$ **Definition (2 Marks):** A system is **causal** if the output at any time $t_0$ (or $n_0$) depends only on input values at $t \leq t_0$ (or $n \leq n_0$), and not on future inputs. **Test $y(t) = x(-t)$ (2.5 Marks):** To compute $y(1) = x(-1)$ → past input ✓ To compute $y(-1) = x(1)$ → **future** input ✗ Since the output at $t = -1$ depends on $x(1)$ (future), the system is **NON-CAUSAL.** ✗ **Test $y[n] = x[-n]$ (2.5 Marks):** To compute $y[1] = x[-1]$ → past input ✓ To compute $y[-1] = x[1]$ → **future** input ✗ Since the output at $n = -1$ depends on $x[1]$ (future), the system is **NON-CAUSAL.** ✗ --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Confusing "linear" with "linear in form":** A system like $y(t) = a x(t) + b$ looks linear but is **affine**, not linear. The constant $b$ violates the zero-input/zero-output test. **Always test $x(t) = 0 \Rightarrow y(t) = 0$ first.** (Costs 1–2 marks) > > 2. **Time invariance with time-varying coefficients:** Systems like $y(t) = t \cdot x(t)$ or $y[n] = n \cdot x[n]$ are **time-VARIANT**, not time-invariant, because the coefficient changes with time. The $t$ or $n$ in the output is **not** shifted. (Costs 2–3 marks) > > 3. **Causality in discrete time with negative indices:** $y[n] = x[-n]$ is **not causal** because at $n = -1$, it needs $x[1]$. Do not assume causality just because the argument is "smaller" in absolute value. (Costs 1–2 marks) > > 4. **Stability of accumulators/integrators:** Systems like $y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} x[k]$ are **NOT BIBO stable** because a constant input produces a linearly growing output. The impulse response is the unit step, which is **not absolutely summable**. (Costs 2 marks) > > 5. **Memory and current input:** A system like $y(t) = \cos(t) \cdot x(t)$ is **memoryless** because the multiplier $\cos(t)$ is a known time function, not a delayed input. (Costs 1 mark) ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Revision Checklist** ### 🔑 Time Invariance - Test: $T\{x(t-t_0)\} \stackrel{?}{=} y(t-t_0)$ - A coefficient that depends on $t$ (or $n$) makes the system **time-variant** - LTI systems are characterized entirely by their **impulse response** $h(t)$ or $h[n]$ ### 🔑 Linearity - Two parts: **Additivity** + **Homogeneity** = **Superposition** - Quick test: $x(t) = 0 \Rightarrow y(t) = 0$ (necessary but not sufficient — verify superposition too) - All systems of the form $y = \int h(\tau) x(t-\tau) d\tau$ (LTI) are linear ### 🔑 Causality - Memoryless and causal are **NOT the same thing** - **Memoryless** = depends only on current input - **Causal** = depends only on present and past (no future) - All real-time systems **must be causal** ### 🔑 Memory - Memoryless: $y(t) = f(x(t))$ for any function $f$ - With memory: any dependence on $x(t-k)$ for $k \neq 0$ ### 🔑 Stability (BIBO) - Bounded input → Bounded output - LTI check: $\int \vert h(t) \vert \, dt < \infty$ (CT) or $\sum \vert h[n] \vert < \infty$ (DT) - Integrators, accumulators, and $y = \frac{1}{1-z^{-1}}$ (pole on unit circle) are **unstable** ### 🔑 Master Rule - **LTI + Causal + Stable** = Realizable, characterizable, analyzable — the **gold standard** of system design.

topics.title: Convolution integral and convolution sum (graphical and any one matrix method)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT522 - INTRODUCTION TO SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Convolution integral and convolution sum (graphical and any one matrix method)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Convolution** is a mathematical operation that combines two signals to produce a third signal, expressing how the shape of one signal is modified by the other. In the context of **Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) systems**, the convolution operation is fundamental because it directly relates the **input signal** and the **impulse response** of the system to the **output signal**.

The convolution operation exists in two principal forms, depending on whether the signals are **continuous-time (CT)** or **discrete-time (DT)**:

### Continuous-Time Convolution Integral

For two continuous-time signals $x(t)$ and $h(t)$, the convolution integral is formally defined as:

$$
y(t) = x(t) * h(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau) \cdot h(t - \tau) \, d\tau
$$

where:
- $y(t)$ is the **output signal**
- $x(t)$ is the **input signal**
- $h(t)$ is the **impulse response** of the LTI system
- $*$ denotes the convolution operator
- $\tau$ is a dummy integration variable
- $t - \tau$ represents the time-shifted and time-reversed version of $h$

### Discrete-Time Convolution Sum

For two discrete-time sequences $x[n]$ and $h[n]$, the convolution sum is formally defined as:

$$
y[n] = x[n] * h[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} x[k] \cdot h[n - k]
$$

where:
- $y[n]$ is the **output sequence**
- $x[n]$ is the **input sequence**
- $h[n]$ is the **impulse response** of the discrete LTI system
- $k$ is the dummy summation index
- $n$ is the output time index

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Blurring & Echo" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy: The Sliding Stamp & Ink Pad**
> 
> Imagine you have an **ink pad** (representing the impulse response $h$) shaped like a small smear. You are asked to stamp this ink pad repeatedly along a long line that contains different amounts of ink at each point (representing the input signal $x$).
> 
> - At each point on the line, you press the ink pad down. The smear **overlaps** with the existing ink.
> - The **total darkness** at any given point on the line is the sum of all the ink-smear contributions from every nearby stamping position.
> - As the smear slides across, each input point "leaves a smeared impression" on the output. This **smeared, overlapping, weighted addition** is precisely what convolution does mathematically.

Another everyday analogy is **echo in a canyon**: The output sound you hear is the original sound (**input signal**) plus multiple **delayed and scaled versions** of itself (the system's impulse response). Convolution mathematically adds up all these echoes into one output waveform.

## 1.3 Physical Significance in Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In electrical engineering, convolution is the **cornerstone of LTI system analysis**:
> - **Signal Processing**: Filtering, smoothing, de-noising
> - **Communications**: Channel equalization, modulation/demodulation
> - **Control Systems**: Determining step response from impulse response
> - **Image Processing**: Blurring, sharpening, edge detection
> - **Biomedical**: ECG/EEG signal analysis

The convolution integral/sum essentially answers the question: **"If I know what the system does to a single unit impulse, can I determine what the system does to any arbitrary input?"** The answer is a definitive **YES** — by convolving the input with the impulse response.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three-Step Convolution Procedure (Universal for Both Integral and Sum)

The convolution operation, despite its seemingly complex mathematical form, follows a **systematic three-step graphical procedure** that is identical in spirit for both continuous and discrete cases:

### Step 1: Folding (Time Reversal)
- Take the second signal $h(t)$ or $h[n]$ and **reflect it about the vertical axis** (i.e., about $\tau = 0$ or $k = 0$).
- This produces $h(-\tau)$ or $h(-k)$.

### Step 2: Shifting (Time Translation)
- Shift the folded signal by $t$ (continuous) or $n$ (discrete) along the time axis.
- This produces $h(t - \tau)$ or $h[n - k]$.
- As $t$ or $n$ varies, the shifted folded signal slides across the time axis.

### Step 3: Multiplication and Integration/Summation
- **Multiply** the first signal $x(\tau)$ (or $x[k]$) with the shifted folded signal $h(t - \tau)$ (or $h[n - k]$).
- **Integrate** (continuous) or **Sum** (discrete) the product over all $\tau$ (or $k$).
- The result of this operation at a particular $t$ (or $n$) is **one point** of the output $y(t)$ (or $y[n]$).
- Repeat for all values of $t$ or $n$ to obtain the complete output.

## 2.2 Detailed Step-by-Step Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these three steps?**
> 
> - **Folding** is needed because the convolution integral uses $h(t - \tau)$, where the negative sign implies a time reversal.
> - **Shifting** is needed because we want to evaluate the overlap between $x$ and a time-shifted version of $h$.
> - **Multiplication + Integration** computes the **area of overlap** (continuous) or **sum of overlapping products** (discrete) at each shift position. This area/sum is precisely the convolution value at that time instant.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Formula / Concept** | **Expression** | **Domain** | **Physical Meaning** |
|------------------------|-----------------|-------------|----------------------|
| Convolution Integral (CT) | $y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau) \cdot h(t - \tau) \, d\tau$ | Continuous-time | Overlap area of $x(\tau)$ and shifted $h(t-\tau)$ |
| Convolution Sum (DT) | $y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} x[k] \cdot h[n - k]$ | Discrete-time | Weighted sum of overlapping products |
| Commutative Property | $x * h = h * x$ | Both | Order of operands does not matter |
| Associative Property | $(x * h_1) * h_2 = x * (h_1 * h_2)$ | Both | Grouping does not matter |
| Distributive Property | $x * (h_1 + h_2) = x * h_1 + x * h_2$ | Both | Convolution distributes over addition |
| Identity Element | $x[n] * \delta[n] = x[n]$ (DT) and $x(t) * \delta(t) = x(t)$ (CT) | Both | Convolution with unit impulse returns original |
| Shift Property | $x[n] * \delta[n - n_0] = x[n - n_0]$ | Both | Convolution with shifted impulse shifts the signal |
| Width Property (DT) | If $x[n]$ has $N_1$ nonzero samples and $h[n]$ has $N_2$ nonzero samples, then $y[n]$ has $N_1 + N_2 - 1$ nonzero samples | Discrete | Output length of convolution sum |
| Causal LTI Output | $y(t) = \int_{0}^{t} x(\tau) h(t - \tau) d\tau$ (for $t \geq 0$) | Both | Limits reduce to $[0, t]$ for causal systems |
| Convolution with Unit Step | $u(t) * u(t) = r(t) = t \cdot u(t)$ | Continuous | Two unit steps convolve to a ramp |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note on Width Property**: The width property ($N_1 + N_2 - 1$) is **extremely important** for the matrix (tabular) method. It tells you the **total number of nonzero samples** in the output sequence. For KTU exam problems, students are expected to verify this count.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is convolution so important in engineering?**
> 
> 1. **LTI System Response**: Any LTI system is completely characterized by its impulse response $h(t)$ or $h[n]$. The output for **any** input is obtained by convolving the input with $h$. This is the **most fundamental result** in signals and systems theory.
> 
> 2. **Filter Design**: Digital filters (FIR, IIR) are designed by specifying their impulse response, and their action on any input is described by convolution.
> 
> 3. **Modulation in Communication**: The multiplication of a message signal with a carrier in the time domain corresponds to convolution in the frequency domain.
> 
> 4. **Sampling Theorem Reconstruction**: An ideal low-pass filter reconstruction uses convolution with a sinc function.
> 
> 5. **Image Processing**: 2D convolution is used for blurring (Gaussian kernel), edge detection (Sobel, Laplacian kernels), and many other operations.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Convolution Integral for LTI Systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theorem**: For any LTI system with impulse response $h(t)$, the output $y(t)$ for an arbitrary input $x(t)$ is given by the convolution integral.

### Derivation Steps

**Step 1: Express the input as a superposition of impulses.**

Any continuous-time signal $x(t)$ can be represented as a weighted sum (integral) of time-shifted impulses using the **sifting property** of the Dirac delta function:

$$
x(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau) \cdot \delta(t - \tau) \, d\tau
$$

Here, $x(\tau)$ acts as the weight applied to an impulse located at time $t = \tau$. This represents $x(t)$ as a continuum of impulses, each scaled by the value of $x$ at that point.

**Step 2: Use the Time-Invariance Property.**

The response of an LTI system to a unit impulse $\delta(t)$ is, by definition, the **impulse response** $h(t)$. By time-invariance, the response to a time-shifted impulse $\delta(t - \tau)$ is $h(t - \tau)$.

**Step 3: Use the Linearity Property.**

By linearity, the response of the system to a weighted sum of inputs is the same weighted sum of individual responses. Therefore, the response to the integral of impulses is the integral of the individual responses:

$$
y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau) \cdot h(t - \tau) \, d\tau
$$

This is the **convolution integral**. The derivation is complete.

## 3.2 Worked Example 1: Convolution Integral — Graphical Method

**Problem**: Compute the convolution $y(t) = x(t) * h(t)$ where:

$$
x(t) = e^{-t} \cdot u(t)
$$

$$
h(t) = u(t) - u(t - 1)
$$

This means $x(t)$ is a decaying exponential starting at $t = 0$, and $h(t)$ is a rectangular pulse of width 1 (from $t = 0$ to $t = 1$).

### Solution

**Step 1: Write the convolution integral.**

$$
y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau) \cdot h(t - \tau) \, d\tau
$$

**Step 2: Determine the limits of integration based on the support of the signals.**

- $x(\tau) = e^{-\tau} \cdot u(\tau)$ is nonzero for $\tau \geq 0$.
- $h(t - \tau) = u(t - \tau) - u(t - \tau - 1)$ is nonzero for $0 \leq t - \tau \leq 1$, i.e., $t - 1 \leq \tau \leq t$.

The product $x(\tau) \cdot h(t - \tau)$ is nonzero only when **both** signals are nonzero. Therefore, the integration limits are determined by the intersection:

- Lower limit: $\max(0, t - 1)$
- Upper limit: when $\tau = t$ (since $u(t-\tau) = 1$ for $\tau \leq t$ and $u(t-\tau-1) = 0$ for $\tau \leq t-1$, the upper limit is $t$).

So:

$$
y(t) = \int_{\max(0, t-1)}^{t} e^{-\tau} \, d\tau
$$

**Step 3: Evaluate the integral for different ranges of $t$.**

**Case 1: $t < 0$.** No overlap. $y(t) = 0$.

**Case 2: $0 \leq t < 1$.** The lower limit is $0$ (since $t - 1 < 0 < 0$ wait, $\max(0, t-1) = 0$).

$$
y(t) = \int_{0}^{t} e^{-\tau} \, d\tau = \left[ -e^{-\tau} \right]_{0}^{t} = -e^{-t} - (-1) = 1 - e^{-t}
$$

**Case 3: $t \geq 1$.** The lower limit is $t - 1$ (since $t - 1 \geq 0$).

$$
y(t) = \int_{t-1}^{t} e^{-\tau} \, d\tau = \left[ -e^{-\tau} \right]_{t-1}^{t} = -e^{-t} + e^{-(t-1)} = e^{-t+1} - e^{-t} = e^{-t}(e - 1)
$$

**Step 4: Write the final result.**

$$
y(t) = \begin{cases} 0, & t < 0 \\ 1 - e^{-t}, & 0 \leq t < 1 \\ (e - 1) \cdot e^{-t}, & t \geq 1 \end{cases}
$$

**Verification at boundaries:**
- At $t = 0$: $y(0) = 1 - e^0 = 0$ ✓
- At $t = 1$: $y(1) = 1 - e^{-1} = (e-1)/e$ from Case 2, and $y(1) = (e-1) \cdot e^{-1} = (e-1)/e$ from Case 3 ✓ (Continuous!)

## 3.3 Worked Example 2: Convolution Sum — Graphical Method

**Problem**: Compute $y[n] = x[n] * h[n]$ where:

$$
x[n] = \{1, 2, 3\} \quad \text{(i.e., } x[0]=1, x[1]=2, x[2]=3\text{)}
$$

$$
h[n] = \{1, 1, 1\} \quad \text{(i.e., } h[0]=1, h[1]=1, h[2]=1\text{)}
$$

### Solution

**Step 1: Identify the lengths.**

- Length of $x[n]$: $N_1 = 3$
- Length of $h[n]$: $N_2 = 3$
- Length of $y[n]$: $N_1 + N_2 - 1 = 3 + 3 - 1 = 5$
- $y[n]$ will be nonzero for $n = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4$.

**Step 2: Apply the graphical three-step procedure (Folding, Shifting, Multiplying, Summing).**

- **Fold**: $h[-k] = \{1, 1, 1\}$ reversed — since it's symmetric, it stays the same: $\{1, 1, 1\}$.
- **Shift** by $n$ and **multiply** with $x[k]$:

For $n = 0$: $h[-k]$ and $x[k]$ overlap only at $k = 0$ (assuming $x$ starts at $k=0$).
Wait, let me be careful. $h[0-k] = h[-k]$ is nonzero for $k = 0, -1, -2$. $x[k]$ is nonzero for $k = 0, 1, 2$. Overlap at $k = 0$ only.

$y[0] = x[0] \cdot h[0] = 1 \cdot 1 = 1$.

For $n = 1$: $h[1-k]$ is nonzero when $1-k \in \{0,1,2\}$, i.e., $k \in \{-1, 0, 1\}$. $x[k]$ is nonzero for $k \in \{0, 1, 2\}$. Overlap at $k = 0, 1$.

$y[1] = x[0] \cdot h[1] + x[1] \cdot h[0] = 1 \cdot 1 + 2 \cdot 1 = 3$.

For $n = 2$: $h[2-k]$ is nonzero when $2-k \in \{0,1,2\}$, i.e., $k \in \{0, 1, 2\}$. $x[k]$ is nonzero for $k \in \{0, 1, 2\}$. Overlap at $k = 0, 1, 2$.

$y[2] = x[0] \cdot h[2] + x[1] \cdot h[1] + x[2] \cdot h[0] = 1 \cdot 1 + 2 \cdot 1 + 3 \cdot 1 = 6$.

For $n = 3$: $h[3-k]$ is nonzero when $3-k \in \{0,1,2\}$, i.e., $k \in \{1, 2, 3\}$. $x[k]$ is nonzero for $k \in \{0, 1, 2\}$. Overlap at $k = 1, 2$.

$y[3] = x[1] \cdot h[2] + x[2] \cdot h[1] = 2 \cdot 1 + 3 \cdot 1 = 5$.

For $n = 4$: $h[4-k]$ is nonzero when $4-k \in \{0,1,2\}$, i.e., $k \in \{2, 3, 4\}$. $x[k]$ is nonzero for $k \in \{0, 1, 2\}$. Overlap at $k = 2$.

$y[4] = x[2] \cdot h[2] = 3 \cdot 1 = 3$.

**Step 3: Write the final output.**

$$
y[n] = \{1, 3, 6, 5, 3\}, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4
$$

## 3.4 Worked Example 3: Convolution Sum — Matrix (Tabular) Method

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The matrix method is an **elegant and computationally efficient** way to perform discrete convolution, especially useful for KTU exam problems. It exploits the linearity of the operation.

### Problem

Compute $y[n] = x[n] * h[n]$ using the matrix method where:

$$
x[n] = \{2, 1, 3\} \quad (x[0]=2, x[1]=1, x[2]=3)
$$

$$
h[n] = \{1, 4, 2\} \quad (h[0]=1, h[1]=4, h[2]=2)
$$

### Matrix Method Procedure

**Step 1: Determine the output length.**

Length of $y[n] = N_1 + N_2 - 1 = 3 + 3 - 1 = 5$ samples (for $n = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4$).

**Step 2: Construct the matrix.**

Place the values of $h[n-k]$ (shifted $h$) along the **rows** corresponding to each output index, and place the values of $x[k]$ along the **columns** corresponding to each $k$ value.

The convolution formula is:

$$
y[n] = \sum_{k} x[k] \cdot h[n-k]
$$

For $k = 0$: multiply $x[0]$ with $h[n]$
For $k = 1$: multiply $x[1]$ with $h[n-1]$
For $k = 2$: multiply $x[2]$ with $h[n-2]$

The matrix layout is as follows (we write $h[n-k]$ in each cell):

$$
\begin{array}{c|ccccc}
n \backslash k & k=0 & k=1 & k=2 & \text{Sum} \\
\hline
n=0 & h[0] \cdot x[0] & 0 & 0 & y[0] \\
n=1 & h[1] \cdot x[0] & h[0] \cdot x[1] & 0 & y[1] \\
n=2 & h[2] \cdot x[0] & h[1] \cdot x[1] & h[0] \cdot x[2] & y[2] \\
n=3 & 0 & h[2] \cdot x[1] & h[1] \cdot x[2] & y[3] \\
n=4 & 0 & 0 & h[2] \cdot x[2] & y[4] \\
\end{array}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute numerical values.**

Using $x[0]=2, x[1]=1, x[2]=3$ and $h[0]=1, h[1]=4, h[2]=2$:

- $h[0] \cdot x[0] = 1 \cdot 2 = 2$
- $h[1] \cdot x[0] = 4 \cdot 2 = 8$
- $h[2] \cdot x[0] = 2 \cdot 2 = 4$
- $h[0] \cdot x[1] = 1 \cdot 1 = 1$
- $h[1] \cdot x[1] = 4 \cdot 1 = 4$
- $h[2] \cdot x[1] = 2 \cdot 1 = 2$
- $h[0] \cdot x[2] = 1 \cdot 3 = 3$
- $h[1] \cdot x[2] = 4 \cdot 3 = 12$
- $h[2] \cdot x[2] = 2 \cdot 3 = 6$

The complete matrix is:

$$
\begin{array}{c|cccc}
n \backslash k & k=0 & k=1 & k=2 & \text{Output} \\
\hline
n=0 & 2 & 0 & 0 & 2 \\
n=1 & 8 & 1 & 0 & 9 \\
n=2 & 4 & 4 & 3 & 11 \\
n=3 & 0 & 2 & 12 & 14 \\
n=4 & 0 & 0 & 6 & 6 \\
\end{array}
$$

**Step 4: Sum the columns for each row to get $y[n]$.**

$$
y[0] = 2 + 0 + 0 = 2
$$

$$
y[1] = 8 + 1 + 0 = 9
$$

$$
y[2] = 4 + 4 + 3 = 11
$$

$$
y[3] = 0 + 2 + 12 = 14
$$

$$
y[4] = 0 + 0 + 6 = 6
$$

**Step 5: Write the final output.**

$$
y[n] = \{2, 9, 11, 14, 6\}, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4
$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix for Choosing the Right Method

> [!NOTE]
> The following table helps decide which method to apply based on the problem type.

| **Method** | **Best Used For** | **Advantages** | **Disadvantages** |
|------------|--------------------|----------------|---------------------|
| Analytical (Direct Formula) | Simple signals (rectangles, exponentials, unit steps) | Quick, gives closed-form expression | Difficult for complex signals |
| Graphical (Folding-Shifting) | Visualizing the overlap, building intuition | Clear geometric understanding | Time-consuming for long signals |
| Matrix (Tabular) | Discrete-time sequences, finite-length signals | Systematic, less error-prone, fast | Only practical for DT convolution |
| Numerical/Sampling | Continuous signals on a computer | Applicable to any signal | Approximate, requires sampling rate choice |
| Convolution Table | Quick reference for standard pairs | Very fast | Limited to known pairs |
