# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: Short-Answer Question
> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
> 
> **Q: Define convolution integral. State and prove the commutative property of convolution.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1 Mark):** The convolution of two continuous-time signals $x(t)$ and $h(t)$ is defined as:

$$
y(t) = x(t) * h(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau) h(t - \tau) \, d\tau
$$

**Commutative Property Statement (0.5 Mark):**

$$
x(t) * h(t) = h(t) * x(t)
$$

**Proof (1.5 Marks):** Starting from the definition:

$$
x(t) * h(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau) h(t - \tau) \, d\tau
$$

Apply substitution: Let $\lambda = t - \tau$, so $d\lambda = -d\tau$. When $\tau \to -\infty$, $\lambda \to +\infty$. When $\tau \to +\infty$, $\lambda \to -\infty$.

$$
x(t) * h(t) = \int_{+\infty}^{-\infty} x(t - \lambda) h(\lambda) \cdot (-d\lambda) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} h(\lambda) x(t - \lambda) \, d\lambda = h(t) * x(t)
$$

Hence proved. ✓

---

### Question A2: Short-Answer Question
> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
> 
> **Q: List any four properties of convolution. Mention the significance of convolution in LTI system analysis.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Four Properties (2 Marks):**
1. **Commutative**: $x * h = h * x$
2. **Associative**: $(x * h_1) * h_2 = x * (h_1 * h_2)$
3. **Distributive**: $x * (h_1 + h_2) = x * h_1 + x * h_2$
4. **Identity**: $x(t) * \delta(t) = x(t)$ and $x[n] * \delta[n] = x[n]$

**Significance in LTI Systems (1 Mark):** For any Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) system, the output $y(t)$ for an arbitrary input $x(t)$ is obtained by convolving the input with the system's impulse response $h(t)$. This is expressed as $y(t) = x(t) * h(t)$. The impulse response **completely characterizes** an LTI system, making convolution the **fundamental tool** for system analysis and design.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme regulations, Part B questions in the End Semester Examination carry **14 marks** and offer **internal choice** between two alternative questions. Each sub-part below is designed for 7 marks.

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
> 
> **(a)** Compute the convolution $y(t) = x(t) * h(t)$ using the **graphical method**, where:
> 
> $$x(t) = u(t) - u(t-2) \quad \text{(rectangular pulse of width 2 starting at } t=0\text{)}$$
> 
> $$h(t) = e^{-t} \cdot u(t) \quad \text{(decaying exponential starting at } t=0\text{)}$$
> 
> Plot $x(\tau)$, $h(-\tau)$, $h(t-\tau)$ for a representative value of $t$, and determine $y(t)$ for all $t$. **(7 Marks)**
> 
> **(b)** Two discrete-time sequences are given as $x[n] = \{1, 2, 1\}$ and $h[n] = \{1, 1\}$. Find $y[n] = x[n] * h[n]$ using the **matrix method**. Also verify the width property. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1: Write the convolution integral.**

$$
y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau) h(t - \tau) \, d\tau
$$

**Step 2: Identify the nonzero regions.**

- $x(\tau) = 1$ for $0 \leq \tau \leq 2$ (rectangular pulse)
- $h(t - \tau) = e^{-(t-\tau)}$ for $t - \tau \geq 0$, i.e., $\tau \leq t$

**Step 3: Graphical procedure.**

- **Fold**: $h(-\tau) = e^{\tau} u(-\tau)$ (defined for $\tau \leq 0$)
- **Shift by $t$**: $h(t-\tau) = e^{-(t-\tau)} u(t-\tau)$, nonzero for $\tau \leq t$

**Step 4: Determine overlap and integrate.**

The overlap region is $\max(0, ...) \leq \tau \leq \min(2, t)$.

**Case 1: $t < 0$.** No overlap. $y(t) = 0$.

**Case 2: $0 \leq t \leq 2$.** Overlap is $0 \leq \tau \leq t$:

$$
y(t) = \int_{0}^{t} 1 \cdot e^{-(t-\tau)} \, d\tau = e^{-t} \int_{0}^{t} e^{\tau} \, d\tau = e^{-t} \left[ e^{\tau} \right]_{0}^{t} = e^{-t}(e^t - 1) = 1 - e^{-t}
$$

**Case 3: $t > 2$.** Overlap is $0 \leq \tau \leq 2$ (the entire $x$ overlaps):

$$
y(t) = \int_{0}^{2} 1 \cdot e^{-(t-\tau)} \, d\tau = e^{-t} \int_{0}^{2} e^{\tau} \, d\tau = e^{-t} (e^2 - 1)
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
y(t) = \begin{cases} 0, & t < 0 \\ 1 - e^{-t}, & 0 \leq t \leq 2 \\ (e^2 - 1) e^{-t}, & t > 2 \end{cases}
$$

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1: Determine lengths.**

- $N_1 = 3$ (length of $x$), $N_2 = 2$ (length of $h$)
- $N_1 + N_2 - 1 = 4$ (length of $y$)
- $y[n]$ is nonzero for $n = 0, 1, 2, 3$.

**Step 2: Construct the matrix.**

Using $x = \{x[0], x[1], x[2]\} = \{1, 2, 1\}$ and $h = \{h[0], h[1]\} = \{1, 1\}$:

$$
\begin{array}{c|ccc|c}
n \backslash k & k=0 & k=1 & k=2 & \text{Output } y[n] \\
\hline
n=0 & h[0]x[0] = 1 & 0 & 0 & 1 \\
n=1 & h[1]x[0] = 1 & h[0]x[1] = 2 & 0 & 3 \\
n=2 & 0 & h[1]x[1] = 2 & h[0]x[2] = 1 & 3 \\
n=3 & 0 & 0 & h[1]x[2] = 1 & 1 \\
\end{array}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the row sums.**

$$
y[0] = 1, \quad y[1] = 1 + 2 = 3, \quad y[2] = 2 + 1 = 3, \quad y[3] = 1
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
y[n] = \{1, 3, 3, 1\}, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, 3
$$

**Verification of Width Property:** Output length is **4** = $3 + 2 - 1$ ✓

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]**
> 
> **(a)** Determine the convolution $y(t) = x(t) * h(t)$ graphically, where:
> 
> $$x(t) = t \cdot [u(t) - u(t-2)] \quad \text{(ramp of duration 2)}$$
> 
> $$h(t) = u(t) - u(t-1) \quad \text{(rectangular pulse of width 1)}$$
> 
> Show clearly the folding and shifting steps. **(7 Marks)**
> 
> **(b)** Compute the convolution sum $y[n] = x[n] * h[n]$ using the **graphical method**, where:
> 
> $$x[n] = \{1, -1, 2, 0, 1\} \quad \text{(starting at } n=0\text{)}$$
> 
> $$h[n] = \{2, 1, 3\} \quad \text{(starting at } n=0\text{)}$$
> 
> Sketch the folded and shifted versions of $h$ for each relevant value of $n$. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1: Define the signals.**

- $x(\tau) = \tau$ for $0 \leq \tau \leq 2$, zero elsewhere
- $h(\tau) = 1$ for $0 \leq \tau \leq 1$, zero elsewhere

**Step 2: Apply the convolution formula.**

$$
y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau) h(t - \tau) \, d\tau
$$

$h(t - \tau) = 1$ for $0 \leq t - \tau \leq 1$, i.e., $t - 1 \leq \tau \leq t$.

**Step 3: Determine overlap regions.**

Overlap: $\max(0, t-1) \leq \tau \leq \min(2, t)$.

**Case 1: $t < 0$.** No overlap, $y(t) = 0$.

**Case 2: $0 \leq t \leq 1$.** Overlap is $0 \leq \tau \leq t$:

$$
y(t) = \int_{0}^{t} \tau \cdot 1 \, d\tau = \frac{\tau^2}{2} \bigg|_{0}^{t} = \frac{t^2}{2}
$$

**Case 3: $1 < t \leq 2$.** Overlap is $t - 1 \leq \tau \leq t$:

$$
y(t) = \int_{t-1}^{t} \tau \, d\tau = \frac{\tau^2}{2} \bigg|_{t-1}^{t} = \frac{t^2 - (t-1)^2}{2} = \frac{2t - 1}{2} = t - \frac{1}{2}
$$

**Case 4: $2 < t \leq 3$.** Overlap is $t - 1 \leq \tau \leq 2$:

$$
y(t) = \int_{t-1}^{2} \tau \, d\tau = \frac{\tau^2}{2} \bigg|_{t-1}^{2} = \frac{4 - (t-1)^2}{2} = \frac{4 - t^2 + 2t - 1}{2} = \frac{3 + 2t - t^2}{2}
$$

**Case 5: $t > 3$.** No overlap, $y(t) = 0$.

**Final Answer:**

$$
y(t) = \begin{cases} 0, & t < 0 \\ \dfrac{t^2}{2}, & 0 \leq t \leq 1 \\ t - \dfrac{1}{2}, & 1 < t \leq 2 \\ \dfrac{3 + 2t - t^2}{2}, & 2 < t \leq 3 \\ 0, & t > 3 \end{cases}
$$

**Continuity Check:**
- At $t = 1$: Case 2 gives $1/2$, Case 3 gives $1 - 1/2 = 1/2$ ✓
- At $t = 2$: Case 3 gives $3/2$, Case 4 gives $(3 + 4 - 4)/2 = 3/2$ ✓

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1: Lengths and ranges.**

- $x[n]$: length 5, indices $0$ to $4$
- $h[n]$: length 3, indices $0$ to $2$
- $y[n]$: length $5 + 3 - 1 = 7$, indices $0$ to $6$

**Step 2: Apply the formula $y[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{4} x[k] h[n-k]$.**

For each $n$, only $h[n-k]$ for $k$ in range and $n-k \in \{0, 1, 2\}$ contributes.

**For $n = 0$:** $h[0-k]$ nonzero for $k=0$ (only $h[0]$). $y[0] = x[0] h[0] = 1 \cdot 2 = 2$.

**For $n = 1$:** $h[1-k]$ nonzero for $k=0,1$ (i.e., $h[1]$ and $h[0]$).
$y[1] = x[0] h[1] + x[1] h[0] = 1 \cdot 1 + (-1) \cdot 2 = 1 - 2 = -1$.

**For $n = 2$:** $h[2-k]$ nonzero for $k=0,1,2$ (i.e., $h[2]$, $h[1]$, $h[0]$).
$y[2] = x[0] h[2] + x[1] h[1] + x[2] h[0] = 1 \cdot 3 + (-1) \cdot 1 + 2 \cdot 2 = 3 - 1 + 4 = 6$.

**For $n = 3$:** $h[3-k]$ nonzero for $k=1,2,3$ (since $n-k \in \{0,1,2\}$).
$y[3] = x[1] h[2] + x[2] h[1] + x[3] h[0] = (-1) \cdot 3 + 2 \cdot 1 + 0 \cdot 2 = -3 + 2 + 0 = -1$.

**For $n = 4$:** $h[4-k]$ nonzero for $k=2,3,4$.
$y[4] = x[2] h[2] + x[3] h[1] + x[4] h[0] = 2 \cdot 3 + 0 \cdot 1 + 1 \cdot 2 = 6 + 0 + 2 = 8$.

**For $n = 5$:** $h[5-k]$ nonzero for $k=3,4,5$ but $x[k]$ is nonzero only up to $k=4$.
$y[5] = x[3] h[2] + x[4] h[1] = 0 \cdot 3 + 1 \cdot 1 = 1$.

**For $n = 6$:** $h[6-k]$ nonzero for $k=4,5,6$ but $x[k]$ is nonzero only up to $k=4$.
$y[6] = x[4] h[2] = 1 \cdot 3 = 3$.

**Final Answer:**

$$
y[n] = \{2, -1, 6, -1, 8, 1, 3\}, \quad n = 0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 
> 1. **Sign Errors in Convolution Sum**: A very common mistake is to forget to flip the second signal before shifting. Always remember: **FOLD first, then SHIFT**. Many students mistakenly multiply $x[k]$ by $h[k+n]$ (wrong direction) instead of $h[n-k]$.
> 
> 2. **Wrong Limits of Integration**: For continuous convolution, students often forget to break the problem into multiple time-interval cases. The limits of integration change as the overlap region changes. Missing a case = lost marks.
> 
> 3. **Width Property Mismatch**: In the matrix method, the output sequence must have exactly $N_1 + N_2 - 1$ nonzero samples. If your output has a different number, you have an error.
> 
> 4. **Misaligned Indices**: When sequences are given as $\{a, b, c\}$ "starting at $n=0$," some students assume they start at $n=1$. Always read carefully.
> 
> 5. **Forgetting to Verify Continuity**: For continuous convolution, the piecewise function must be **continuous at the boundaries**. Always check the left and right limits at each boundary point.
> 
> 6. **Mixing Up the Fold Direction**: Folding is reflection about the **vertical axis** (the imaginary axis for time origin). Confusing this with reflection about the horizontal axis loses 1-2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist**

- ✅ **Convolution Integral** is defined for continuous-time LTI systems as $y(t) = \int x(\tau) h(t-\tau) d\tau$, relating input $x(t)$, impulse response $h(t)$, and output $y(t)$.
- ✅ **Convolution Sum** is defined for discrete-time LTI systems as $y[n] = \sum x[k] h[n-k]$, performing the same role as the integral in the discrete domain.
- ✅ **Three-step Graphical Procedure**: **FOLD** (time-reverse $h$) → **SHIFT** (by $t$ or $n$) → **MULTIPLY and INTEGRATE/SUM** (compute area of overlap).
- ✅ **Width Property (DT)**: If $x[n]$ has $N_1$ nonzero samples and $h[n]$ has $N_2$ nonzero samples, then $y[n]$ has $N_1 + N_2 - 1$ nonzero samples.
- ✅ **Matrix Method (Tabular Method)**: Construct an $N_1 + N_2 - 1$ by $\max(N_1, N_2)$ table; fill cells with products $x[k] \cdot h[n-k]$; sum each row to get $y[n]$.
- ✅ **Commutative, Associative, Distributive Properties** all hold for both continuous and discrete convolution.
- ✅ **Convolution with Impulse**: $x(t) * \delta(t) = x(t)$ and $x[n] * \delta[n] = x[n]$. This is the **identity property**.
- ✅ **LTI System Output**: $y = x * h$ is the **fundamental equation** governing LTI system behavior.
- ✅ **Causal LTI Systems**: For $x(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$ and $h(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$, the integration limits reduce to $0$ to $t$.
- ✅ **Continuous vs. Discrete Boundary Continuity**: The piecewise output $y(t)$ must be continuous at all transition points.
- ✅ **Common Test Pairs**: $u(t) * u(t) = t \cdot u(t)$ (ramp), $e^{-at} u(t) * u(t) = \frac{1 - e^{-at}}{a} u(t)$.
- ✅ **Method Selection**: Use **graphical** for visualization/short problems, **analytical** for closed-form expressions, **matrix** for finite-length discrete sequences.
- ✅ **KTU Exam Tip**: Always state the **properties used** in your solution, draw clear **diagrams** for graphical problems, and **verify the width property** for the matrix method.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Impulse and step response
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT522 - INTRODUCTION TO SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Impulse and step response

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the framework of **Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) systems**, the **impulse response** and **step response** are the two canonical (standard, universally used) output signals that completely characterize the behavior of any LTI system. Mathematically:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Impulse Response** is defined as the output of a relaxed (all initial conditions are zero) LTI system when the input is a unit impulse function $\delta(t)$ (continuous-time) or $\delta[n]$ (discrete-time). It is denoted by $h(t)$ or $h[n]$ respectively.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step Response** is defined as the output of a relaxed LTI system when the input is a unit step function $u(t)$ (continuous-time) or $u[n]$ (discrete-time). It is denoted by $s(t)$ or $s[n]$ respectively.

A system is termed "**relaxed at time $t_0$**" if there is no energy stored in it prior to $t_0$. For such relaxed systems, the impulse response and step response serve as the complete "fingerprint" — knowing either one entirely determines the system's output for *any* arbitrary input.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are standing in an empty, perfectly still swimming pool (this represents the system at rest). Now imagine two experiments:

- **The Impulse Response Experiment** 🪨: You drop a **single heavy stone** (the impulse) into the pool from a great height. The "splash pattern" that ripples out — its size, speed of decay, and oscillation — is unique to that pool. This is the **impulse response**. It tells you how the system "rings" when struck instantly.

- **The Step Response Experiment** 🌊: Instead of dropping a stone, you suddenly turn on a water tap and pour water continuously into the pool (a sustained, constant input — the step). The way the water level rises over time — smoothly, with overshoot, oscillating, or slowly — is the **step response**. It tells you how the system accumulates/reaches a new steady state.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Beautiful Connection**: The step response is simply the *running total* (integration or summation) of the impulse response. Why? Because a step is just a sum of infinitely many tiny impulses piled on top of each other, and a linear system simply sums up the responses to each of those tiny impulses.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- The unit impulse $\delta(t)$ has an **area of 1** (mathematically: $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t)\,dt = 1$) and is dimensionless in a normalized sense, but in SI units it carries the unit of the input (e.g., Volts, Newtons).
- The unit step $u(t)$ is **dimensionless** (or carries the unit of the input signal) and represents an instantaneous jump from 0 to **1** at $t=0$.
- Time constants $\tau$ in the step response are measured in **seconds (s)**.
- The steady-state value of $s(t)$ as $t \to \infty$ equals the **DC gain** of the system, a critical metric in control engineering.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Logic — Step by Step

**Step 1: System Characterization via Impulse Response**
For any LTI system, the response to an arbitrary input $x(t)$ is given by the **convolution integral**:

$$y(t) = x(t) * h(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) h(t-\tau)\, d\tau$$

This means that once you know $h(t)$, you can predict the output for **every possible input** — impulse response is the most complete descriptor of an LTI system.

**Step 2: Defining the Step Response**
Applying the definition, the step response is:

$$s(t) = u(t) * h(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} u(\tau) h(t-\tau)\, d\tau$$

**Step 3: Deriving the Fundamental Relationship**
Since $u(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} \delta(\tau)\, d\tau$ (the step is the integral of the impulse), and exploiting LTI properties, the step response is the *time integral* of the impulse response:

$$s(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} h(\tau)\, d\tau$$

Conversely, the impulse response is the *time derivative* of the step response:

$$h(t) = \frac{d\,s(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 4: Causality, Stability, and the Response**
- **Causal LTI system** $\Rightarrow$ $h(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$, equivalently $s(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$.
- **BIBO Stable LTI system** $\Rightarrow$ $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} |h(\tau)|\, d\tau < \infty$ (absolutely integrable), and the step response reaches a **finite steady-state value**.

**Step 5: The Discrete-Time Counterpart**
The same logic holds with summations replacing integrals:

$$s[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} h[k] \quad \text{and} \quad h[n] = s[n] - s[n-1]$$

**Step 6: Response in the Frequency Domain**
Taking the Laplace transform (continuous-time) or Z-transform (discrete-time):

$$H(s) = \mathcal{L}\{h(t)\}, \quad S(s) = \frac{H(s)}{s}$$

This is because $\mathcal{L}\{u(t)\} = \frac{1}{s}$, and the transform of a convolution is a product.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU module examination.**

| # | Concept | Continuous-Time (CT) | Discrete-Time (DT) |
|---|---------|---------------------|-------------------|
| 1 | **Impulse Response Definition** | $h(t) = y(t)$ when $x(t) = \delta(t)$ | $h[n] = y[n]$ when $x[n] = \delta[n]$ |
| 2 | **Step Response Definition** | $s(t) = y(t)$ when $x(t) = u(t)$ | $s[n] = y[n]$ when $x[n] = u[n]$ |
| 3 | **Step from Impulse** | $s(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} h(\tau)\, d\tau$ | $s[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{n} h[k]$ |
| 4 | **Impulse from Step** | $h(t) = \dfrac{ds(t)}{dt}$ | $h[n] = s[n] - s[n-1]$ |
| 5 | **Arbitrary Input Output** | $y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau)h(t-\tau)d\tau$ | $y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k]h[n-k]$ |
| 6 | **Laplace / Z-domain link** | $S(s) = H(s)/s$ | $S(z) = \dfrac{H(z)}{1 - z^{-1}}$ |
| 7 | **Causality Condition** | $h(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$ | $h[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$ |
| 8 | **BIBO Stability** | $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h(\tau)\vert d\tau < \infty$ | $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[n] \vert < \infty$ |
| 9 | **Steady-State Value** | $\lim_{t \to \infty} s(t) = H(0)$ | $\lim_{n \to \infty} s[n] = H(e^{j0})$ |
| 10 | **Final Value Theorem** | $\lim_{t \to \infty} s(t) = \lim_{s \to 0} s\cdot S(s)$ | $\lim_{n \to \infty} s[n] = \lim_{(1-z^{-1})\to 1} (1-z^{-1})S(z)$ |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Control Systems**: Step response specifications (rise time $t_r$, peak time $t_p$, settling time $t_s$, percent overshoot $\%M_p$, steady-state error $e_{ss}$) are the **primary design metrics** in control engineering. Every industrial PID controller is tuned using step response data.
- **Filter Design**: Digital and analog filters (low-pass, high-pass, band-pass) are routinely specified and validated by their impulse and step responses.
- **Communication Channels**: Channel equalization in 4G/5G/6G systems uses the impulse response of the multipath channel to undo distortion.
- **Audio & Acoustics**: Room impulse responses (RIRs) are used in concert hall design, noise cancellation, and audio convolution reverb.
- **Biomedical Engineering**: ECG, EEG, and EMG systems characterize electrode and amplifier behavior through step and impulse responses.
- **Structural Engineering**: The impulse response of a building or bridge reveals its natural frequencies, used in earthquake engineering.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Step Response as Integral of Impulse Response

We begin with the formal definition that the step response is the output when the input is $u(t)$:

$$s(t) = y_u(t) = u(t) * h(t)$$

Writing out the convolution integral explicitly:

$$s(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} u(\tau) \cdot h(t-\tau)\, d\tau$$

Now we use the **sifting property** of the unit step function. Recall that $u(\tau) = 0$ for $\tau < 0$ and $u(\tau) = 1$ for $\tau \geq 0$. Therefore, the integrand is non-zero only when $\tau \geq 0$:

$$s(t) = \int_{0}^{\infty} h(t-\tau)\, d\tau$$

Next, we perform the substitution $\lambda = t - \tau$. Then $d\lambda = -d\tau$. The limits transform as: when $\tau = 0$, $\lambda = t$; and when $\tau \to \infty$, $\lambda \to -\infty$. Reversing the limits to remove the negative sign:

$$s(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} h(\lambda)\, d\lambda$$

Renaming $\lambda$ back to $\tau$ (dummy variable) gives the final result:

$$\boxed{\,s(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} h(\tau)\, d\tau\,}$$

This is a **KTU-board-favorite result**. Memorize it.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Impulse Response as Derivative of Step Response

Starting from the result above, differentiate both sides with respect to $t$. By the **Fundamental Theorem of Calculus**:

$$\frac{ds(t)}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}\int_{-\infty}^{t} h(\tau)\, d\tau = h(t)$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\,h(t) = \frac{d\,s(t)}{dt}\,}$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Laplace-Domain Relationship

In the $s$-domain, the impulse input has transform $\mathcal{L}\{\delta(t)\} = 1$, and the step input has transform $\mathcal{L}\{u(t)\} = 1/s$. Since the output in the $s$-domain is the product of the input transform and the system's transfer function $H(s)$:

For impulse input:
$$H(s) = H(s) \cdot 1 = H(s)$$

For step input:
$$S(s) = H(s) \cdot \frac{1}{s}$$

Hence:
$$S(s) = \frac{H(s)}{s} \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad H(s) = s \cdot S(s)$$

This agrees with the time-domain derivation because multiplication by $s$ in Laplace corresponds to differentiation in time, and division by $s$ corresponds to integration.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-style problem**: Given $h(t) = e^{-2t} u(t)$, find the step response $s(t)$.

**Step 1**: Write down the integral formula from the derivation above.

$$s(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} h(\tau)\, d\tau = \int_{-\infty}^{t} e^{-2\tau} u(\tau)\, d\tau$$

**Step 2**: Apply the unit step restriction. Since $u(\tau) = 0$ for $\tau < 0$, the integrand is non-zero only for $\tau \geq 0$. We must consider two cases.

**Case (a): $t < 0$**
The upper limit of integration $t$ is less than $0$, so the entire integration range $[\tau = -\infty, \tau = t]$ lies in the region where $u(\tau) = 0$:

$$s(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} 0 \cdot d\tau = 0 \quad \text{for } t < 0$$

**Case (b): $t \geq 0$**
The integration range spans from $\tau = -\infty$ (where $u(\tau) = 0$) to $\tau = t$ (where $u(\tau) = 1$). Effectively, we integrate the exponential from $0$ to $t$:

$$s(t) = \int_{0}^{t} e^{-2\tau}\, d\tau$$

**Step 3**: Evaluate the integral.

$$s(t) = \left[ \frac{e^{-2\tau}}{-2} \right]_{0}^{t} = -\frac{1}{2}\left(e^{-2t} - e^{0}\right) = \frac{1}{2}\left(1 - e^{-2t}\right)$$

**Step 4**: Combine both cases using the unit step function.

$$\boxed{\,s(t) = \frac{1}{2}\left(1 - e^{-2t}\right) u(t)\,}$$

**Step 5 (Verification)**: Compute the steady-state value.

$$\lim_{t \to \infty} s(t) = \frac{1}{2}(1 - 0) = \frac{1}{2}$$

This equals $H(0) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} h(t)\, dt = \int_0^{\infty} e^{-2t}\, dt = \frac{1}{2}$ ✓. The system is **stable** and **causal**.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Properties of Impulse and Step Responses

| Property | Impulse Response $h(t)$ | Step Response $s(t)$ |
|----------|------------------------|---------------------|
| **Initial Value** | $h(0^+)$ (may be impulse or finite) | $s(0^+) = \int_{-\infty}^{0^+} h(\tau)d\tau$ |
| **Final Value (stable system)** | $\to 0$ (typically) | $\to H(0) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} h(\tau)d\tau$ |
| **Laplace Domain** | $H(s)$ | $H(s)/s$ |
| **Forces the system suddenly?** | Yes (instantaneous shock) | No (sustained input) |
| **Reveals memory of system?** | Yes (entire temporal behavior) | Yes (cumulative behavior) |
| **Used for** | System identification, deconvolution | Rise time, overshoot, settling time |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — System Response Architecture

The following Mermaid flowchart illustrates the complete functional architecture of how an LTI system produces both responses, and how they interrelate mathematically.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode([Signal and System Analysis Begins])
    startNode --> inputChoice{Choose Input Signal}
    inputChoice -->|Impulse delta t| impPath[Apply x of t equals delta of t]
    inputChoice -->|Step u of t| stepPath[Apply x of t equals u of t]
    impPath --> sysBlock1[Relaxed LTI System H of s]
    stepPath --> sysBlock2[Relaxed LTI System H of s]
    sysBlock1 --> outImp[Output h of t equals Impulse Response]
    sysBlock2 --> outStep[Output s of t equals Step Response]
    outImp --> relCheck{Relationship between h and s}
    outStep --> relCheck
    relCheck --> integOp[Integration: s of t equals integral of h of tau d tau from minus infinity to t]
    relCheck --> diffOp[Differentiation: h of t equals d s of t divided by d t]
    relCheck --> laplaceOp[Laplace Domain: S of s equals H of s divided by s]
    integOp --> finalOut[Complete System Characterization]
    diffOp --> finalOut
    laplaceOp --> finalOut
    finalOut --> useCase{Practical Use}
    useCase --> uc1[Control System Design]
    useCase --> uc2[Filter Specification]
    useCase --> uc3[Channel Equalization]
    useCase --> uc4[System Identification]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Causality and Stability Check Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    hInput[Given h of t or h of n]
    hInput --> causalCheck{h of t equals zero for t less than zero?}
    causalCheck -->|Yes| causalYes[System is Causal]
    causalCheck -->|No| causalNo[System is Non Causal]
    hInput --> stableCheck{Integral of absolute value of h of tau d tau less than infinity}
    stableCheck -->|Yes| stableYes[System is BIBO Stable]
    stableCheck -->|No| stableNo[System is Unstable]
    causalYes --> combinedCheck[Combine Causal and Stable]
    stableYes --> combinedCheck
    causalNo --> combinedCheck
    stableNo --> combinedCheck
    combinedCheck --> stepBehaviour[Predict Step Response Behaviour]
    stepBehaviour -->|Stable| ssFinite[Steady State Value is Finite]
    stepBehaviour -->|Unstable| ssUnbounded[Step Response Grows Without Bound]
```

### 4.3 ASCII Time-Domain Sketch of Typical Responses

For a stable, causal first-order system with $h(t) = e^{-at} u(t)$ where $a > 0$:

```
IMPULSE RESPONSE h(t):
   h(t)
    |
  1 |*
    | *
    |  * *
    |     * * *
    |          * * * * * * *
  0 |___________________________*_*_*_*_→ t
    0
   (Decays exponentially from 1 to 0)


STEP RESPONSE s(t):
   s(t)
    |
  1 |                    ____________  ← Steady-state = 1/a
    |                .---'
1/a |            .-
    |         .-
    |       .-
    |     .-
    |   .-
  0 |__.-_______________________________→ t
    0
   (Rises smoothly from 0 to 1/a, no overshoot)
```

For a second-order underdamped system (e.g., $h(t) = e^{-\alpha t}\cos(\omega_d t) u(t)$):

```
STEP RESPONSE s(t) for Underdamped 2nd-Order System:
   s(t)
    |        ___
    |       /   \         ___
  1 |------/-----\-------/---\---------  ← Steady-state
    |     /       \     /     \      /
    |    /         \   /       \    /
    |   /           \ /         \  /
    |  /             X           \/
  0 |_/______________________________→ t
    0
   (Overshoots, oscillates, eventually settles)
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define impulse response and step response of an LTI system. State the relationship between them.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **impulse response** of a relaxed LTI system is the output $h(t)$ obtained when the input is a unit impulse $\delta(t)$. The **step response** $s(t)$ is the output when the input is a unit step $u(t)$. The fundamental relationship is:

$$s(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} h(\tau)\, d\tau \quad \text{and} \quad h(t) = \frac{d\,s(t)}{dt}$$

Equivalently, in the Laplace domain: $S(s) = H(s)/s$. **(1 mark for each definition + 1 mark for the relationship).**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*An LTI system has impulse response $h(t) = e^{-3t} u(t)$. Justify whether the system is causal and BIBO stable.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Causality check (1.5 marks):** Since $h(t) = e^{-3t} u(t)$, we have $h(t) = 0$ for all $t < 0$. Therefore, the system **is causal**.

**Stability check (1.5 marks):** Compute the absolute integral:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} |h(t)|\, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-3t}\, dt = \left[\frac{e^{-3t}}{-3}\right]_0^{\infty} = \frac{1}{3} < \infty$$

Since the integral converges to a finite value $1/3$, the system **is BIBO stable**.

---

### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

#### ✏️ QUESTION A — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**(a)** For a relaxed LTI system, the step response is observed to be:
$$s(t) = (1 - e^{-4t})\, u(t)$$
**(i)** Determine the impulse response $h(t)$ of the system. **(3 marks)**
**(ii)** Determine the output $y(t)$ of the system for the input $x(t) = e^{-t} u(t)$. **(4 marks)**

**(b)** A causal LTI system is described by the differential equation:
$$\frac{dy(t)}{dt} + 5\,y(t) = 5\,x(t)$$
**(i)** Find the transfer function $H(s)$ and the impulse response $h(t)$. **(3 marks)**
**(ii)** Find the step response $s(t)$ and its steady-state value. **(4 marks)**

**MODEL SOLUTION:**

**Part (a)(i) — Impulse Response from Step Response (3 marks):**

Differentiate $s(t) = (1 - e^{-4t}) u(t)$ using the product rule:

$$h(t) = \frac{ds(t)}{dt} = \frac{d}{dt}\big[(1 - e^{-4t})u(t)\big]$$

The function $(1 - e^{-4t})$ is 0 at $t=0$, so the impulse at $t=0$ from the unit step *multiplied by zero* vanishes. Thus:

$$h(t) = (1 - e^{-4t})\delta(t) + 4 e^{-4t} u(t)$$

$$h(t) = 0 \cdot \delta(t) + 4 e^{-4t} u(t)$$

$$\boxed{h(t) = 4 e^{-4t} u(t)}$$

**Part (a)(ii) — Output for Exponential Input (4 marks):**

Use the convolution $y(t) = x(t) * h(t)$:

$$y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau) h(t-\tau) d\tau = \int_0^{t} e^{-\tau} \cdot 4 e^{-4(t-\tau)} d\tau$$

$$= 4 e^{-4t} \int_0^{t} e^{-\tau} e^{4\tau} d\tau = 4 e^{-4t} \int_0^{t} e^{3\tau} d\tau$$

$$= 4 e^{-4t} \left[\frac{e^{3\tau}}{3}\right]_0^{t} = 4 e^{-4t} \cdot \frac{e^{3t} - 1}{3}$$

$$= \frac{4}{3} \left(e^{-t} - e^{-4t}\right)$$

Multiplying by $u(t)$ to enforce causality:

$$\boxed{y(t) = \frac{4}{3}\left(e^{-t} - e^{-4t}\right) u(t)}$$

**Part (b)(i) — Transfer Function and Impulse Response (3 marks):**

Take the Laplace transform of the differential equation (zero initial conditions for a relaxed system):

$$sY(s) + 5Y(s) = 5X(s) \quad \Rightarrow \quad Y(s)[s + 5] = 5X(s)$$

$$H(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{X(s)} = \frac{5}{s+5}$$

To find $h(t)$, perform partial-fraction-free inverse Laplace using the standard pair $\mathcal{L}^{-1}\{1/(s+a)\} = e^{-at} u(t)$:

$$\boxed{h(t) = 5 e^{-5t} u(t)}$$

**Part (b)(ii) — Step Response and Steady-State (4 marks):**

The step response in Laplace domain is:

$$S(s) = \frac{H(s)}{s} = \frac{5}{s(s+5)}$$

Apply partial fractions:

$$\frac{5}{s(s+5)} = \frac{A}{s} + \frac{B}{s+5}$$

Multiply both sides by $s(s+5)$: $5 = A(s+5) + Bs$

Setting $s = 0$: $5 = 5A \Rightarrow A = 1$.
Setting $s = -5$: $5 = -5B \Rightarrow B = -1$.

So:

$$S(s) = \frac{1}{s} - \frac{1}{s+5}$$

Inverse Laplace transform:

$$\boxed{s(t) = \left(1 - e^{-5t}\right) u(t)}$$

**Steady-state value** (using final value theorem):

$$\lim_{t \to \infty} s(t) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot S(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{5}{s(s+5)} = \lim_{s \to 0} \frac{5}{s+5} = \frac{5}{5} = 1$$

This also equals $H(0) = 5/5 = 1$ ✓.

---

#### ✏️ QUESTION B — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme] (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** An LTI system is described by the impulse response:
$$h(t) = 2 e^{-3t} u(t) + \delta(t-1)$$
**(i)** Verify whether the system is causal and BIBO stable. **(3 marks)**
**(ii)** Determine the step response $s(t)$ of the system. **(4 marks)**

**(b)** A discrete-time LTI system is described by the difference equation:
$$y[n] - \frac{1}{2} y[n-1] = x[n]$$
**(i)** Find the system function $H(z)$ and the impulse response $h[n]$. **(3 marks)**
**(ii)** Find the step response $s[n]$ and verify the steady-state value. **(4 marks)**

**MODEL SOLUTION:**

**Part (a)(i) — Causality and Stability Check (3 marks):**

**Causality:** $h(t)$ contains $\delta(t-1)$ which is non-zero at $t=1$ (future time), and $2e^{-3t}u(t)$ which is non-zero for $t \geq 0$. Since $h(t) \neq 0$ for $t = 1 > 0$, this is **OK** — there is no output for $t < 0$. The system **is causal** because $h(t) = 0$ for all $t < 0$. **(1.5 marks)**

**Stability:** Compute the absolute integral:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} |h(t)|\, dt = \int_0^{\infty} 2 e^{-3t}\, dt + \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} |\delta(t-1)|\, dt$$

The integral of $|\delta(t-1)|$ over all reals is 1 (unit area). So:

$$= 2 \cdot \frac{1}{3} + 1 = \frac{2}{3} + 1 = \frac{5}{3} < \infty$$

The system **is BIBO stable**. **(1.5 marks)**

**Part (a)(ii) — Step Response (4 marks):**

$$s(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} h(\tau)\, d\tau = \int_{-\infty}^{t} \left[2e^{-3\tau}u(\tau) + \delta(\tau-1)\right] d\tau$$

$$= \int_0^{t} 2 e^{-3\tau}\, d\tau + \int_{-\infty}^{t} \delta(\tau-1)\, d\tau$$

The first integral:

$$\int_0^{t} 2 e^{-3\tau}\, d\tau = 2 \left[\frac{e^{-3\tau}}{-3}\right]_0^{t} = \frac{2}{3}\left(1 - e^{-3t}\right) \quad \text{for } t \geq 0$$

The second integral (sifting property of the delta function):

$$\int_{-\infty}^{t} \delta(\tau-1)\, d\tau = u(t-1)$$

Therefore:

$$s(t) = \frac{2}{3}\left(1 - e^{-3t}\right) u(t) + u(t-1)$$

**Part (b)(i) — System Function and Impulse Response (3 marks):**

Take the Z-transform of the difference equation:

$$Y(z) - \frac{1}{2} z^{-1} Y(z) = X(z)$$

$$H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \frac{1}{1 - \frac{1}{2} z^{-1}}$$

Using the standard Z-transform pair $\mathcal{Z}^{-1}\left\{\dfrac{1}{1-az^{-1}}\right\} = a^n u[n]$ with $a = 1/2$:

$$\boxed{h[n] = \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^n u[n]}$$

**Part (b)(ii) — Step Response and Steady-State (4 marks):**

In the Z-domain:

$$S(z) = \frac{H(z)}{1 - z^{-1}} = \frac{1}{(1 - z^{-1})(1 - \frac{1}{2} z^{-1})}$$

Apply partial fractions:

$$S(z) = \frac{A}{1 - z^{-1}} + \frac{B}{1 - \frac{1}{2} z^{-1}}$$

Multiply both sides by $(1 - z^{-1})(1 - \frac{1}{2}z^{-1})$:

$$1 = A\left(1 - \frac{1}{2}z^{-1}\right) + B(1 - z^{-1})$$

Set $z^{-1} = 1$ (i.e., $z = 1$): $1 = A(1 - 1/2) = A/2 \Rightarrow A = 2$.
Set $z^{-1} = 2$ (i.e., $z = 1/2$): $1 = B(1 - 2) = -B \Rightarrow B = -1$.

So:

$$S(z) = \frac{2}{1 - z^{-1}} - \frac{1}{1 - \frac{1}{2}z^{-1}}$$

Inverse Z-transform:

$$s[n] = 2\,u[n] - \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^n u[n] = \left[2 - \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^n\right] u[n]$$

**Steady-state value:**

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} s[n] = 2 - 0 = 2$$

This equals $H(1) = 1/(1 - 1/2) = 2$ ✓.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the unit step $u(t)$ or $u[n]$** in the final answer. The system is causal only if the response is explicitly multiplied by the step function. **(−1 to −2 marks)**
> 2. **Confusing $h(t)$ and $H(s)$**: $h(t)$ is a time-domain function; $H(s)$ is its Laplace transform. They are not interchangeable. **(−1 mark)**
> 3. **Improper convolution limits**: Always enforce $u(\tau)$ in the integrand to correctly set the lower limit to 0 for causal systems.
> 4. **Final value theorem misuse**: You must verify all poles of $sS(s)$ are in the open left-half plane (continuous) or strictly inside the unit circle (discrete) before applying the final value theorem. Blindly applying it for unstable systems is a **common error**.
> 5. **Missing steady-state verification**: KTU examiners often award 1 mark for verifying the answer using $H(0)$ or the final value theorem — don't skip it.

---

### 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The **impulse response $h(t)$** is the most complete time-domain description of an LTI system.
- ✅ The **step response $s(t)$** is the integral of the impulse response: $s(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} h(\tau)\, d\tau$.
- ✅ The impulse response is the derivative of the step response: $h(t) = \dfrac{ds(t)}{dt}$.
- ✅ In the **Laplace domain**: $S(s) = H(s)/s$, which makes computing $s(t)$ via partial fractions a powerful KTU exam technique.
- ✅ In the **Z-domain**: $S(z) = H(z)/(1 - z^{-1})$.
- ✅ **Causality** requires $h(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$ (and $h[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$).
- ✅ **BIBO Stability** requires $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h(\tau)\vert d\tau < \infty$ (or $\sum \vert h[n]\vert < \infty$).
- ✅ **Steady-state value** of step response = $H(0) = \int h(t)\, dt = H(s)\big|_{s=0}$ — use this as a **self-check**.
- ✅ **Time constants $\tau$** govern how fast the response settles; smaller $\tau$ = faster response.
- ✅ **Second-order underdamped systems** exhibit overshoot, ringing, and oscillation in their step response.
- ✅ Always **multiply the final answer by $u(t)$ or $u[n]$** to enforce causality in KTU answers.
- ✅ The **final value theorem** is your friend for quick steady-state computation, but check pole locations first.
- ✅ The convolution integral is **commutative**: $x(t) * h(t) = h(t) * x(t)$ — choose whichever form is computationally easier.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fourier transform: existence and properties of continuous time Fourier transform
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT522 - INTRODUCTION TO SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fourier transform: existence and properties of continuous time Fourier transform

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition: The Continuous-Time Fourier Transform (CTFT)

The **Continuous-Time Fourier Transform (CTFT)** is a mathematical transform that decomposes a continuous, non-periodic time-domain signal $x(t)$ into its constituent frequency components. It extends the concept of the Fourier Series (which handles periodic signals) to aperiodic signals by treating the fundamental period $T \to \infty$, causing the discrete harmonic spectrum to merge into a continuous spectrum.

The forward (analysis) equation and inverse (synthesis) equation are given by:

$$X(j\omega) = \mathcal{F}\{x(t)\} = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t)\, e^{-j\omega t}\, dt$$

$$x(t) = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\{X(j\omega)\} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} X(j\omega)\, e^{j\omega t}\, d\omega$$

Where:
- $X(j\omega)$ is the **frequency-domain representation** (complex spectrum).
- $\omega = 2\pi f$ is the **angular frequency** in **radians/second (rad/s)**.
- $j = \sqrt{-1}$ is the imaginary unit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The notation $X(j\omega)$ is preferred over $X(\omega)$ in KTU-approved textbooks because it explicitly indicates that the Fourier transform is a function of a purely imaginary exponential $e^{j\omega t}$. The independent variable $j\omega$ emphasizes the Laplace transform connection $s = \sigma + j\omega$ evaluated at $\sigma = 0$.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Prism of Sound

Imagine you are standing in front of a **prism** holding a beam of white sunlight. The prism does not create the colors (red, orange, violet); it merely *separates* the white light into its constituent spectral colors. The **Fourier Transform is the mathematical prism for signals**.

- A piece of music (a complex waveform) is played in a room.
- Your ear/brain is effectively performing a "Fourier analysis" by identifying the individual piano notes, drum beats, and vocal frequencies that have been superimposed.
- The Fourier transform $X(j\omega)$ is the formal, mathematical recipe that tells you *exactly* how much of each "pure tone" (frequency $\omega$) is present in the signal $x(t)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Concept:** Any well-behaved signal $x(t)$ can be thought of as a **continuous sum (integral) of infinite complex exponentials** $e^{j\omega t}$ of all possible frequencies, each scaled by a complex amplitude $X(j\omega)$.

## 1.3 Why Do We Need a Fourier Transform?

In the **time domain**, a signal's information is encoded in its amplitude variation with respect to time. In the **frequency domain**, the same information is encoded in the amplitude and phase of its spectral components. Many real-world engineering problems become dramatically simpler in the frequency domain:

- **Filtering:** Removing noise above 5 kHz from an audio recording is impossible to express in the time domain, but trivial in the frequency domain (a rectangular window).
- **Communication:** Modulation, demodulation, and bandwidth allocation are inherently spectral operations.
- **Control Systems:** Stability and bandwidth of a system are analyzed via its frequency response $H(j\omega)$.
- **Image Processing:** Edge detection, blurring, and compression (like JPEG) all rely on 2D Fourier analysis.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Application:** In a **5G smartphone base station**, signals are converted between time and frequency domains thousands of times per second using algorithms based on the Fast Fourier Transform (FFT) to enable Orthogonal Frequency Division Multiplexing (OFDM).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Existence of the Fourier Transform: The Dirichlet Conditions

Not every signal $x(t)$ has a Fourier transform. The transform $X(j\omega)$ exists if and only if $x(t)$ satisfies the **Dirichlet Conditions** for absolute integrability. These conditions are **sufficient** (not always strictly necessary, but standard in KTU examinations).

A signal $x(t)$ has a valid CTFT if:

1. **Condition 1 (Absolute Integrability):** The signal must be absolutely integrable over all time.
   $$\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert x(t) \vert \, dt < \infty$$
   This guarantees the integral $\int x(t) e^{-j\omega t} dt$ converges. Since $\vert e^{-j\omega t} \vert = 1$, the magnitude of the integrand is bounded by $\vert x(t) \vert$.

2. **Condition 2 (Finite Number of Discontinuities):** In any finite interval, the signal $x(t)$ has only a **finite number of maxima, minima, and discontinuities**.

3. **Condition 3 (Finite Energy in a Period for periodic signals):** For periodic signals, the signal must have finite energy in one period.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Signals like $x(t) = e^{-at}u(t)$ for $a > 0$ are absolutely integrable and have a valid CTFT. However, signals that are not absolutely integrable (like $u(t)$, $\sin(\omega_0 t)$, or periodic signals) can still have Fourier transforms if we admit **generalized functions (distributions)** like the Dirac delta $\delta(\omega)$. KTU questions frequently test this exact nuance.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet: Properties of CTFT

The following table contains the **most frequently tested properties** of the Continuous-Time Fourier Transform as per the KTU 2024 syllabus. Let $x(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} X(j\omega)$ and $y(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} Y(j\omega)$.

| **Property** | **Time Domain** | **Frequency Domain** | **KTU Significance** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Linearity** | $a\,x(t) + b\,y(t)$ | $a\,X(j\omega) + b\,Y(j\omega)$ | Foundation for all system analysis. |
| **Time Shifting** | $x(t - t_0)$ | $e^{-j\omega t_0} X(j\omega)$ | Magnitude unchanged; phase shifted by $-\omega t_0$. |
| **Frequency Shifting** | $e^{j\omega_0 t} x(t)$ | $X(j(\omega - \omega_0))$ | Core principle behind Amplitude Modulation (AM). |
| **Time Scaling** | $x(at)$ | $\frac{1}{\vert a \vert} X\!\left(j\frac{\omega}{a}\right)$ | Compression in time $\Rightarrow$ Expansion in frequency. |
| **Time Reversal** | $x(-t)$ | $X(-j\omega)$ | Useful for even/odd symmetry decomposition. |
| **Conjugation** | $x^*(t)$ | $X^*(-j\omega)$ | Crucial for real signals. |
| **Duality** | $X(jt)$ | $2\pi\,x(-\omega)$ | Symmetry property; lets us compute inverse pairs. |
| **Convolution** | $x(t) * y(t)$ | $X(j\omega) \cdot Y(j\omega)$ | The most important property: LTI system response. |
| **Multiplication** | $x(t) \cdot y(t)$ | $\frac{1}{2\pi} X(j\omega) * Y(j\omega)$ | Dual of convolution. Used in sampling theorem. |
| **Differentiation (Time)** | $\frac{d^n x(t)}{dt^n}$ | $(j\omega)^n X(j\omega)$ | Converts derivatives to algebraic multiplications. |
| **Integration (Time)** | $\int_{-\infty}^{t} x(\tau)d\tau$ | $\frac{X(j\omega)}{j\omega} + \pi X(0)\delta(\omega)$ | Used in solving differential equations. |
| **Parseval's Theorem** | $E = \int \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt$ | $E = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int \vert X(j\omega) \vert^2 d\omega$ | Energy conservation between time and frequency. |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL FORMULA:** The **Convolution Property** is the cornerstone of Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) system analysis. If $h(t)$ is the impulse response and $x(t)$ is the input, the output is $y(t) = x(t) * h(t)$, and in the frequency domain, $Y(j\omega) = X(j\omega) \cdot H(j\omega)$, where $H(j\omega)$ is the **Frequency Response** of the system.

## 2.3 Why Are These Properties Important in Engineering?

- **Modulation Theorem (Frequency Shifting):** When you tune a radio to 98.6 MHz, the audio signal (baseband) is multiplied by a high-frequency carrier. In the frequency domain, this shifts the audio spectrum to the carrier frequency.
- **Differentiation Property:** A differentiator circuit (op-amp based) has frequency response $H(j\omega) = j\omega RC$. This is why differentiators inherently **amplify high-frequency noise**.
- **Parseval's Theorem:** Engineers use it to compute the total power of a signal in the frequency domain, which is essential for signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) calculations in communication receivers.
- **Duality:** A rectangular pulse in time has a sinc function in frequency. By duality, a rectangular pulse in frequency (an ideal low-pass filter) has a sinc impulse response.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** In the time domain, **shifting** $\Rightarrow$ phase ramp in frequency. **Scaling** $\Rightarrow$ inverse scaling plus amplitude division. **Differentiation** $\Rightarrow$ multiplication by $j\omega$. **Integration** $\Rightarrow$ division by $j\omega$ (with a DC delta correction).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: The Time Shifting Property

**Statement:** If $x(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} X(j\omega)$, then $x(t - t_0) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} e^{-j\omega t_0} X(j\omega)$.

**Step-by-Step Proof:**

We start with the formal definition applied to the shifted signal $x(t - t_0)$.

$$\mathcal{F}\{x(t - t_0)\} = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t - t_0)\, e^{-j\omega t}\, dt$$

Introduce a substitution: Let $\tau = t - t_0$. Then, the differential becomes $d\tau = dt$. The limits of integration remain $-\infty$ to $+\infty$ because the shift does not change the bounds.

$$= \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, e^{-j\omega(\tau + t_0)}\, d\tau$$

Using the algebraic identity $e^{A+B} = e^A \cdot e^B$, we separate the constant term involving $t_0$ from the integrand.

$$= \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, e^{-j\omega \tau}\, e^{-j\omega t_0}\, d\tau$$

Since $e^{-j\omega t_0}$ does not depend on the integration variable $\tau$, we pull it out of the integral as a constant factor.

$$= e^{-j\omega t_0} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, e^{-j\omega \tau}\, d\tau$$

The remaining integral is, by definition, exactly the Fourier transform $X(j\omega)$ of $x(t)$.

$$= e^{-j\omega t_0} X(j\omega)$$

$$\boxed{\therefore x(t - t_0) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} e^{-j\omega t_0} X(j\omega)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** Shifting a signal in time by $t_0$ does **not change** its magnitude spectrum $\vert X(j\omega) \vert$. It only introduces a **linear phase shift** of $-\omega t_0$ radians. This is critical in antenna array design, where path delays correspond to phase shifts.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: The Differentiation in Time Property

**Statement:** If $x(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} X(j\omega)$, then $\dfrac{dx(t)}{dt} \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} j\omega\, X(j\omega)$.

**Step-by-Step Proof:**

We begin with the inverse Fourier transform (synthesis equation).

$$x(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} X(j\omega)\, e^{j\omega t}\, d\omega$$

Differentiating both sides with respect to $t$:

$$\frac{dx(t)}{dt} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} X(j\omega) \cdot \frac{\partial}{\partial t}\left(e^{j\omega t}\right) d\omega$$

The derivative of $e^{j\omega t}$ with respect to $t$ is simply $j\omega e^{j\omega t}$ (since $\omega$ is constant with respect to $t$).

$$\frac{dx(t)}{dt} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} j\omega\, X(j\omega)\, e^{j\omega t}\, d\omega$$

This integral is precisely the inverse Fourier transform of the function $j\omega X(j\omega)$.

$$\boxed{\therefore \frac{dx(t)}{dt} \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} j\omega\, X(j\omega)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Generalization:** By induction, the $n$-th derivative is $\dfrac{d^n x(t)}{dt^n} \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} (j\omega)^n X(j\omega)$. This property is the foundation of solving linear differential equations in the frequency domain, transforming calculus problems into algebra.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: The Convolution Property

**Statement:** If $x(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} X(j\omega)$ and $y(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} Y(j\omega)$, then $x(t) * y(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} X(j\omega) \cdot Y(j\omega)$.

**Step-by-Step Proof:**

Recall the convolution integral definition:

$$z(t) = x(t) * y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, y(t - \tau)\, d\tau$$

Taking the Fourier transform of both sides:

$$Z(j\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, y(t - \tau)\, d\tau \right] e^{-j\omega t}\, dt$$

Interchanging the order of integration (justified by Fubini's theorem for well-behaved signals):

$$Z(j\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau) \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} y(t - \tau)\, e^{-j\omega t}\, dt \right] d\tau$$

Applying the **Time Shifting Property** to the inner integral (with shift $t_0 = \tau$):

$$Z(j\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau) \left[ e^{-j\omega \tau} Y(j\omega) \right] d\tau$$

Since $Y(j\omega)$ is independent of $\tau$, we extract it from the inner integral:

$$Z(j\omega) = Y(j\omega) \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, e^{-j\omega \tau}\, d\tau$$

The remaining integral is the definition of $X(j\omega)$.

$$Z(j\omega) = X(j\omega) \cdot Y(j\omega)$$

$$\boxed{\therefore x(t) * y(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} X(j\omega) \cdot Y(j\omega)}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Impact:** This is the **single most important property in signal processing**. It allows an LTI system with impulse response $h(t)$ to be analyzed by simple multiplication $Y(j\omega) = X(j\omega)H(j\omega)$ in the frequency domain, completely bypassing the complex convolution integral in the time domain.

## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example: Application of Multiple Properties

**Problem:** Find the Fourier transform of the signal $g(t) = \dfrac{d}{dt}\left\{ e^{-2t} u(t) \right\}$.

**Solution Strategy:** We will use the **Differentiation Property** combined with the known transform of the decaying exponential.

**Step 1:** Identify the base signal whose transform we know.

The base signal is $x(t) = e^{-2t} u(t)$ (a right-sided decaying exponential).

**Step 2:** Recall the standard CTFT pair for a decaying exponential.

For $x(t) = e^{-at}u(t)$ with $a > 0$:

$$X(j\omega) = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-at} e^{-j\omega t}\, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-(a+j\omega)t}\, dt = \frac{1}{a + j\omega}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the known result with $a = 2$.

$$e^{-2t} u(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} \frac{1}{2 + j\omega}$$

**Step 4:** Apply the Differentiation Property.

Since $g(t) = \dfrac{d}{dt}\{ e^{-2t} u(t) \}$, by the differentiation property:

$$G(j\omega) = j\omega \cdot \frac{1}{2 + j\omega}$$

**Step 5:** Simplify the final expression (algebraic manipulation).

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{j\omega}{2 + j\omega}$$

We can perform polynomial long division or rewrite as a constant plus a remainder:

$$\frac{j\omega}{2 + j\omega} = \frac{(j\omega + 2) - 2}{2 + j\omega} = 1 - \frac{2}{2 + j\omega}$$

$$\boxed{G(j\omega) = 1 - \frac{2}{2 + j\omega} = \frac{j\omega}{2 + j\omega}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Check:** The derivative of $e^{-2t}u(t)$ is $-2e^{-2t}u(t) + e^{-2t}\delta(t) = \delta(t) - 2e^{-2t}u(t)$. Its Fourier transform is $1 - \frac{2}{2+j\omega}$, which matches our result perfectly. This confirms the algebraic manipulation is correct.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: LTI System Analysis via Fourier Transform

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the complete signal flow path for analyzing a Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) system in the frequency domain, demonstrating the **Convolution Property** in action.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Input Signal x of t"] --> B["Forward CTFT"]
    B --> C["Spectrum X of j omega"]
    C --> D["Multiply by H of j omega"]
    D --> E["Output Spectrum Y of j omega"]
    E --> F["Inverse CTFT"]
    F --> G["Output Signal y of t"]
    
    H["Impulse Response h of t"] --> I["Forward CTFT"]
    I --> J["Frequency Response H of j omega"]
    J --> D
    
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style G fill:#e1f5ff
    style C fill:#fff4e1
    style E fill:#fff4e1
    style J fill:#ffe1e1
    style D fill:#e1ffe1
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: The CTFT Property Hierarchy

This diagram maps out the **logical dependencies** between the various properties of the Fourier Transform, showing which properties are derived from which.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Linearity Property"] --> P2["Time Shifting Property"]
    P2 --> P3["Frequency Shifting Property"]
    P2 --> P4["Convolution Property"]
    P2 --> P5["Differentiation in Time"]
    P3 --> P6["Modulation Theorem Application"]
    P5 --> P7["Integration in Time"]
    P4 --> P8["LTI System Analysis Y equals H times X"]
    P5 --> P8
    
    P9["Duality Property"] --> P10["Inverse Transform Computation"]
    P11["Parsevals Theorem"] --> P12["Energy Computation"]
    
    style P1 fill:#ffd700
    style P2 fill:#ffd700
    style P3 fill:#87ceeb
    style P4 fill:#ff6347
    style P5 fill:#90ee90
    style P8 fill:#ff1493
    style P12 fill:#dda0dd
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Block Diagram: Time-Frequency Duality

The following schematic illustrates the **transform pair architecture**, showing the bidirectional mapping between time and frequency domains.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TD["Time Domain"]
        T1["x of t: Continuous Signal"]
        T2["h of t: Impulse Response"]
        T3["y of t: Output equals x convolve h"]
    end
    
    subgraph FD["Frequency Domain"]
        F1["X of j omega: Spectrum"]
        F2["H of j omega: Frequency Response"]
        F3["Y of j omega: Output Spectrum"]
    end
    
    T1 <-->|"Forward and Inverse CTFT"| F1
    T2 <-->|"Forward and Inverse CTFT"| F2
    T3 <-->|"Forward and Inverse CTFT"| F3
    
    T1 -->|"Convolution Operation"| T3
    F1 -->|"Multiplication by H of j omega"| F3
    T2 -->|"Convolution Operation"| T3
    F2 -->|"Multiplication with X of j omega"| F3
    
    style T1 fill:#e0f7fa
    style T3 fill:#e0f7fa
    style F1 fill:#fff3e0
    style F3 fill:#fff3e0
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Timeline: Existence Conditions Decision Tree

A structural decision tree for determining whether a given signal possesses a valid CTFT.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q{"Is x of t absolutely integrable?"}
    Q -->|"YES"| R["CTFT Exists: Standard Function"]
    Q -->|"NO"| S{"Is x of t a Periodic Signal?"}
    S -->|"YES"| T["CTFT Exists: Line Spectrum with Delta Functions"]
    S -->|"NO"| U{"Does x of t have Polynomial Growth?"}
    U -->|"YES, like t to the n"| V["CTFT Exists: Generalized Derivatives of Delta"]
    U -->|"NO"| W["CTFT Does Not Exist"]
    
    R --> Z["Valid CTFT"]
    T --> Z
    V --> Z
    
    style Q fill:#ffe4b5
    style Z fill:#90ee90
    style W fill:#ff6347
```

> [!TIP]
> **Diagram Reading Tip:** In the block diagrams above, observe how the convolution in the time domain (complex integral operation) is replaced by a simple **multiplication** in the frequency domain. This is the fundamental reason engineers prefer frequency-domain analysis for LTI systems.
