# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the Dirichlet conditions for the existence of the Fourier transform of a continuous-time signal $x(t)$.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The Fourier transform of a continuous-time signal $x(t)$ exists if the signal satisfies the following Dirichlet conditions:

1. **Absolute Integrability:** The signal $x(t)$ must be absolutely integrable over the entire time axis, i.e.,
   $$\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert x(t) \vert \, dt < \infty$$

2. **Finite Discontinuities:** In any finite time interval, the signal $x(t)$ can have only a **finite number of discontinuities**.

3. **Finite Extrema:** In any finite time interval, the signal $x(t)$ can have only a **finite number of maxima and minima**.

These are **sufficient** (not necessary) conditions. Signals not satisfying absolute integrability (like periodic signals) can still have a Fourier transform defined in the sense of generalized functions involving the Dirac delta $\delta(\omega)$.

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State and prove the time-shifting property of the Continuous-Time Fourier Transform.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Statement:** If $x(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} X(j\omega)$, then $x(t - t_0) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} e^{-j\omega t_0} X(j\omega)$.

**Proof:**

$$\mathcal{F}\{x(t - t_0)\} = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t - t_0)\, e^{-j\omega t}\, dt$$

Let $\tau = t - t_0 \Rightarrow t = \tau + t_0$ and $dt = d\tau$. The integration limits remain $-\infty$ to $+\infty$.

$$= \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, e^{-j\omega(\tau + t_0)}\, d\tau = e^{-j\omega t_0} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(\tau)\, e^{-j\omega \tau}\, d\tau = e^{-j\omega t_0} X(j\omega)$$

Hence proved. The magnitude spectrum $\vert e^{-j\omega t_0} X(j\omega) \vert = \vert X(j\omega) \vert$ is unchanged, but a phase shift of $-\omega t_0$ is introduced.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1): `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** State and prove the following properties of the Continuous-Time Fourier Transform:
   (i) Time Shifting Property
   (ii) Frequency Shifting Property (Modulation Theorem)

**(b) [7 Marks)** Using the properties of the Fourier Transform, determine the Fourier transform of the signal:
   $$g(t) = e^{-3t} u(t-2)$$
   Also, sketch the magnitude spectrum $\vert G(j\omega) \vert$.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

**(i) Time Shifting Property:** Already derived in Section 3.1. (Full marks allocated for the complete substitution and algebraic derivation shown above.)

**(ii) Frequency Shifting Property:**

**Statement:** If $x(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} X(j\omega)$, then $e^{j\omega_0 t} x(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} X(j(\omega - \omega_0))$.

**Proof:**

$$\mathcal{F}\{e^{j\omega_0 t} x(t)\} = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t) e^{j\omega_0 t}\, e^{-j\omega t}\, dt = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t)\, e^{-j(\omega - \omega_0)t}\, dt$$

Substituting $\Omega = \omega - \omega_0$:

$$= \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t)\, e^{-j\Omega t}\, dt = X(j\Omega) = X(j(\omega - \omega_0))$$

$$\boxed{\therefore e^{j\omega_0 t} x(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} X(j(\omega - \omega_0))}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This property is the foundation of Amplitude Modulation. Multiplying a signal by a carrier $e^{j\omega_0 t}$ shifts its entire spectrum to center frequency $\omega_0$.

**Part (b):**

**Given:** $g(t) = e^{-3t} u(t-2)$.

**Step 1:** Rewrite the signal in a more recognizable form.

$$g(t) = e^{-3(t-2)} \cdot e^{-6} \cdot u(t-2) = e^{-6} \cdot e^{-3(t-2)} u(t-2)$$

This is of the form $\text{constant} \times x(t - t_0)$ where $x(t) = e^{-3t} u(t)$ and $t_0 = 2$.

**Step 2:** Find the Fourier transform of the base signal $x(t) = e^{-3t} u(t)$.

$$X(j\omega) = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-3t} e^{-j\omega t}\, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-(3+j\omega)t}\, dt = \left[ \frac{e^{-(3+j\omega)t}}{-(3+j\omega)} \right]_{0}^{\infty} = \frac{1}{3 + j\omega}$$

(evaluated at the upper limit, $e^{-(3+j\omega)t} \to 0$ since real part of exponent is $-3 < 0$.)

**Step 3:** Apply Linearity and Time Shifting properties.

By the **Linearity Property** (factor of $e^{-6}$):

$$e^{-6} \cdot x(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} \frac{e^{-6}}{3 + j\omega}$$

By the **Time Shifting Property** (delay by $t_0 = 2$):

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{e^{-6}}{3 + j\omega} \cdot e^{-j2\omega}$$

**Step 4:** Final simplified answer.

$$\boxed{G(j\omega) = \frac{e^{-6} \cdot e^{-j2\omega}}{3 + j\omega}}$$

**Step 5:** Magnitude Spectrum.

$$\vert G(j\omega) \vert = \frac{e^{-6}}{\sqrt{9 + \omega^2}}$$

The magnitude spectrum is a **low-pass characteristic** peaking at $\omega = 0$ with maximum value $e^{-6}/3$, and decaying to zero as $\vert \omega \vert \to \infty$ at a rate of $1/\omega$.

---

### Question B (Choice 2): `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** State and prove the convolution property of the CTFT. Explain its significance in LTI system analysis.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A signal $x(t) = \text{rect}\!\left(\dfrac{t}{\tau}\right)$ is passed through an LTI system with impulse response $h(t) = e^{-2t} u(t)$. Find the output $y(t)$ using the Fourier transform.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** $\text{rect}(t/\tau) = 1$ for $\vert t \vert < \tau/2$ and $0$ otherwise (rectangular pulse of width $\tau$ centered at origin). The known transform pair is $\text{rect}(t/\tau) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} \tau \, \text{sinc}(\omega \tau / 2)$ where $\text{sinc}(x) = \dfrac{\sin(\pi x)}{\pi x}$.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a):**

**Statement:** $x(t) * y(t) \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} X(j\omega) \cdot Y(j\omega)$.

**Proof:** Already shown exhaustively in Section 3.3.

**Significance in LTI System Analysis:**

For an LTI system with impulse response $h(t)$ and input $x(t)$, the output in the time domain is:
$$y(t) = x(t) * h(t)$$

The direct computation of this convolution integral is often very difficult. However, applying the Fourier transform:

$$Y(j\omega) = \mathcal{F}\{x(t) * h(t)\} = X(j\omega) \cdot H(j\omega)$$

The output spectrum is the **product** of the input spectrum and the system's frequency response $H(j\omega) = \mathcal{F}\{h(t)\}$. We can then take the inverse CTFT to recover $y(t)$. This converts a complex integral (convolution) into simple algebraic multiplication.

**Part (b):**

**Given:** $x(t) = \text{rect}(t/\tau)$ and $h(t) = e^{-2t} u(t)$.

**Step 1:** Find $X(j\omega)$.

Using the standard transform pair:

$$X(j\omega) = \tau \, \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{\omega \tau}{2}\right) = \tau \cdot \frac{\sin(\omega \tau / 2)}{\omega \tau / 2} = \frac{2\sin(\omega \tau / 2)}{\omega}$$

**Step 2:** Find $H(j\omega)$.

$$H(j\omega) = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-2t} e^{-j\omega t}\, dt = \frac{1}{2 + j\omega}$$

**Step 3:** Compute $Y(j\omega)$ using the convolution property.

$$Y(j\omega) = X(j\omega) \cdot H(j\omega) = \frac{2\sin(\omega \tau / 2)}{\omega (2 + j\omega)}$$

**Step 4:** Take the inverse Fourier transform.

The denominator has factors of $\omega$ and $(2 + j\omega)$. This corresponds to a time-domain operation that involves an **integration** of a decaying exponential. Using the integration and frequency shift properties:

$$y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{t} e^{-2\tau} u(\tau) \cdot \text{rect}\!\left(\frac{\tau}{\tau_0}\right) d\tau \text{ (evaluated in time domain)}$$

**Step 5:** Express the final answer in piecewise form.

$$\boxed{y(t) = \begin{cases} 0, & t < -\tau/2 \\ \frac{1}{2}\left(1 - e^{-2(t + \tau/2)}\right), & -\tau/2 \le t \le \tau/2 \\ \frac{1}{2}\left(e^{-2(t - \tau/2)} - e^{-2(t + \tau/2)}\right), & t > \tau/2 \end{cases}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign Error in Exponential:** When applying the time-shifting property, students often write $e^{+j\omega t_0}$ instead of $e^{-j\omega t_0}$. This costs 1-2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the $\frac{1}{2\pi}$ Factor:** In the **Multiplication Property**, the factor $\frac{1}{2\pi}$ must accompany the convolution in the frequency domain. Omitting it loses 2 marks.
> 3. **Conjugate Property for Real Signals:** For real $x(t)$, students must remember $X(-j\omega) = X^*(j\omega)$, which gives an even magnitude and odd phase. KTU examiners specifically test this.
> 4. **Integration Property DC Term:** When using $\int x(t)dt \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{F}} \frac{X(j\omega)}{j\omega} + \pi X(0)\delta(\omega)$, students frequently forget the impulse term $\pi X(0)\delta(\omega)$ that arises from the DC component.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Preparation:**

- **Existence:** A signal has a valid CTFT if it is absolutely integrable ($\int \vert x(t) \vert dt < \infty$) and has a finite number of discontinuities (Dirichlet conditions). Periodic signals and unit step have transforms only in the generalized (delta function) sense.

- **Notation:** Use $X(j\omega)$, **not** $X(\omega)$, as it reminds you that the spectrum lives on the imaginary axis of the $s$-plane.

- **Time Shifting** $x(t - t_0) \Rightarrow$ Multiply $X(j\omega)$ by $e^{-j\omega t_0}$. The magnitude is invariant; only the phase is altered linearly.

- **Frequency Shifting** $e^{j\omega_0 t} x(t) \Rightarrow$ Shift $X(j\omega)$ to $X(j(\omega - \omega_0))$. This is the **Modulation Theorem** and is the basis of all radio communication.

- **Differentiation in Time** $\frac{dx}{dt} \Rightarrow (j\omega) X(j\omega)$. Higher orders: $(j\omega)^n X(j\omega)$.

- **Convolution in Time** $\Rightarrow$ **Multiplication in Frequency** $X(j\omega) \cdot Y(j\omega)$. This is the **most important** property for LTI system analysis.

- **Multiplication in Time** $\Rightarrow$ **Convolution in Frequency** scaled by $\frac{1}{2\pi}$. Critical for the Sampling Theorem.

- **Parseval's Theorem** states that signal energy is preserved: $E = \int \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int \vert X(j\omega) \vert^2 d\omega$.

- **Duality** allows you to compute the inverse transform of a function $X(j\omega)$ by finding the forward transform of $X(jt)$ and then substituting $t \to -\omega$ and multiplying by $2\pi$.

- **Conjugate Symmetry** for real signals: $X(-j\omega) = X^*(j\omega)$. This implies $\vert X(j\omega) \vert$ is even and $\angle X(j\omega)$ is odd.

- **Standard Pairs to Memorize:** $\delta(t) \leftrightarrow 1$, $1 \leftrightarrow 2\pi\delta(\omega)$, $e^{-at}u(t) \leftrightarrow \frac{1}{a+j\omega}$, $\text{rect}(t/\tau) \leftrightarrow \tau\text{sinc}(\omega\tau/2)$.

- **The Convolution Property is King:** Every KTU problem involving an LTI system will eventually require this property. Practice it until it is second nature.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Concept of frequency response
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT522 - INTRODUCTION TO SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Concept of frequency response

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

The **Frequency Response** of a Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) system is the measure of the system's steady-state output magnitude and phase as a function of the input frequency, when the input is a complex sinusoid (or equivalently, a complex exponential) of that frequency.

Mathematically, for a continuous-time LTI system with impulse response $h(t)$, the frequency response is given by the **Continuous-Time Fourier Transform (CTFT)** of $h(t)$:

$$H(j\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} h(t)\,e^{-j\omega t}\,dt$$

For a discrete-time LTI system with impulse response $h[n]$, the frequency response is given by the **Discrete-Time Fourier Transform (DTFT)** of $h[n]$:

$$H(e^{j\Omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} h[n]\,e^{-j\Omega n}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (Board Examiner's Favourite Line):**
> The frequency response completely characterizes an LTI system in the frequency domain. It tells the system engineer *how much* the system amplifies/attenuates each frequency component, and *by how much* it delays (phase-shifts) that component.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Audio Equalizer" of an LTI System

Imagine you are a sound engineer at a concert. You have an **audio equalizer** in front of you, with sliders for different frequency bands (bass, mid, treble). When a song plays:

- If you push **up the bass slider (e.g., 100 Hz)**, the bass becomes louder → the system **amplifies** low frequencies.
- If you push **down the treble slider (e.g., 10 kHz)**, the high notes become softer → the system **attenuates** high frequencies.
- Some sliders might also slightly **delay** (shift in time) certain frequencies, which is the **phase shift**.

An LTI system behaves **exactly like this equalizer**. The frequency response $H(j\omega)$ is the "curve" that tells you, for every frequency $\omega$, how much the slider is up (magnitude) and how much it is shifted (phase).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Example:** A low-pass filter (like the bass blocker in a subwoofer) lets low frequencies pass and blocks high frequencies. Its $|H(j\omega)|$ is **1 (unity)** at low $\omega$ and **0** at high $\omega$. This is a textbook case of frequency-selective behavior.

### 1.3 Key Physical Metrics

- **Magnitude Response:** $\vert H(j\omega) \vert$ — the gain (amplification factor) at frequency $\omega$. Unitless.
- **Phase Response:** $\angle H(j\omega)$ — the phase shift (in **radians** or **degrees**) introduced at frequency $\omega$.
- **Bandwidth:** The range of frequencies over which $\vert H(j\omega) \vert \geq \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$ of its peak value (the **-3 dB** point). Measured in **rad/s** (CT) or **rad/sample** (DT).
- **Resonant Frequency ($\omega_0$):** The frequency at which $\vert H(j\omega) \vert$ reaches its maximum. Measured in **rad/s** (CT).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why" Behind Frequency Response — Eigenfunction Property

The deepest reason frequency response works is the **Eigenfunction Property** of LTI systems:

- A complex exponential $e^{j\omega_0 t}$ is an **eigenfunction** of any LTI system.
- When fed into an LTI system, the output is **the same complex exponential**, scaled by a complex constant (the eigenvalue).

For input $x(t) = e^{j\omega_0 t}$:

$$y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} h(\tau)\,x(t-\tau)\,d\tau = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} h(\tau)\,e^{j\omega_0(t-\tau)}\,d\tau$$

$$y(t) = e^{j\omega_0 t}\,\underbrace{\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} h(\tau)\,e^{-j\omega_0 \tau}\,d\tau}_{H(j\omega_0)}$$

$$y(t) = H(j\omega_0)\cdot e^{j\omega_0 t}$$

> [!TIP]
> The complex constant $H(j\omega_0)$ is exactly the frequency response evaluated at $\omega = \omega_0$. This is the most elegant single derivation in Signals & Systems — **memorize it for KTU exams**.

### 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step Concept Breakdown

1. **Decompose** the input signal $x(t)$ into a sum (integral) of complex exponentials via Fourier Transform: $X(j\omega) = \mathcal{F}\{x(t)\}$.
2. **Multiply** each frequency component by the system's frequency response: $Y(j\omega) = H(j\omega)\cdot X(j\omega)$.
3. **Reconstruct** the output via Inverse Fourier Transform: $y(t) = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\{Y(j\omega)\}$.

> This is the **Input-Output Relationship in Frequency Domain**: $Y(j\omega) = H(j\omega) X(j\omega)$.

### 2.3 Sinusoidal Steady-State Response (The "Examiner's Favourite" Formula)

If the input is a pure sinusoid $x(t) = A\cos(\omega_0 t + \phi)$, the steady-state output is:

$$y_{ss}(t) = A\,\vert H(j\omega_0) \vert\,\cos\!\big(\omega_0 t + \phi + \angle H(j\omega_0)\big)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The output sinusoid has the **same frequency** $\omega_0$ as the input. Only the **amplitude** is scaled by $\vert H(j\omega_0) \vert$ and the **phase** is shifted by $\angle H(j\omega_0)$.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Concept** | **Continuous-Time (CT)** | **Discrete-Time (DT)** |
|---|---|---|
| Frequency Response | $H(j\omega) = \displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} h(t)e^{-j\omega t}dt$ | $H(e^{j\Omega}) = \displaystyle\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} h[n]e^{-j\Omega n}$ |
| Magnitude Response | $\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \sqrt{\{\text{Re}\}^2 + \{\text{Im}\}^2}$ | $\vert H(e^{j\Omega}) \vert = \sqrt{\{\text{Re}\}^2 + \{\text{Im}\}^2}$ |
| Phase Response | $\angle H(j\omega) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\text{Im}}{\text{Re}}\right)$ | $\angle H(e^{j\Omega}) = \tan^{-1}\!\left(\dfrac{\text{Im}}{\text{Re}}\right)$ |
| Periodicity of $H$ | Not periodic in $\omega$ (most systems) | **Always periodic** with period $2\pi$ in $\Omega$ |
| Frequency Domain I/O | $Y(j\omega) = H(j\omega)\,X(j\omega)$ | $Y(e^{j\Omega}) = H(e^{j\Omega})\,X(e^{j\Omega})$ |
| Sinusoidal Output (CT) | $A\cos(\omega_0 t + \phi) \;\rightarrow\; A\vert H(j\omega_0)\vert\cos(\omega_0 t + \phi + \angle H)$ | $A\cos(\Omega_0 n + \phi) \;\rightarrow\; A\vert H(e^{j\Omega_0})\vert\cos(\Omega_0 n + \phi + \angle H)$ |
| Group Delay | $\tau_g(\omega) = -\dfrac{d}{d\omega}\angle H(j\omega)$ | $\tau_g(\Omega) = -\dfrac{d}{d\Omega}\angle H(e^{j\Omega})$ |
| Bode Magnitude | $20\log_{10}\vert H(j\omega) \vert$ in **decibels (dB)** | $20\log_{10}\vert H(e^{j\Omega}) \vert$ in **dB** |
| Units of Frequency | **rad/s** (or Hz) | **rad/sample** (or cycles/sample) |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Audio Engineering:** Graphic equalizers and bass/treble knobs are direct graphical controls of $\vert H(j\omega) \vert$.
- **Telecommunications:** Bandpass filters select specific channels (e.g., FM radio stations at 88–108 MHz).
- **Control Systems:** Bode plots of $H(j\omega)$ determine **stability margins** (gain & phase margins).
- **Biomedical:** ECG/EEG machines use frequency-selective filters to remove 50/60 Hz powerline noise.
- **Image Processing:** 2D frequency response determines blur, sharpening, and edge detection.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Frequency Response from Convolution Theorem

We start from the time-domain input-output relationship of an LTI system:

$$y(t) = x(t) * h(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(\tau)\,h(t-\tau)\,d\tau$$

**Step 1 — Take the Fourier Transform of both sides:**

$$Y(j\omega) = \mathcal{F}\{x(t) * h(t)\}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Convolution Theorem (a property of the Fourier Transform):**

The Fourier Transform of a convolution of two signals is the product of their individual Fourier Transforms:

$$\mathcal{F}\{x(t) * h(t)\} = X(j\omega) \cdot H(j\omega)$$

**Step 3 — Therefore:**

$$Y(j\omega) = X(j\omega)\cdot H(j\omega)$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the frequency response:**

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{Y(j\omega)}{X(j\omega)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This equation is the **frequency-domain definition** of an LTI system. It says: *The frequency response is the ratio of the output's Fourier Transform to the input's Fourier Transform.* This is the formula you will use in 80% of KTU problems.

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Response to a Sinusoidal Input (Sinusoidal Steady-State)

Let the input be $x(t) = A\cos(\omega_0 t + \phi)$. By Euler's formula, this can be expressed as a sum of two complex exponentials:

$$x(t) = \frac{A}{2}\left[e^{j(\omega_0 t + \phi)} + e^{-j(\omega_0 t + \phi)}\right] = \frac{A}{2}e^{j\phi}e^{j\omega_0 t} + \frac{A}{2}e^{-j\phi}e^{-j\omega_0 t}$$

**Step 1 — Apply the system to the first exponential:** Using the eigenfunction property, $e^{j\omega_0 t} \rightarrow H(j\omega_0)e^{j\omega_0 t}$. So:

$$\frac{A}{2}e^{j\phi}e^{j\omega_0 t} \;\rightarrow\; \frac{A}{2}e^{j\phi}H(j\omega_0)e^{j\omega_0 t}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the system to the second exponential:** For $e^{-j\omega_0 t}$, the system scales by $H(-j\omega_0)$:

$$\frac{A}{2}e^{-j\phi}e^{-j\omega_0 t} \;\rightarrow\; \frac{A}{2}e^{-j\phi}H(-j\omega_0)e^{-j\omega_0 t}$$

**Step 3 — Write the total output:**

$$y(t) = \frac{A}{2}H(j\omega_0)e^{j\phi}e^{j\omega_0 t} + \frac{A}{2}H(-j\omega_0)e^{-j\phi}e^{-j\omega_0 t}$$

**Step 4 — For real $h(t)$ (real systems), conjugate symmetry holds:** $H(-j\omega_0) = H^*(j\omega_0)$. Let $H(j\omega_0) = \vert H(j\omega_0) \vert e^{j\angle H(j\omega_0)}$.

$$y(t) = \frac{A}{2}\vert H(j\omega_0) \vert\left[e^{j(\omega_0 t + \phi + \angle H)} + e^{-j(\omega_0 t + \phi + \angle H)}\right]$$

**Step 5 — Apply Euler's identity back:**

$$\boxed{y_{ss}(t) = A\,\vert H(j\omega_0) \vert\,\cos\!\big(\omega_0 t + \phi + \angle H(j\omega_0)\big)}$$

### 3.3 Worked Example 1: RC Low-Pass Filter

An RC low-pass filter has impulse response $h(t) = \frac{1}{RC}e^{-t/RC}u(t)$. Find its frequency response.

**Step 1 — Apply the CTFT definition:**

$$H(j\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \frac{1}{RC}e^{-t/RC}u(t)\,e^{-j\omega t}\,dt$$

**Step 2 — Since $u(t) = 0$ for $t < 0$, the lower limit becomes 0:**

$$H(j\omega) = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{RC}e^{-t/RC}e^{-j\omega t}\,dt = \frac{1}{RC}\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-(1/RC + j\omega)t}\,dt$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integral (standard exponential):**

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{1}{RC}\left[\frac{e^{-(1/RC + j\omega)t}}{-(1/RC + j\omega)}\right]_0^{\infty}$$

**Step 4 — Apply limits:** At $t \to \infty$, $e^{-\infty} = 0$. At $t = 0$, $e^{0} = 1$:

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{1}{RC}\left[0 - \frac{1}{-(1/RC + j\omega)}\right] = \frac{1}{RC}\cdot\frac{1}{(1/RC + j\omega)}$$

**Step 5 — Simplify:**

$$H(j\omega) = \frac{1}{1 + j\omega RC}$$

**Step 6 — Magnitude and Phase (KTU's most-asked follow-up):**

$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + (\omega RC)^2}}, \quad \angle H(j\omega) = -\tan^{-1}(\omega RC)$$

At $\omega = 0$ (DC): $\vert H \vert = 1$ (passes through). At $\omega = \frac{1}{RC}$ (cutoff): $\vert H \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}$. At $\omega \to \infty$: $\vert H \vert \to 0$ (blocks high frequencies). This confirms the **low-pass** nature.

### 3.4 Worked Example 2: Discrete-Time Moving Average Filter

A 3-point moving average filter has $h[n] = \frac{1}{3}\{\delta[n] + \delta[n-1] + \delta[n-2]\}$. Find its frequency response.

**Step 1 — Apply the DTFT definition:**

$$H(e^{j\Omega}) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} h[n]\,e^{-j\Omega n} = \frac{1}{3}\left[e^{-j0} + e^{-j\Omega} + e^{-j2\Omega}\right]$$

**Step 2 — Factor the geometric series:**

$$H(e^{j\Omega}) = \frac{1}{3}e^{-j\Omega}\left[e^{j\Omega} + 1 + e^{-j\Omega}\right] = \frac{1}{3}e^{-j\Omega}\left[1 + 2\cos(\Omega)\right]$$

**Step 3 — Magnitude and Phase:**

$$\vert H(e^{j\Omega}) \vert = \frac{1}{3}\vert 1 + 2\cos(\Omega) \vert, \quad \angle H(e^{j\Omega}) = -\Omega \quad (\text{when } 1 + 2\cos\Omega > 0)$$

**Step 4 — Verification at specific frequencies:**

- At $\Omega = 0$: $\vert H \vert = \frac{1}{3}(1+2) = 1$ → DC passes through fully. ✓
- At $\Omega = \pi$: $\vert H \vert = \frac{1}{3}\vert 1 - 2 \vert = \frac{1}{3}$ → High frequencies attenuated. ✓
- At $\Omega = \frac{2\pi}{3}$: $1 + 2\cos(2\pi/3) = 1 - 1 = 0$ → **Complete null** (filter blocks this frequency).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define the term "frequency response" of an LTI system. Why is it important in signal processing?**

**Model Answer:**
The frequency response of an LTI system is defined as the Fourier Transform of its impulse response $h(t)$ (or $h[n]$). For a continuous-time system: $H(j\omega) = \displaystyle\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} h(t)\,e^{-j\omega t}\,dt$. It is important because it completely characterizes how the system modifies the amplitude and phase of each sinusoidal component of the input. It is a single complex-valued function $H(j\omega) = \vert H(j\omega) \vert e^{j\angle H(j\omega)}$ that contains both the magnitude response (gain at each frequency) and the phase response (phase shift at each frequency). It is fundamental to filter design, communication systems, and control systems.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**State and explain the eigenfunction property of LTI systems.**

**Model Answer:**
The eigenfunction property states that complex exponentials $e^{j\omega_0 t}$ (CT) or $e^{j\Omega_0 n}$ (DT) are eigenfunctions of any LTI system. When a complex exponential is input to an LTI system, the output is the same complex exponential, multiplied by a complex constant — the **eigenvalue**, which is the frequency response evaluated at that frequency. Formally: $e^{j\omega_0 t} \xrightarrow{\text{LTI}} H(j\omega_0)\cdot e^{j\omega_0 t}$. This property is the reason why the Fourier Transform is the natural tool for analyzing LTI systems, and it is the foundation of frequency-domain analysis.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Module End Examination pattern: Two questions of 14 marks each are given, and the student answers **one** (internal choice). Each question typically has sub-parts (a) and (b) of 7 marks each.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper - 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** An LTI system has impulse response $h(t) = e^{-3t}u(t)$. Determine the frequency response $H(j\omega)$, magnitude response $\vert H(j\omega) \vert$, and phase response $\angle H(j\omega)$. **(7 marks)**

**Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Apply the CTFT definition:**

$$H(j\omega) = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-3t}\,e^{-j\omega t}\,dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-(3+j\omega)t}\,dt$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the integral:**

$$H(j\omega) = \left[\frac{e^{-(3+j\omega)t}}{-(3+j\omega)}\right]_0^{\infty} = \frac{1}{3+j\omega}$$

**Step 3 — Magnitude Response:**

$$\vert H(j\omega) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3^2 + \omega^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{9 + \omega^2}}$$

**Step 4 — Phase Response:**

$$\angle H(j\omega) = -\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{\omega}{3}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Key Observations:**
- At $\omega = 0$: $\vert H \vert = \frac{1}{3}$, $\angle H = 0^\circ$ → DC gain is $1/3$.
- At $\omega = 3$: $\vert H \vert = \frac{1}{3\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.236$, $\angle H = -45^\circ$.
- At $\omega \to \infty$: $\vert H \vert \to 0$ → high frequencies are blocked.
- This system is a **low-pass filter** with cutoff frequency $\omega_c = 3$ rad/s.

---

**(b)** For the system in part (a), determine the steady-state output $y_{ss}(t)$ when the input is $x(t) = 6\cos(4t + \pi/3)$. **(7 marks)**

**Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Evaluate magnitude and phase at $\omega_0 = 4$ rad/s:**

$$\vert H(j4) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{9 + 16}} = \frac{1}{5} = 0.2$$

$$\angle H(j4) = -\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{4}{3}\right) = -53.13^\circ = -0.9273 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the sinusoidal steady-state formula:**

$$y_{ss}(t) = A\,\vert H(j\omega_0) \vert\,\cos(\omega_0 t + \phi + \angle H(j\omega_0))$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $A = 6$, $\omega_0 = 4$, $\phi = \pi/3$:**

$$y_{ss}(t) = 6 \times 0.2 \times \cos\!\left(4t + \frac{\pi}{3} - 0.9273\right)$$

$$y_{ss}(t) = 1.2\,\cos\!\left(4t + 1.0472 - 0.9273\right) = 1.2\,\cos(4t + 0.1199) \text{ rad}$$

**Step 4 — Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{y_{ss}(t) = 1.2\,\cos(4t + 0.12) \text{ rad} \quad \text{or} \quad 1.2\,\cos(4t + 6.87^\circ)}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper - 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** A discrete-time LTI system has impulse response $h[n] = (0.5)^n u[n]$. Find the frequency response $H(e^{j\Omega})$, its magnitude, and its phase. **(7 marks)**

**Solution (a):**

**Step 1 — Apply the DTFT definition:**

$$H(e^{j\Omega}) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (0.5)^n e^{-j\Omega n} = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} (0.5\,e^{-j\Omega})^n$$

**Step 2 — Recognize the geometric series (valid since $\vert 0.5e^{-j\Omega}\vert = 0.5 < 1$):**

$$H(e^{j\Omega}) = \frac{1}{1 - 0.5\,e^{-j\Omega}}$$

**Step 3 — Rationalize the denominator by multiplying numerator and denominator by the complex conjugate $(1 - 0.5\,e^{j\Omega})$:**

$$H(e^{j\Omega}) = \frac{1 - 0.5\,e^{j\Omega}}{(1 - 0.5\,e^{-j\Omega})(1 - 0.5\,e^{j\Omega})}$$

**Step 4 — Expand the denominator:**

$$(1 - 0.5\cos\Omega - j0.5\sin\Omega)(1 - 0.5\cos\Omega + j0.5\sin\Omega) = (1 - 0.5\cos\Omega)^2 + (0.5\sin\Omega)^2$$

$$= 1 - \cos\Omega + 0.25\cos^2\Omega + 0.25\sin^2\Omega = 1.25 - \cos\Omega$$

**Step 5 — Final Cartesian form (numerator expanded):**

$$H(e^{j\Omega}) = \frac{1 - 0.5\cos\Omega - j\,0.5\sin\Omega}{1.25 - \cos\Omega}$$

**Step 6 — Magnitude Response:**

$$\vert H(e^{j\Omega}) \vert = \frac{\sqrt{(1 - 0.5\cos\Omega)^2 + (0.5\sin\Omega)^2}}{1.25 - \cos\Omega}$$

**Step 7 — Phase Response:**

$$\angle H(e^{j\Omega}) = -\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.5\sin\Omega}{1 - 0.5\cos\Omega}\right)$$

---

**(b)** For the system in part (a), determine the output $y[n]$ when the input is $x[n] = 4\cos(\pi n/2 + \pi/4)$. **(7 marks)**

**Solution (b):**

**Step 1 — Evaluate $H(e^{j\Omega})$ at $\Omega_0 = \pi/2$:**

$$H(e^{j\pi/2}) = \frac{1}{1 - 0.5\,e^{-j\pi/2}} = \frac{1}{1 - 0.5(-j)} = \frac{1}{1 + 0.5j}$$

**Step 2 — Magnitude and Phase of $H(e^{j\pi/2})$:**

$$\vert H(e^{j\pi/2}) \vert = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1^2 + 0.5^2}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1.25}} = \frac{1}{1.118} \approx 0.8944$$

$$\angle H(e^{j\pi/2}) = -\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{0.5}{1}\right) = -26.57^\circ = -0.4636 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the discrete-time sinusoidal steady-state formula:**

$$y_{ss}[n] = A\,\vert H(e^{j\Omega_0}) \vert\,\cos(\Omega_0 n + \phi + \angle H(e^{j\Omega_0}))$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $A = 4$, $\Omega_0 = \pi/2$, $\phi = \pi/4$:**

$$y_{ss}[n] = 4 \times 0.8944 \times \cos\!\left(\frac{\pi n}{2} + \frac{\pi}{4} - 0.4636\right)$$

$$y_{ss}[n] = 3.578 \times \cos\!\left(\frac{\pi n}{2} + 0.7854 - 0.4636\right) = 3.578\,\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi n}{2} + 0.3218\right) \text{ rad}$$

**Step 5 — Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{y_{ss}[n] = 3.578\,\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi n}{2} + 0.3218\right) \text{ rad} \quad \text{or} \quad 3.578\,\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi n}{2} + 18.43^\circ\right)}$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting the lower limit:** When taking the CTFT of $h(t) = e^{-at}u(t)$, students often write $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}$ but should explicitly change the lower limit to **0** because of $u(t)$. *(-1 mark)*
> 2. **Mixing up $\omega$ and $f$:** The Fourier Transform uses angular frequency $\omega$ in **rad/s**, not $f$ in Hz. The two are related by $\omega = 2\pi f$. *(-1 mark)*
> 3. **Sign error in phase:** If $H(j\omega) = \frac{1}{a + j\omega}$, the phase is $-\tan^{-1}(\omega/a)$, NOT $+\tan^{-1}(\omega/a)$. The negative sign is critical. *(-1 mark)*
> 4. **Forgetting to express the final answer in time-domain form:** In sinusoidal problems, students compute $H(j\omega_0)$ correctly but forget to write the actual $y(t)$ using the formula $A\vert H\vert\cos(\omega_0 t + \phi + \angle H)$. *(-2 marks)*
> 5. **Not stating that the output is a *steady-state* response:** The sinusoidal response formula gives the steady-state output, assuming the transient has died out. State this explicitly. *(-1 mark)*

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Frequency Response Definition:** Fourier Transform of impulse response. CT: $H(j\omega) = \int h(t)e^{-j\omega t}dt$. DT: $H(e^{j\Omega}) = \sum h[n]e^{-j\Omega n}$.
- **Two Faces of $H$:** Magnitude $\vert H \vert$ (gain) and Phase $\angle H$ (delay) at every frequency.
- **Eigenfunction Property:** Complex exponentials pass through LTI systems unchanged in shape, only scaled by $H(j\omega_0)$.
- **Input-Output Equation (Frequency Domain):** $Y(j\omega) = H(j\omega) X(j\omega)$.
- **Sinusoidal Steady-State Output:** $A\cos(\omega_0 t + \phi) \rightarrow A\vert H(j\omega_0)\vert\cos(\omega_0 t + \phi + \angle H(j\omega_0))$.
- **Frequency Selective Behaviour:** A system's $\vert H \vert$ curve defines whether it is a low-pass, high-pass, bandpass, or bandstop filter.
- **Bode Plot Convention:** Magnitude is plotted in **decibels (dB)** = $20\log_{10}\vert H \vert$, frequency on a **log scale**.
- **DT Frequency Response is Always Periodic** with period $2\pi$ in $\Omega$ — a unique property of discrete-time systems.
- **Group Delay:** $\tau_g(\omega) = -\frac{d}{d\omega}\angle H(j\omega)$ — the time delay experienced by each frequency. Linear phase = constant group delay = no distortion.
- **Practical Filter Types:** Low-pass, High-pass, Bandpass, Bandstop, All-pass.
- **Common Exam Formats:** (1) Given $h(t)$, find $H(j\omega)$, $\vert H \vert$, $\angle H$. (2) Given input sinusoid and $H(j\omega_0)$, find steady-state output. (3) Sketch Bode plot from $H(j\omega)$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Significance of Fourier transform and difference from Fourier series; energy and power spectral density
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT522 - INTRODUCTION TO SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Significance of Fourier transform and difference from Fourier series; energy and power spectral density

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Fourier Transform — Formal Definition

The **Fourier Transform (FT)** is a mathematical transformation that decomposes a continuous-time, non-periodic signal $x(t)$ into its constituent frequency components. It extends the idea of the Fourier Series to aperiodic signals by treating the fundamental period $T_0 \to \infty$, which causes the discrete harmonic spectrum to collapse into a **continuous frequency spectrum**.

**Forward Fourier Transform:**

$$X(f) = \mathcal{F}\{x(t)\} = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t)\, e^{-j2\pi f t}\, dt$$

**Inverse Fourier Transform:**

$$x(t) = \mathcal{F}^{-1}\{X(f)\} = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} X(f)\, e^{+j2\pi f t}\, df$$

Here, $X(f)$ is called the **spectrum** of the signal. It is generally a **complex-valued function** whose magnitude $\vert X(f) \vert$ gives the amplitude density and whose phase $\angle X(f)$ gives the phase spectrum.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Fourier Transform is defined only for signals that satisfy the **Dirichlet Conditions**:
> 1. The signal $x(t)$ must be absolutely integrable: $\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert x(t) \vert\, dt < \infty$.
> 2. $x(t)$ must have a finite number of maxima, minima, and discontinuities in any finite interval.
> 
> The Fourier Transform may exist even when the first condition is relaxed slightly (e.g., for periodic signals using impulses), but these are the sufficient conditions for guaranteed existence.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Prism of Sound

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — White Light Through a Prism:**
> 
> Imagine you hear a single guitar string being plucked. The sound you perceive is a **mixture** of a fundamental note and several overtones (harmonics). Your ear cannot directly "see" these individual tones — they are blended together in time.
> 
> The **Fourier Transform acts like a mathematical prism** that takes this blended sound wave $x(t)$ in the time domain and **splits it into a rainbow of pure frequency components** $X(f)$. Each color in the rainbow corresponds to one pure sine wave of a specific frequency.
> 
> - **Fourier Series** = Prism used on **repeating musical loops** (gives you a discrete set of allowed notes).
> - **Fourier Transform** = Prism used on **one-shot sounds like a clap or thunder** (gives you a continuous spread of frequencies).
> 
> The significance is this: once we know $X(f)$, we know *exactly* how the signal's energy or power is distributed across frequencies — and this tells engineers how to design filters, communication channels, and audio equipment.

---

## 1.3 Energy Spectral Density (ESD) and Power Spectral Density (PSD) — Formal Definition

### Energy Spectral Density $\psi_x(f)$

For an **energy signal** (finite total energy, zero average power), the Energy Spectral Density is defined as the **squared magnitude of the Fourier Transform**:

$$\psi_x(f) = \vert X(f) \vert^2 \quad \text{(units: Joules per Hertz, J/Hz)}$$

### Power Spectral Density $S_x(f)$

For a **power signal** (infinite total energy, finite non-zero average power), the Power Spectral Density is defined via the **autocorrelation function** $R_x(\tau)$ through the **Wiener–Khinchin Theorem**:

$$S_x(f) = \mathcal{F}\{R_x(\tau)\} = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} R_x(\tau)\, e^{-j2\pi f \tau}\, d\tau$$

with units of **Watts per Hertz (W/Hz)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Exam Keyword:** The PSD is a *real, even, and non-negative* function of frequency. Always remember: $S_x(f) \geq 0$ for all $f$, and $S_x(-f) = S_x(f)$.

---

## 1.4 Intuitive Interpretation

| Concept | Time Domain | Frequency Domain Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| **ESD** $\psi_x(f)$ | $E = \int \vert x(t) \vert^2\, dt$ | Energy per unit bandwidth |
| **PSD** $S_x(f)$ | $P = \lim_{T\to\infty} \frac{1}{T}\int_{-T/2}^{T/2}\vert x(t)\vert^2\, dt$ | Power per unit bandwidth |

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Intuition:** If you tune a radio to frequency $f_0$ with a narrow bandwidth $\Delta f$, then $S_x(f_0) \cdot \Delta f \approx$ the signal power your radio actually captures. The PSD literally tells you "how much signal power lives in each frequency band."

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Significance of the Fourier Transform

The Fourier Transform is significant for **four principal engineering reasons**:

1. **Spectral Analysis:** It reveals the frequency content of aperiodic signals, which Fourier Series cannot handle. This is essential for analyzing transient signals like radar pulses, biomedical spikes, or speech bursts.

2. **Linear System Analysis:** When a Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) system with impulse response $h(t)$ receives an input $x(t)$, the output spectrum is simply:
   $$Y(f) = H(f) \cdot X(f)$$
   where $H(f) = \mathcal{F}\{h(t)\}$ is the **transfer function**. This converts convolution in time into multiplication in frequency, dramatically simplifying analysis.

3. **Filter Design:** Communication systems (low-pass, high-pass, band-pass filters) are designed entirely in the frequency domain by specifying $H(f)$.

4. **Sampling & Reconstruction:** The FT forms the theoretical basis of the **Sampling Theorem** (Nyquist criterion): a bandlimited signal $x(t)$ with maximum frequency $f_m$ can be perfectly reconstructed from its samples if sampled at $f_s > 2f_m$.

---

## 2.2 Fourier Series vs. Fourier Transform — Critical Differences

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is a guaranteed KTU 14-mark question area. Master the comparison table below.**

| Parameter | **Fourier Series (FS)** | **Fourier Transform (FT)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Signal type** | Periodic, continuous-time | Aperiodic, continuous-time |
| **Spectrum type** | Discrete spectrum (harmonics at $f_0, 2f_0, 3f_0, \dots$) | Continuous spectrum over all $f \in (-\infty, \infty)$ |
| **Fundamental relation** | Period $T_0$ is finite, $f_0 = 1/T_0$ | Period $T_0 \to \infty$, so $f_0 \to 0$ (spacing vanishes) |
| **Mathematical form** | Summation: $x(t) = \sum a_k e^{jk\omega_0 t}$ | Integral: $x(t) = \int X(f) e^{j2\pi f t} df$ |
| **Coefficient** | $a_k = \frac{1}{T_0}\int_{T_0} x(t) e^{-jk\omega_0 t} dt$ | $X(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-j2\pi f t} dt$ |
| **Magnitude** | $a_k$ has units of signal amplitude | $X(f)$ has units of amplitude·seconds (amplitude density) |
| **Convergence** | Converges to $x(t)$ at points of continuity, average at jumps | Converges where Dirichlet conditions hold |
| **Existence conditions** | Periodic & finite power over one period | Absolutely integrable (Dirichlet conditions) |
| **Domain** | Frequency variable is discrete index $k$ | Frequency variable is continuous $f$ |

**The Big Idea:** As $T_0 \to \infty$ in the FS representation, the spacing between harmonics $\Delta f = 1/T_0 \to 0$, and the discrete coefficients $a_k \cdot T_0$ become a continuous function $X(f)$. This is the **limiting relationship**:

$$X(f) = \lim_{T_0 \to \infty}\left[ a_k \cdot T_0 \right]_{f = k f_0}$$

---

## 2.3 Energy Spectral Density (ESD) — Detailed Properties

For an energy signal $x(t)$ with FT $X(f)$:

$$\psi_x(f) = \vert X(f) \vert^2 = X(f) \cdot X^*(f)$$

**Properties of ESD:**

- $\psi_x(f)$ is **real and non-negative**.
- $\psi_x(f)$ is an **even function**: $\psi_x(-f) = \psi_x(f)$.
- The **total energy** of the signal is obtained by integrating the ESD over all frequencies (**Rayleigh's / Parseval's Theorem**):

$$E = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2\, dt = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert X(f) \vert^2\, df = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \psi_x(f)\, df$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the FT formula have $\psi_x(f)$ and not $S_x(f)$ for energy signals?** Because for energy signals the FT $X(f)$ itself exists, and Parseval's theorem directly connects the time-domain energy to the squared magnitude of the FT. For power signals the FT may not converge (e.g., sinusoids), so we use the autocorrelation route via Wiener–Khinchin.

---

## 2.4 Power Spectral Density (PSD) — Detailed Properties

For a power signal $x(t)$, the **autocorrelation function** is:

$$R_x(\tau) = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T} \int_{-T/2}^{+T/2} x(t)\, x(t+\tau)\, dt$$

By the **Wiener–Khinchin Theorem**, the PSD is the FT of $R_x(\tau)$:

$$S_x(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} R_x(\tau)\, e^{-j2\pi f \tau}\, d\tau$$

**Properties of PSD:**

- $S_x(f)$ is **real, even, and non-negative**: $S_x(f) \geq 0$, $S_x(-f) = S_x(f)$.
- The **total average power** is the area under the PSD curve:

$$P = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} S_x(f)\, df$$

- For a deterministic periodic signal with FS coefficients $a_k$, the PSD is a **line spectrum (impulse train)**:

$$S_x(f) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert a_k \vert^2\, \delta(f - k f_0)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** For a sinusoid $x(t) = A\cos(2\pi f_0 t)$, the PSD is $S_x(f) = \frac{A^2}{4}\left[\delta(f - f_0) + \delta(f + f_0)\right]$. The total power integrates to $A^2/2$ as expected.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this table — it covers 80% of numerical problems in Module 2.**

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $X(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-j2\pi f t} dt$ | Forward Fourier Transform |
| 2 | $x(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} X(f) e^{j2\pi f t} df$ | Inverse Fourier Transform |
| 3 | $\psi_x(f) = \vert X(f) \vert^2$ | Energy Spectral Density |
| 4 | $E = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \psi_x(f) df$ | Parseval's Theorem (Energy) |
| 5 | $R_x(\tau) = \lim_{T\to\infty} \frac{1}{T} \int_{-T/2}^{T/2} x(t) x(t+\tau) dt$ | Autocorrelation |
| 6 | $S_x(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} R_x(\tau) e^{-j2\pi f \tau} d\tau$ | Wiener–Khinchin (PSD) |
| 7 | $P = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} S_x(f) df$ | Total Average Power |
| 8 | $S_x(f) = \sum_k \vert a_k \vert^2 \delta(f - kf_0)$ | PSD of a periodic signal |
| 9 | $E \cdot B = \int \vert X(f) \vert^2 df$ | Energy–Bandwidth relation |
| 10 | $Y(f) = H(f)\, X(f)$ | LTI system in frequency domain |
| 11 | $\mathcal{F}\{\delta(t)\} = 1$ | FT of unit impulse |
| 12 | $\mathcal{F}\{1\} = \delta(f)$ | FT of DC (used in PSD of sinusoids) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** PSD is the workhorse of **wireless communication link budgets** (it determines how much transmitter power falls in a receiver's bandwidth), **radar** (matched filtering), and **noise analysis** (thermal noise PSD is $N_0/2$, the **two-sided** white noise floor). ESD is fundamental in **quantum mechanics**, **optical pulses**, and **short-duration transient analysis**.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Fourier Series to Fourier Transform

We start with the **complex exponential Fourier Series** of a periodic signal $x(t)$ of period $T_0$:

$$x(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} a_k\, e^{jk\omega_0 t}, \qquad \omega_0 = \frac{2\pi}{T_0}$$

$$a_k = \frac{1}{T_0} \int_{-T_0/2}^{+T_0/2} x(t)\, e^{-jk\omega_0 t}\, dt$$

**Step 1 — Identify the relationship between $a_k$ and the FT.**

Substitute the expression for $a_k$ back into $x(t)$:

$$x(t) = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} \left[\frac{1}{T_0} \int_{-T_0/2}^{+T_0/2} x(\tau)\, e^{-jk\omega_0 \tau}\, d\tau\right] e^{jk\omega_0 t}$$

Here we have replaced the dummy integration variable $t$ with $\tau$ inside the inner integral to avoid confusion with the outer summation variable.

**Step 2 — Group the exponential terms.**

$$x(t) = \frac{1}{T_0} \int_{-T_0/2}^{+T_0/2} x(\tau) \left[ \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} e^{jk\omega_0 (t - \tau)} \right] d\tau$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the impulse train.**

Using the Fourier Series representation of an impulse train:

$$\sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} e^{jk\omega_0 (t - \tau)} = T_0 \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} \delta(t - \tau - k T_0)$$

So $x(t)$ becomes:

$$x(t) = \int_{-T_0/2}^{+T_0/2} x(\tau) \left[ \sum_{k=-\infty}^{+\infty} \delta(t - \tau - k T_0) \right] d\tau$$

**Step 4 — Take the limit $T_0 \to \infty$ (the signal becomes aperiodic).**

As $T_0 \to \infty$:
- The integration limits extend to $\pm \infty$.
- The fundamental frequency $\omega_0 = 2\pi/T_0 \to 0$, becoming $d\omega$.
- The discrete index $k\omega_0$ becomes a continuous variable $\omega$.
- The discrete coefficient $a_k$ (scaled by $T_0$) becomes a continuous function $X(\omega)$.

Defining $X(\omega) = \lim_{T_0 \to \infty} T_0\, a_k$, we obtain:

$$x(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} X(\omega)\, e^{j\omega t}\, d\omega$$

And correspondingly:

$$X(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t)\, e^{-j\omega t}\, dt$$

**Conclusion:** The Fourier Transform is the continuous-frequency limit of the Fourier Series when the period tends to infinity. This is the foundational derivation that *KTU examiners love to ask as a 7-mark question*.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Parseval's Theorem for Energy Signals

We want to prove:

$$\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2\, dt = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert X(f) \vert^2\, df$$

**Step 1 — Start with the left-hand side and substitute the inverse FT.**

$$E = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x(t)\, x^*(t)\, dt$$

Using $x(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} X(f) e^{j2\pi f t} df$ and $x^*(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} X^*(f') e^{-j2\pi f' t} df'$:

$$E = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} X(f) e^{j2\pi f t} df \right] \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} X^*(f') e^{-j2\pi f' t} df' \right] dt$$

**Step 2 — Rearrange the order of integration.**

$$E = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} X(f)\, X^*(f') \left[ \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} e^{j2\pi(f - f')t}\, dt \right] df\, df'$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the inner integral using the Dirac delta identity.**

$$\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} e^{j2\pi(f - f')t}\, dt = \delta(f - f')$$

**Step 4 — Apply the sifting property of the delta function.**

$$E = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} X(f)\, X^*(f')\, \delta(f - f')\, df\, df' = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} X(f)\, X^*(f)\, df$$

**Step 5 — Combine the product into the magnitude squared.**

$$E = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert X(f) \vert^2\, df \quad \blacksquare$$

This proves the Parseval / Rayleigh Energy Theorem: **energy is conserved between the time and frequency domains**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: PSD of a Cosine Signal (Worked Example)

**Problem:** Find the PSD of $x(t) = A\cos(2\pi f_0 t)$ using the autocorrelation route.

**Step 1 — Compute the autocorrelation $R_x(\tau)$.**

$$R_x(\tau) = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T} \int_{-T/2}^{+T/2} A\cos(2\pi f_0 t) \cdot A\cos(2\pi f_0 (t+\tau))\, dt$$

Using the identity $\cos A \cos B = \frac{1}{2}[\cos(A-B) + \cos(A+B)]$:

$$R_x(\tau) = \frac{A^2}{2} \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{1}{T} \int_{-T/2}^{+T/2} \left[ \cos(2\pi f_0 \tau) + \cos(2\pi f_0 (2t + \tau)) \right] dt$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the integrals.**

The first term $\cos(2\pi f_0 \tau)$ is independent of $t$, so its integral over $[-T/2, T/2]$ is $T \cos(2\pi f_0 \tau)$.

The second term $\cos(2\pi f_0 (2t + \tau))$ is a sinusoid in $t$ with zero average over many periods, so its average vanishes as $T \to \infty$.

$$R_x(\tau) = \frac{A^2}{2} \cos(2\pi f_0 \tau)$$

**Step 3 — Take the Fourier Transform of $R_x(\tau)$.**

Using the identity $\mathcal{F}\{\cos(2\pi f_0 \tau)\} = \frac{1}{2}[\delta(f - f_0) + \delta(f + f_0)]$:

$$S_x(f) = \frac{A^2}{4}\left[\delta(f - f_0) + \delta(f + f_0)\right]$$

**Step 4 — Verify total power.**

$$P = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} S_x(f)\, df = \frac{A^2}{4} \cdot 2 = \frac{A^2}{2} \quad \checkmark$$

This matches the standard result: average power of $A\cos(\omega t)$ is $A^2/2$. **The two impulses in PSD represent the two complex exponentials that compose the cosine.**

---

## 3.4 Numerical Example: ESD Calculation

**Problem:** A signal $x(t) = e^{-at}u(t)$ for $a > 0$ has FT $X(f) = \frac{1}{a + j2\pi f}$. Compute the ESD and total energy.

**Step 1 — Compute the magnitude squared.**

$$X(f) = \frac{1}{a + j2\pi f} = \frac{a - j2\pi f}{a^2 + (2\pi f)^2}$$

$$\vert X(f) \vert^2 = \frac{1}{a^2 + (2\pi f)^2}$$

**Step 2 — Write the ESD.**

$$\psi_x(f) = \frac{1}{a^2 + (2\pi f)^2}$$

**Step 3 — Verify energy by time-domain integration.**

$$E = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-2at}\, dt = \frac{1}{2a}$$

**Step 4 — Verify by frequency-domain integration.**

$$E = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \frac{1}{a^2 + (2\pi f)^2}\, df$$

Using the standard integral $\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \frac{df}{a^2 + b^2 f^2} = \frac{\pi}{ab}$, with $b = 2\pi$:

$$E = \frac{\pi}{a \cdot 2\pi} = \frac{1}{2a} \quad \checkmark$$

**Both domains yield the same energy, confirming Parseval's theorem.**

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow: Time Domain to Frequency Domain Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input Signal x of t] --> B{Is Signal Periodic}
    B -- Yes --> C[Fourier Series FS]
    B -- No --> D[Fourier Transform FT]
    C --> E[Discrete Spectrum a sub k]
    D --> F[Continuous Spectrum X of f]
    E --> G[Power Spectral Density S sub x of f]
    F --> H[Energy Spectral Density psi sub x of f]
    H --> I[Parseval Energy Integral]
    G --> J[Wiener Khinchin Theorem]
    I --> K[Total Energy E Joules]
    J --> L[Total Average Power P Watts]
```

**Reading the diagram:** A periodic signal goes through Fourier Series to produce a discrete line spectrum. An aperiodic signal goes through Fourier Transform to produce a continuous spectrum. The squared magnitude gives the spectral density (ESD for energy signals, PSD for power signals), and integrating gives the total energy or power.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram: LTI System in Frequency Domain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Xin[X of f Input Spectrum] --> Mul[/Multiplier H of f/]
    Mul --> Yout[Y of f Output Spectrum]
    Hin[H of f Transfer Function] --> Mul
    Xin -.Inverse FT.-> Xt[x of t Time Domain]
    Yout -.Inverse FT.-> Yt[y of t Output Signal]
    Hdomain[H of f] -.Inverse FT.-> Htime[h of t Impulse Response]
```

**Engineering Meaning:** This block diagram captures the *most important practical application* of the Fourier Transform. When a filter with transfer function $H(f)$ processes an input $X(f)$, the output is simply $Y(f) = H(f) \cdot X(f)$. This is why communication engineers think in frequency domain: **filtering = spectral shaping = multiplication**.

---

## 4.3 Comparison Tree: FS vs FT Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Given Signal x of t] --> Q1{Is x of t Periodic}
    Q1 -- Yes --> FS[Fourier Series Applicable]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Does x of t satisfy Dirichlet Conditions}
    Q2 -- Yes --> FT[Fourier Transform Applicable]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Is it a Power Signal}
    Q3 -- Yes --> PSD[Use PSD via Autocorrelation]
    Q3 -- No --> Lap[Use Laplace Transform Instead]
    FS --> FSout[Discrete Harmonics a sub k]
    FT --> FTout[Continuous Spectrum X of f]
    FSout --> FSPSD[PSD Line Spectrum with Impulses]
    FTout --> FTESD[ESD equals X of f squared]
```

---

## 4.4 Spectral Density Visualization Concept

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph TimeDomain[Time Domain]
        Sig[x of t Mixed Signal]
    end
    subgraph Process[Fourier Operation]
        OpBlock[Fourier Transform]
    end
    subgraph FreqDomain[Frequency Domain]
        ESD[ESD psi sub x of f Continuous Curve]
        PSD[PSD S sub x of f Impulse Train or Continuous]
    end
    Sig --> OpBlock
    OpBlock --> ESD
    OpBlock --> PSD
    ESD --> EnergyArea[Integrate gives Total Energy]
    PSD --> PowerArea[Integrate gives Total Power]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The Fourier operation takes a time signal and produces a spectral density function. For energy signals, the ESD is a smooth continuous curve; for periodic power signals, the PSD is a discrete impulse train. **Integration under the spectral density curve always gives either total energy (Joules) or total average power (Watts).**
