# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State and explain the condition for the existence of the Fourier Transform of a continuous-time signal.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Fourier Transform of a continuous-time signal $x(t)$ exists if the signal satisfies the **Dirichlet Conditions**:

1. **Absolute Integrability:** The signal must be absolutely integrable over all time, i.e.,
   $$\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert x(t) \vert\, dt < \infty$$
   This guarantees the integral $\int x(t) e^{-j\omega t} dt$ converges.

2. **Finite Oscillations:** The signal $x(t)$ must have only a **finite number of discontinuities** and a **finite number of maxima and minima** within any finite time interval.

If both Dirichlet conditions are satisfied, the Fourier Transform $X(\omega)$ is guaranteed to exist. Signals that fail absolute integrability (e.g., $\sin(t)$, unit step) may still have FTs in the generalized sense (using distributions/delta functions).

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between energy spectral density and power spectral density.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | **Energy Spectral Density (ESD)** | **Power Spectral Density (PSD)** |
|---|---|---|
| Applicable to | **Energy signals** (finite $E$, zero $P$) | **Power signals** (infinite $E$, finite $P$) |
| Definition | $\psi_x(f) = \vert X(f) \vert^2$ | $S_x(f) = \mathcal{F}\{R_x(\tau)\}$ |
| Units | Joules per Hertz (J/Hz) | Watts per Hertz (W/Hz) |
| Total quantity | $\int \psi_x(f) df = E$ (total energy) | $\int S_x(f) df = P$ (average power) |
| Example | Single rectangular pulse | Periodic sinusoid |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** State and prove **Parseval's theorem** for energy signals. Explain its physical significance. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For the signal $x(t) = e^{-2t}u(t)$: **(i)** find the Fourier Transform $X(f)$, **(ii)** find the ESD $\psi_x(f)$, and **(iii)** calculate the total energy of the signal in both time and frequency domains. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Statement:** For an energy signal $x(t)$ with Fourier Transform $X(f)$, the total energy can be computed in either domain:

$$E = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert x(t) \vert^2\, dt = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \vert X(f) \vert^2\, df$$

**Proof:** Already derived in Section 3.2 above.

**Physical Significance:**
- **Energy conservation:** The total energy of a signal is preserved when transforming between the time and frequency domains.
- **Interpretation of $\vert X(f) \vert^2$:** The quantity $\vert X(f) \vert^2 df$ represents the **energy contained in the infinitesimal frequency band** $[f, f+df]$.
- **Practical use:** Engineers can compute signal energy by integrating the squared magnitude spectrum, which is often easier than integrating in the time domain (e.g., for signals that are not closed-form in time but have a known spectrum).

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**(i) Fourier Transform:**

Using the standard pair $\mathcal{F}\{e^{-at}u(t)\} = \dfrac{1}{a + j2\pi f}$ with $a = 2$:

$$X(f) = \frac{1}{2 + j2\pi f} = \frac{1}{2(1 + j\pi f)}$$

**(ii) Energy Spectral Density:**

$$\psi_x(f) = \vert X(f) \vert^2 = \frac{1}{4 + (2\pi f)^2} = \frac{1}{4(1 + \pi^2 f^2)}$$

**(iii) Total Energy — Time Domain:**

$$E = \int_{0}^{\infty} \vert e^{-2t} \vert^2\, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-4t}\, dt = \left[\frac{e^{-4t}}{-4}\right]_{0}^{\infty} = \frac{1}{4} \text{ Joules}$$

**Total Energy — Frequency Domain (Parseval's verification):**

$$E = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \frac{1}{4 + 4\pi^2 f^2}\, df = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \frac{df}{4(1 + \pi^2 f^2)}$$

Using the standard result $\int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} \frac{df}{1 + \pi^2 f^2} = \frac{1}{\pi} \cdot \pi = 1$:

$$E = \frac{1}{4} \cdot 1 = \frac{1}{4} \text{ Joules} \quad \checkmark$$

Both methods yield the **same energy of $0.25$ J**, confirming Parseval's theorem.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Compare the **Fourier Series** and **Fourier Transform** representations of signals. Show mathematically how the Fourier Transform can be derived as the limiting case of the Fourier Series as the period tends to infinity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A signal $x(t)$ has autocorrelation $R_x(\tau) = 5 + 3\cos(2\pi \cdot 10\, \tau)$. Find the **Power Spectral Density** $S_x(f)$, sketch it, and compute the **total average power**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Comparison:** Refer to the comparison table in Section 2.2.

**Limiting Derivation:** Refer to the full derivation in Section 3.1 (Step 1 to Step 4).

**Key Steps to Write in Exam:**
1. Start with complex exponential FS: $a_k = \frac{1}{T_0}\int_{T_0} x(t) e^{-jk\omega_0 t} dt$.
2. Define $X(k\omega_0) = T_0 a_k = \int_{-T_0/2}^{T_0/2} x(t) e^{-jk\omega_0 t} dt$.
3. Use $\omega_0 = 2\pi/T_0 \to 0$ as $T_0 \to \infty$, and $k\omega_0 \to \omega$ (continuous).
4. Limits $\pm T_0/2 \to \pm \infty$, integration becomes $X(\omega) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) e^{-j\omega t} dt$.
5. Conclude: FT is the **continuous-frequency** limit of FS for **infinite period**.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Find the FT of $R_x(\tau)$.**

Given: $R_x(\tau) = 5 + 3\cos(20\pi \tau)$

This has a DC component of $5$ and a cosine at $f_0 = 10$ Hz with amplitude $3$.

Using the FT pair:
- $\mathcal{F}\{\text{constant } 5\} = 5\, \delta(f)$
- $\mathcal{F}\{\cos(2\pi f_0 \tau)\} = \frac{1}{2}[\delta(f - f_0) + \delta(f + f_0)]$

**Step 2 — Combine to get the PSD:**

$$S_x(f) = 5\, \delta(f) + \frac{3}{2}\left[\delta(f - 10) + \delta(f + 10)\right] \quad \text{W/Hz}$$

**Step 3 — Sketch of PSD:**

The PSD consists of three impulses:
- One impulse of weight **5** at $f = 0$ Hz.
- One impulse of weight **1.5** at $f = +10$ Hz.
- One impulse of weight **1.5** at $f = -10$ Hz.

(Students should draw the frequency axis with arrows/impulses at these locations.)

**Step 4 — Total Average Power:**

By the Wiener–Khinchin theorem, the total power is the area under $S_x(f)$:

$$P = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} S_x(f)\, df = 5 + 1.5 + 1.5 = 8 \text{ Watts}$$

**Verification using $R_x(0)$:** For any autocorrelation function, $R_x(0) =$ average power:

$$P = R_x(0) = 5 + 3\cos(0) = 5 + 3 = 8 \text{ W} \quad \checkmark$$

Both methods agree: **total average power = 8 Watts**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the $1/2\pi$ or $1/T_0$ factors:** When using the angular frequency form $X(\omega)$, remember the inverse is $x(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int X(\omega) e^{j\omega t} d\omega$. Mixing up $f$ and $\omega$ form costs 2–3 marks.
> 
> 2. **Applying ESD to power signals:** Energy signals have $\psi_x(f) = \vert X(f) \vert^2$. For power signals, the FT often does not exist (e.g., $\cos(\omega t)$ has impulses in FT), so you **must** use the autocorrelation–PSD route. Mixing these is a 3-mark penalty.
> 
> 3. **Skipping the Dirichlet conditions:** In "existence of FT" questions, students often write only the absolute integrability condition. The second condition (finite maxima/minima/discontinuities) is **mandatory** for full marks.
> 
> 4. **In PSD of a cosine, missing the factor $A^2/4$:** The correct PSD is $\frac{A^2}{4}[\delta(f-f_0) + \delta(f+f_0)]$. A common error is $\frac{A^2}{2}$ per impulse, giving wrong total power. The factor of $1/2$ comes from $\cos^2 = (1 + \cos 2\theta)/2$ averaging.
> 
> 5. **Parseval's theorem sign errors:** Energy is $\int \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt$, NOT $\int x(t) x(-t) dt$ (which is correlation). Be precise.
> 
> 6. **Units:** Always mention units — J/Hz for ESD, W/Hz for PSD. KTU examiners reward unit awareness.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Master these before the exam:**

- [ ] **Fourier Transform** converts a time-domain signal $x(t)$ to frequency domain $X(f)$ via the integral $\int x(t) e^{-j2\pi f t} dt$.
- [ ] **Fourier Series** is for **periodic** signals → **discrete** spectrum at $kf_0$.
- [ ] **Fourier Transform** is for **aperiodic** signals → **continuous** spectrum.
- [ ] FT is obtained from FS by taking the **limit $T_0 \to \infty$**; the spacing $\Delta f = 1/T_0 \to 0$ and $T_0 a_k \to X(f)$.
- [ ] **Dirichlet conditions** for FT existence: absolute integrability + finite extrema/discontinuities.
- [ ] **ESD** $\psi_x(f) = \vert X(f) \vert^2$ — defined only for **energy signals** — has units J/Hz.
- [ ] **PSD** $S_x(f) = \mathcal{F}\{R_x(\tau)\}$ — defined for **power signals** — has units W/Hz.
- [ ] **Parseval's Theorem (Energy):** $E = \int \vert x(t) \vert^2 dt = \int \vert X(f) \vert^2 df = \int \psi_x(f) df$.
- [ ] **Total Power:** $P = \int S_x(f) df = R_x(0)$ (sampled at $\tau = 0$).
- [ ] **PSD of cosine** $A\cos(2\pi f_0 t)$: $S_x(f) = \frac{A^2}{4}[\delta(f - f_0) + \delta(f + f_0)]$.
- [ ] **Wiener–Khinchin Theorem:** PSD and autocorrelation are Fourier Transform pairs.
- [ ] **LTI system property:** $Y(f) = H(f) X(f)$ — convolution in time becomes multiplication in frequency.
- [ ] **LTI systems preserve energy** (up to gain): if $\int \vert h(t) \vert^2 dt < \infty$, then $E_y = \int \vert H(f) \vert^2 \psi_x(f) df$.
- [ ] **Real-world use:** PSD is used in communication link budgets, radar, noise analysis (thermal noise PSD $= kT/2$, Johnson–Nyquist formula). ESD is used in pulse design, optical signals, biomedical spike analysis.
- [ ] **For periodic signals** (which are power signals), the PSD is a **discrete line spectrum** (impulse train) whose weights are $\vert a_k \vert^2$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characterization of LTI systems: differential equation representation of continuous time LTI systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT522 - INTRODUCTION TO SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Characterization of LTI systems: differential equation representation of continuous time LTI systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Linear Time-Invariant (LTI) system** in the continuous-time domain is a system that simultaneously satisfies the properties of **linearity** (superposition and homogeneity) and **time-invariance** (shifted input produces a correspondingly shifted output). Such a system can be completely characterized in the time domain by a **Linear Constant-Coefficient Ordinary Differential Equation (LCCODE)** of order $N$, which mathematically relates the output $y(t)$ and its derivatives to the input $x(t)$ and its derivatives.

The canonical form of an $N^{th}$ order LCCODE for an LTI system is:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \frac{d^k y(t)}{dt^k} = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \frac{d^k x(t)}{dt^k}$$

where the coefficients $a_k$ and $b_k$ are **real constants** (with $a_N \neq 0$ for the system order to be $N$). The integers $N$ and $M$ are the order of the output derivative and the highest input derivative respectively, with $N \geq M$ required to make the system **causal and physically realizable**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The differential equation representation is the *time-domain* counterpart of the transfer function $H(s)$ in the Laplace/s-domain. Finding the impulse response $h(t)$ from a differential equation is a frequently tested 14-mark problem.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Think of an LTI system like a **mechanical spring-mass-damper** attached to a wall:
- The **input** $x(t)$ is the force you push into the spring.
- The **output** $y(t)$ is how far the mass moves from rest.
- The **mass**, **spring stiffness**, and **damper friction** are the *constant physical parameters* — they never change, which gives us the **time-invariance**.
- Because the spring's restoring force is linearly proportional to displacement (Hooke's Law), the system obeys **linearity**.

When you push the mass, the equation $m\ddot{y} + c\dot{y} + ky = x(t)$ tells the complete story of how the mass responds. This is exactly the role a differential equation plays for an LTI system — it is the **"law of physics"** that the system must obey at every instant $t$.

## 1.3 Key Terminology at a Glance

| Term | Meaning | Physical Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Order ($N$)** | Highest derivative of output | Equal to number of independent energy-storage elements |
| **Causal System** | Output depends only on present/past input | Requires $N \geq M$ |
| **Natural Response** | Solution to homogeneous equation | Depends on initial conditions, system "memory" |
| **Forced Response** | Particular solution | The system's reaction driven by the input |
| **Total Response** | Natural + Forced | Complete time-domain behavior |
| **Impulse Response $h(t)$** | Output when $x(t) = \delta(t)$ | Complete characterization of the LTI system |

> [!NOTE]
> **Constant Coefficients = Time-Invariance.** If the coefficients $a_k$ or $b_k$ were functions of $t$ (e.g., $a_k(t)$), the system would be **Linear Time-Variant (LTV)**, and the entire differential-equation machinery discussed here would no longer apply in the same form.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of the LCCODE Representation

A continuous-time LTI system is described by a single master equation. Let us dissect its anatomy step-by-step:

### Step 1 — The Left-Hand Side (Output Side)
The expression $\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \dfrac{d^k y(t)}{dt^k}$ represents the **system's own internal dynamics**. It contains the output $y(t)$ and all its time derivatives, weighted by the constant coefficients $a_k$.

- When $k=0$, the term is simply $a_0 y(t)$.
- When $k=1$, the term is $a_1 \dfrac{dy(t)}{dt}$, the first derivative (rate of change).
- When $k=N$, the term is $a_N \dfrac{d^N y(t)}{dt^N}$, the highest derivative that defines the order.

The number $N$ is the **order of the system**.

### Step 2 — The Right-Hand Side (Input Side)
The expression $\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \dfrac{d^k x(t)}{dt^k}$ represents the **driving function** — the way external inputs $x(t)$ are coupled into the system.

- If $M = 0$, the input appears *directly* with no derivatives: a "direct-form" feedforward.
- If $M > 0$, the system contains terms like $\frac{dx}{dt}$, which mathematically require differentiators. Such systems are harder to implement physically and have poor noise immunity.

### Step 3 — The Causal / Realizability Constraint
For a system to be **physically realizable** (i.e., not able to predict the future), the differential equation must not require future values of $x(t)$ to produce $y(t)$. This forces:

$$N \geq M$$

In practice, most KTU problems use $N = M$ or $N = M+1$.

### Step 4 — The Two-Part Solution Strategy
For a given input $x(t)$ and a set of $N$ initial conditions, the total response is:

$$y(t) = y_h(t) + y_p(t)$$

- $y_h(t)$: **Homogeneous (Natural) Response** — obtained by setting the RHS to zero. Solved using the *Characteristic Equation*.
- $y_p(t)$: **Particular (Forced) Response** — any function that satisfies the full equation. Its form depends on the input $x(t)$.

## 2.2 The Characteristic Equation

Setting the RHS to zero gives the **homogeneous equation**:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \frac{d^k y(t)}{dt^k} = 0$$

Substituting the trial solution $y_h(t) = e^{\lambda t}$ yields the **algebraic characteristic equation**:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \lambda^k = 0 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad a_N \lambda^N + a_{N-1} \lambda^{N-1} + \cdots + a_1 \lambda + a_0 = 0$$

The $N$ roots $\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \ldots, \lambda_N$ (possibly complex, possibly repeated) determine the natural response.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **exam-grade material** — every formula below is fair game for KTU questions.

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Statement | Engineering Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | General LCCODE | $\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k y^{(k)}(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k x^{(k)}(t)$ | Time-domain description of LTI system |
| 2 | Order of the system | $N$ (highest output derivative) | Equal to # of energy-storage elements |
| 3 | Causality constraint | $N \geq M$ | Output must not depend on future input |
| 4 | Characteristic Equation | $\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \lambda^k = 0$ | Roots govern natural response |
| 5 | Natural Response (distinct roots) | $y_h(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} C_i e^{\lambda_i t}$ | System's own memory / transient |
| 6 | Natural Response (repeated root, multiplicity $r$) | $C_1 e^{\lambda_1 t} + C_2 t e^{\lambda_1 t} + \cdots + C_r t^{r-1} e^{\lambda_1 t}$ | Handles repeated eigenvalues |
| 7 | Forced Response $y_p(t)$ | Any $y_p$ satisfying the full equation | Steady-state behavior driven by $x(t)$ |
| 8 | Total Response | $y(t) = y_h(t) + y_p(t)$ | Complete system output |
| 9 | Stability condition (BIBO) | $\text{Re}\{\lambda_i\} < 0 \;\;\forall i$ | All characteristic roots have negative real parts |
| 10 | Impulse Response $h(t)$ | $y(t)$ when $x(t) = \delta(t)$ and all ICs = 0 | Complete LTI characterization |
| 11 | Damping Coefficient $\alpha$ (2nd order) | $\alpha = \dfrac{a_1}{2 a_2}$ | Determines under/critical/over-damping |
| 12 | Natural Frequency $\omega_0$ (2nd order) | $\omega_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{a_0}{a_2}}$ | Undamped oscillation frequency |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The differential equation representation of LTI systems is the **backbone of nearly every physical engineering discipline**:

- **Electrical Circuits:** An RLC circuit obeys $L\dfrac{d^2i}{dt^2} + R\dfrac{di}{dt} + \dfrac{1}{C}i = \dfrac{dv}{dt}$, a 2nd-order LCCODE.
- **Mechanical Systems:** A spring-mass-damper obeys $M\ddot{x} + B\dot{x} + Kx = F(t)$.
- **Control Systems:** Every PID controller, servo motor, and robotic arm is described by an LCCODE.
- **Signal Processing:** Audio equalizers, digital filters (their analog prototypes), and biomedical signal analyzers all rely on this representation.
- **Communications:** Channel equalization and echo cancellation both depend on solving LCCODEs.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Form of the Natural Response from the Characteristic Equation

**Problem:** Given a 3rd-order LCCODE with characteristic roots $\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \lambda_3$, derive the general form of $y_h(t)$.

**Step-by-step derivation:**

We start with the homogeneous equation:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \frac{d^k y(t)}{dt^k} = 0$$

Assume an exponential trial solution $y(t) = e^{\lambda t}$ (non-trivial, so $\lambda$ is a constant to be determined). Substituting:

$$y(t) = e^{\lambda t}, \quad \frac{dy}{dt} = \lambda e^{\lambda t}, \quad \frac{d^2y}{dt^2} = \lambda^2 e^{\lambda t}, \quad \ldots, \quad \frac{d^N y}{dt^N} = \lambda^N e^{\lambda t}$$

Plugging into the homogeneous equation:

$$a_N \lambda^N e^{\lambda t} + a_{N-1} \lambda^{N-1} e^{\lambda t} + \cdots + a_1 \lambda e^{\lambda t} + a_0 e^{\lambda t} = 0$$

Factor out the common $e^{\lambda t}$ (which is never zero):

$$e^{\lambda t} \left[ a_N \lambda^N + a_{N-1} \lambda^{N-1} + \cdots + a_1 \lambda + a_0 \right] = 0$$

Since $e^{\lambda t} \neq 0$ for all $t$, the bracketed polynomial must vanish. This is the **characteristic equation**:

$$a_N \lambda^N + a_{N-1} \lambda^{N-1} + \cdots + a_1 \lambda + a_0 = 0$$

For an $N^{th}$-order system, this polynomial yields $N$ roots. For **distinct real roots** $\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \ldots, \lambda_N$, the principle of superposition (linearity) gives:

$$\boxed{y_h(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} C_i e^{\lambda_i t}}$$

where $C_i$ are constants determined by the $N$ initial conditions $y(0^-), y'(0^-), \ldots, y^{(N-1)}(0^-)$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Form of the Natural Response for Repeated Roots

**Problem:** If a characteristic root $\lambda_1$ has multiplicity $r$, derive the corresponding terms of $y_h(t)$.

**Step-by-step derivation:**

Suppose $y_1(t) = e^{\lambda_1 t}$ is one solution. We need $r-1$ additional independent solutions. Try $y(t) = t e^{\lambda_1 t}$. Computing derivatives:

$$\frac{dy}{dt} = e^{\lambda_1 t} + \lambda_1 t e^{\lambda_1 t} = (1 + \lambda_1 t) e^{\lambda_1 t}$$

Substitute into the homogeneous equation. Since $e^{\lambda_1 t}$ is itself a solution, the surviving terms involve the factor $t$:

$$0 = \left[\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \lambda_1^k \right] t e^{\lambda_1 t} + \left[\sum_{k=1}^{N} k a_k \lambda_1^{k-1}\right] e^{\lambda_1 t}$$

The first bracket is zero because $\lambda_1$ is a root. The second bracket is the derivative of the characteristic polynomial, which is also zero because $\lambda_1$ has multiplicity $r \geq 2$. Hence $t e^{\lambda_1 t}$ is also a solution.

Continuing inductively, we obtain $r$ linearly independent solutions for a root of multiplicity $r$:

$$\boxed{e^{\lambda_1 t},\; t e^{\lambda_1 t},\; t^2 e^{\lambda_1 t},\; \ldots,\; t^{r-1} e^{\lambda_1 t}}$$

The complete homogeneous response becomes:

$$y_h(t) = \sum_{i:\text{distinct}} C_i e^{\lambda_i t} + \sum_{j=1}^{r} D_j t^{j-1} e^{\lambda_1 t}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Form of the Natural Response for Complex-Conjugate Roots

**Problem:** Show that complex roots produce sinusoidal components in the natural response.

**Step-by-step derivation:**

Let $\lambda_{1,2} = \alpha \pm j \beta$ be a complex-conjugate pair (where $j = \sqrt{-1}$). The general form gives:

$$y_h(t) = C_1 e^{(\alpha + j\beta)t} + C_2 e^{(\alpha - j\beta)t}$$

Apply Euler's identity: $e^{j\beta t} = \cos(\beta t) + j \sin(\beta t)$. Grouping and re-expressing the arbitrary complex constants $C_1, C_2$ in polar form (so the result is real), we get:

$$\boxed{y_h(t) = e^{\alpha t} \left[ A \cos(\beta t) + B \sin(\beta t) \right]}$$

This is equivalent to a **damped sinusoid**:

$$y_h(t) = E e^{\alpha t} \cos(\beta t - \phi)$$

where $E = \sqrt{A^2 + B^2}$ and $\phi = \tan^{-1}(B/A)$. This is the form encountered in **RLC circuits, control systems, and second-order filters**.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Total Response: Solving the Initial-Value Problem

**Problem:** Solve $\dfrac{d^2 y(t)}{dt^2} + 3\dfrac{dy(t)}{dt} + 2 y(t) = 0$ with $y(0) = 1$ and $\dot{y}(0) = 0$.

**Step-by-step derivation:**

**Step 1:** Form the characteristic equation by replacing $y^{(k)}$ with $\lambda^k$:

$$\lambda^2 + 3\lambda + 2 = 0$$

**Step 2:** Factor the polynomial (sum-and-product rule: numbers summing to 3 and multiplying to 2 are 1 and 2):

$$(\lambda + 1)(\lambda + 2) = 0$$

**Step 3:** Solve for the roots:

$$\lambda_1 = -1, \quad \lambda_2 = -2$$

**Step 4:** Write the general homogeneous response:

$$y(t) = C_1 e^{-t} + C_2 e^{-2t}, \quad t \geq 0$$

**Step 5:** Apply the initial conditions to find $C_1$ and $C_2$. First, $y(0) = 1$:

$$C_1 + C_2 = 1$$

Next, compute $\dot{y}(t) = -C_1 e^{-t} - 2 C_2 e^{-2t}$ and apply $\dot{y}(0) = 0$:

$$-C_1 - 2C_2 = 0 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad C_1 = -2 C_2$$

**Step 6:** Solve the linear system:

$$-2 C_2 + C_2 = 1 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad C_2 = -1, \quad C_1 = 2$$

**Step 7:** Final total response:

$$\boxed{y(t) = 2 e^{-t} - e^{-2t}, \quad t \geq 0}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Forced Response via Method of Undetermined Coefficients

**Problem:** Solve $\dfrac{d^2 y(t)}{dt^2} + 3\dfrac{dy(t)}{dt} + 2 y(t) = 4 e^{3t}$ for $t \geq 0$ with zero initial conditions.

**Step-by-step derivation:**

**Step 1:** Find the natural response. The characteristic equation $\lambda^2 + 3\lambda + 2 = 0$ gives $\lambda_1 = -1, \lambda_2 = -2$ (same as Section 3.4). Therefore:

$$y_h(t) = C_1 e^{-t} + C_2 e^{-2t}$$

**Step 2:** Guess the form of the particular solution. The input is $4e^{3t}$. Since $3$ is **not** a root of the characteristic equation, the trial is:

$$y_p(t) = K e^{3t}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the derivatives: $\dot{y}_p = 3K e^{3t}$ and $\ddot{y}_p = 9K e^{3t}$.

**Step 4:** Substitute into the original ODE:

$$9K e^{3t} + 3(3K e^{3t}) + 2(K e^{3t}) = 4 e^{3t}$$

$$(9K + 9K + 2K) e^{3t} = 4 e^{3t}$$

$$20K e^{3t} = 4 e^{3t}$$

**Step 5:** Equate coefficients:

$$20K = 4 \quad \Longrightarrow \quad K = \frac{1}{5}$$

**Step 6:** So $y_p(t) = \dfrac{1}{5} e^{3t}$.

**Step 7:** Total response: $y(t) = y_h(t) + y_p(t) = C_1 e^{-t} + C_2 e^{-2t} + \dfrac{1}{5} e^{3t}$.

**Step 8:** Apply zero initial conditions. $y(0) = 0$ gives $C_1 + C_2 + \frac{1}{5} = 0$. Also, $\dot{y}(0) = -C_1 - 2C_2 + \frac{3}{5} = 0$.

**Step 9:** Solve the system:

$$\begin{aligned}
C_1 + C_2 &= -\tfrac{1}{5} \\
-C_1 - 2C_2 &= -\tfrac{3}{5}
\end{aligned}$$

Adding: $-C_2 = -\dfrac{1}{5} - \dfrac{3}{5} = -\dfrac{4}{5}$? Let us redo carefully:

From the second equation: $C_1 = \dfrac{3}{5} - 2C_2$. Substituting into the first:

$$\tfrac{3}{5} - 2C_2 + C_2 = -\tfrac{1}{5} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad -C_2 = -\tfrac{4}{5} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad C_2 = \tfrac{4}{5}$$

Then $C_1 = \dfrac{3}{5} - \dfrac{8}{5} = -\dfrac{5}{5} = -1$.

**Step 10:** Final total response:

$$\boxed{y(t) = -e^{-t} + \tfrac{4}{5} e^{-2t} + \tfrac{1}{5} e^{3t}, \quad t \geq 0}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification check:** At $t=0$, $y(0) = -1 + \frac{4}{5} + \frac{1}{5} = 0$ ✓. At $t=0$, $\dot{y}(0) = 1 - \frac{8}{5} + \frac{3}{5} = 0$ ✓.

## 3.6 Analytical Summary Table

| Problem Type | Homogeneous Part | Particular Part | Total Response |
|---|---|---|---|
| Zero input, distinct real roots | $\sum C_i e^{\lambda_i t}$ | 0 | $y_h$ |
| Zero input, complex pair $\alpha \pm j\beta$ | $e^{\alpha t}[A\cos\beta t + B\sin\beta t]$ | 0 | $y_h$ |
| Zero input, repeated root (mult. $r$) | $\sum_{j=0}^{r-1} D_j t^j e^{\lambda_1 t}$ | 0 | $y_h$ |
| Input $K e^{at}$, $a \neq \lambda_i$ | $\sum C_i e^{\lambda_i t}$ | $P e^{at}$ | $y_h + y_p$ |
| Input $K e^{at}$, $a = \lambda_1$ (resonance) | $\sum C_i e^{\lambda_i t}$ | $P t e^{a t}$ | $y_h + y_p$ |
| Input $K \cos(\omega_0 t)$ | $\sum C_i e^{\lambda_i t}$ | $P \cos\omega_0 t + Q \sin\omega_0 t$ | $y_h + y_p$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Representation of an LTI System from its LCCODE

For a generic 2nd-order LCCODE of the form:

$$a_2 \ddot{y}(t) + a_1 \dot{y}(t) + a_0 y(t) = b_2 \ddot{x}(t) + b_1 \dot{x}(t) + b_0 x(t)$$

the signal flow can be represented using **integrators** (the physical dual of differentiators), summing junctions, and gain blocks. The diagram below is the canonical **Direct Form I** realization.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    xnode["INPUT x of t"]:::input
    s2["s^2 gain b2"]:::gain
    s1["s^1 gain b1"]:::gain
    s0["s^0 gain b0"]:::gain
    sumA(("SUM A")):::sum
    int1["INTEGRATOR 1"]:::int
    int2["INTEGRATOR 2"]:::int
    fs2["1 over a2"]:::gain
    sumB(("SUM B")):::sum
    f1["gain a1 over a2"]:::gain
    f0["gain a0 over a2"]:::gain
    ynode["OUTPUT y of t"]:::output

    xnode --> s2 --> sumA
    xnode --> s1 --> sumA
    xnode --> s0 --> sumA
    sumA --> int1 --> int2
    int1 --> f1 --> sumB
    int2 --> f0 --> sumB
    int2 --> fs2 --> sumB
    sumB --> ynode

    classDef input fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef output fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    classDef gain fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    classDef int fill:#f8d7da,stroke:#721c24,color:#000
    classDef sum fill:#e2d5f0,stroke:#5a2d81,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The signal $x(t)$ is split into three parallel branches with gains $b_0, b_1, b_2$, summed at **SUM A**.
- The summed signal passes through two cascaded integrators, producing $\dot{y}$ and $y$ at their outputs.
- The integrators' outputs are fed back through gains $\tfrac{a_1}{a_2}$ and $\tfrac{a_0}{a_2}$, and summed at **SUM B** along with a unity feedforward path from the input of the first integrator (the $\tfrac{1}{a_2}$ gain block).
- The output of SUM B is the system's output $y(t)$.

## 4.2 Conceptual Block Architecture of LTI System Characterization

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["LTI System in Time Domain"]:::node
    B["Differential Equation LCCODE"]:::node
    C["Transfer Function H of s"]:::node
    D["Impulse Response h of t"]:::node
    E["Frequency Response H of j omega"]:::node
    F["Difference Equation Discrete Case"]:::node

    A --> B
    B -->|Laplace Transform| C
    B -->|Inverse Laplace| D
    C -->|Substitute s equals j omega| E
    B -->|Sampling and Discretization| F
    D -->|Convolution with x| A

    classDef node fill:#dbe9f4,stroke:#1f3a68,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> The differential equation representation sits at the **top of the LTI characterization hierarchy**. From it, one can derive every other representation: transfer function (via Laplace), impulse response (via inverse Laplace), frequency response (by evaluating on the imaginary axis), and even the discrete-time difference equation (via sampling and Z-transform).

## 4.3 Signal Flow for the Natural vs. Forced Response

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ic["Initial Conditions y0 and ydot0"]:::ic
    eq["Homogeneous Equation aN yN plus ... equals 0"]:::eq
    yh["Natural Response yh of t"]:::nat
    inp["Forcing Input x of t"]:::force
    fp["Particular Solution yp of t"]:::part
    sum(("PLUS")):::sum2
    yt["Total Output y of t"]:::out

    ic --> eq --> yh
    inp --> fp
    yh --> sum
    fp --> sum --> yt

    classDef ic fill:#fde2e4,stroke:#8b0000,color:#000
    classDef eq fill:#e2ecef,stroke:#1f3a68,color:#000
    classDef nat fill:#fff3cd,stroke:#856404,color:#000
    classDef force fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef part fill:#d4edda,stroke:#155724,color:#000
    classDef sum2 fill:#e2d5f0,stroke:#5a2d81,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#f9d77e,stroke:#7a5c00,color:#000
```
