<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

> State the **Sampling Theorem** for low-pass band-limited signals. Define the term **Nyquist Rate**.

**Model Answer:**

> The Sampling Theorem states that a continuous-time band-limited signal $x(t)$ with maximum frequency $f_m$ Hz can be uniquely reconstructed from its samples $x(nT_s)$ if the sampling frequency $f_s$ satisfies $f_s \geq 2f_m$.
>
> The **Nyquist Rate** is the minimum sampling rate required to avoid aliasing, defined as $f_{Nyq} = 2f_m$ samples per second. Sampling at this rate produces the smallest amount of data sufficient to capture all information in $x(t)$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> What is **aliasing**? How can it be prevented?

**Model Answer:**

> **Aliasing** is the phenomenon in which high-frequency components of a signal appear as lower-frequency components at the receiver when the sampling rate is below the Nyquist rate ($f_s < 2f_m$). The spectral replicas overlap, causing irreversible distortion.
>
> **Prevention methods:**
> 1. **Pre-filtering** the input signal with an **anti-aliasing low-pass filter** whose cutoff is $f_s / 2$ before sampling.
> 2. **Increasing the sampling rate** to satisfy $f_s \geq 2f_m$.
> 3. Using **oversampling techniques** in ADCs to relax analog filter requirements.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

> **(a)** Derive an expression for the spectrum of a sampled signal $x_s(t)$ obtained by multiplying a continuous-time signal $x(t)$ with a periodic impulse train $p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s)$. State clearly the conditions under which the original signal can be perfectly recovered. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A band-limited signal $x(t)$ has spectral content from DC to **5 kHz**.
>     (i) Determine the **minimum sampling rate** and the **Nyquist frequency**.
>     (ii) If the signal is sampled at **12 kHz**, calculate the **Oversampling Ratio (OSR)** and comment on the practicality of using this rate. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Step 1 — Define the impulse train and write its Fourier series:**

$$p(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT_s) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{j 2\pi k f_s t}$$

**Step 2 — Compute its Fourier Transform:**

$$P(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(f - k f_s) = f_s \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(f - k f_s)$$

**Step 3 — Multiply $x(t)$ by $p(t)$:**

$$x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot p(t) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x(t) \, e^{j 2\pi k f_s t}$$

**Step 4 — Take the Fourier Transform using the frequency-shifting property:**

$$X_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(f - k f_s)$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result:**

The spectrum of the sampled signal consists of **infinitely many scaled replicas of $X(f)$**, each centered at $f = k f_s$ for $k = 0, \pm 1, \pm 2, \ldots$

**Step 6 — Condition for perfect recovery:**

The replicas do not overlap **if and only if** $f_s \geq 2 f_m$, where $f_m$ is the highest frequency in $x(t)$. Under this condition, a low-pass filter with cutoff $f_s/2$ and gain $T_s$ extracts the $k=0$ replica, yielding $\hat{x}(t) = x(t)$ exactly.

**Model Solution to (b):**

**(i) Minimum Sampling Rate and Nyquist Frequency:**

Given $f_m = 5$ kHz:

$$f_{Nyq} = 2 f_m = 2 \times 5 = 10 \text{ kHz}$$

$$f_N = \frac{f_{Nyq}}{2} = 5 \text{ kHz}$$

The minimum sampling rate is **10 kHz**, and the Nyquist frequency is **5 kHz**.

**(ii) Oversampling Ratio at $f_s = 12$ kHz:**

$$\text{OSR} = \frac{f_s}{2 f_m} = \frac{12}{2 \times 5} = 1.2$$

**Comment:** An OSR of **1.2** is only **mildly oversampled**. While it technically avoids aliasing, it leaves very little transition band for the anti-aliasing filter, making the filter design difficult in practice. A higher OSR (e.g., 2 or above) would ease filter requirements at the cost of more samples per second.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

> **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the process of **reconstruction of a signal from its samples** using an ideal low-pass filter. Derive the **Whittaker-Shannon interpolation formula**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A signal $x(t) = 5 \cos(2\pi \cdot 800 \, t) + 3 \cos(2\pi \cdot 1500 \, t)$ is sampled at $f_s = 1000$ Hz.
>     (i) Identify the **Nyquist rate** for this signal.
>     (ii) Determine the **aliased frequencies** of both components.
>     (iii) Will perfect reconstruction be possible? Justify. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Step 1 — Block Diagram Description:**

The reconstruction system consists of:
- A **DAC** producing $y(t) = \sum_n x(nT_s) \, p(t - nT_s)$ (a staircase or pulsed waveform).
- An **ideal low-pass filter** $H(f)$ with cutoff $f_c = f_s/2$ and gain $T_s$.

**Step 2 — Ideal Reconstruction Filter:**

$$H(f) = \begin{cases} T_s, & \vert f \vert \leq f_s/2 \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 3 — Impulse Response of the Filter:**

$$h(t) = T_s \cdot \text{sinc}(f_s t) = \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t}{T_s}\right) = \frac{\sin(\pi t / T_s)}{\pi t / T_s}$$

**Step 4 — Convolve with the Sampled Signal:**

$$\hat{x}(t) = x_s(t) * h(t) = \left[ \sum_n x(nT_s) \delta(t - nT_s) \right] * \text{sinc}(t/T_s)$$

**Step 5 — Use the Sifting Property:**

$$\hat{x}(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s) \, \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t - nT_s}{T_s}\right)$$

This is the **Whittaker-Shannon interpolation formula**. Each sample is a coefficient of a shifted sinc function, and their sum reproduces the original continuous signal exactly when $f_s \geq 2f_m$.

**Model Solution to (b):**

**(i) Nyquist Rate:**

The signal has two frequency components: 800 Hz and 1500 Hz. Thus $f_m = 1500$ Hz.

$$f_{Nyq} = 2 \times 1500 = 3000 \text{ Hz}$$

**(ii) Aliased Frequencies at $f_s = 1000$ Hz:**

Since $f_s = 1000 < 3000$ Hz, the signal is **undersampled**. Spectral replicas will overlap.

- **Component 1 (800 Hz):** Find the nearest replica: $800 - 1000 = -200$ Hz → aliased to **200 Hz**.
- **Component 2 (1500 Hz):** Find the nearest replica: $1500 - 1000 = 500$ Hz → **500 Hz** remains in the baseband (since $f_s/2 = 500$ Hz, it folds to DC edge).

$$f_{alias,1} = \vert 800 - 1000 \vert = 200 \text{ Hz}$$

$$f_{alias,2} = \vert 1500 - 1000 \vert = 500 \text{ Hz}$$

**(iii) Reconstruction Possibility:**

**No, perfect reconstruction is NOT possible.** Since $f_s = 1000$ Hz $< f_{Nyq} = 3000$ Hz, the Nyquist criterion is violated. The original 800 Hz tone appears as 200 Hz, and the 1500 Hz tone appears as 500 Hz at the receiver. The two original tones cannot be distinguished from their aliases, and the original signal is **permanently lost**.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the $1/T_s$ scaling factor** in $X_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_k X(f - k f_s)$. Many students write $X_s(f) = \sum_k X(f - k f_s)$ — wrong, this loses 1-2 marks.
>
> 2. **Confusing Nyquist Rate with Nyquist Frequency:** Nyquist Rate = $2 f_m$ (a sampling rate), while Nyquist Frequency = $f_s / 2$ (a signal frequency). Examiners specifically check this distinction.
>
> 3. **Writing "sampling frequency" instead of "sampling rate"** — both are accepted, but consistency matters.
>
> 4. **Forgetting to state the band-limited assumption.** The Sampling Theorem holds **only** for band-limited signals. Real-world signals are never perfectly band-limited, so an anti-aliasing filter is mandatory in practice.
>
> 5. **Drawing the spectrum incorrectly** in frequency-domain problems: forgetting to label the **k = 0, ±1, ±2** replica positions.
>
> 6. **Not mentioning the filter gain $T_s$** in the reconstruction filter — full marks require $H(f) = T_s$ for $\vert f \vert \leq f_s/2$, not just "a low-pass filter."

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for the Sampling Process:**
>
> - **Sampling Theorem:** $f_s \geq 2 f_m$ for band-limited signals with max frequency $f_m$.
> - **Nyquist Rate:** $f_{Nyq} = 2 f_m$ — minimum sampling rate to avoid aliasing.
> - **Nyquist Frequency:** $f_N = f_s / 2$ — half the sampling rate, equals $f_m$ at the threshold.
> - **Sampling Period:** $T_s = 1 / f_s$ — time between two consecutive samples.
> - **Sampled Signal Equation:** $x_s(t) = \sum_n x(nT_s) \, \delta(t - nT_s)$.
> - **Spectrum of Sampled Signal:** $X_s(f) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_k X(f - k f_s)$ — periodic replication.
> - **Aliasing:** Occurs when $f_s < 2 f_m$; spectral replicas overlap; **irreversible** distortion.
> - **Anti-Aliasing Filter:** Low-pass filter with cutoff $f_s / 2$ placed **before** the sampler.
> - **Reconstruction Filter:** Low-pass filter with gain $T_s$ and cutoff $f_s / 2$ placed **after** the DAC.
> - **Whittaker-Shannon Interpolation:** $x(t) = \sum_n x(nT_s) \, \text{sinc}((t - nT_s)/T_s)$.
> - **Oversampling Ratio:** $\text{OSR} = f_s / (2 f_m)$. OSR $> 1$ means oversampling; OSR $= 1$ means Nyquist rate sampling.
> - **Practical Sampling Rates:** Audio CD = 44.1 kHz, Voice = 8 kHz, Studio = 48/96 kHz.
> - **Natural vs Flat-Top:** Natural sampling uses pulse-tops that follow $x(t)$; flat-top holds each sample constant for $T_s$ (used in real ADCs).
> - **Aliasing Formula:** $f_{alias} = \vert f - k f_s \vert$ where $k$ is the integer nearest to $f / f_s$.
> - **No Aliasing Theorem:** If and only if the input is strictly band-limited AND $f_s \geq 2 f_m$, perfect reconstruction is theoretically possible.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Impulse train sampling, sampling theorem, and aliasing effect; zero-order and first-order hold circuits
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT522 - INTRODUCTION TO SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Impulse train sampling, sampling theorem, and aliasing effect; zero-order and first-order hold circuits

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 3 — Impulse Train Sampling, Sampling Theorem, Aliasing & Reconstruction

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1.1 Impulse Train Sampling — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Impulse Train Sampling (Natural Sampling)**
> A continuous-time signal $x(t)$ is multiplied by a periodic train of unit impulses $\delta_{T_s}(t)$ to produce a sampled signal $x_s(t)$. Mathematically:
> $$x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot \delta_{T_s}(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT_s)\,\delta(t - nT_s)$$
> where $T_s$ is the **sampling period** and $f_s = 1/T_s$ is the **sampling frequency** (measured in **Hz**).

The sampled signal $x_s(t)$ is a weighted impulse train, where each impulse carries the amplitude of $x(t)$ at the instant $nT_s$. In the frequency domain, this multiplication becomes convolution with the impulse train spectrum.

### 1.1.2 Sampling Theorem — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Shannon–Nyquist Sampling Theorem**
> A bandlimited signal $x(t)$ having no frequency components above $f_m$ Hz (i.e., $X(f) = 0$ for $\vert f \vert > f_m$) can be **uniquely reconstructed** from its samples $x(nT_s)$ provided the sampling frequency satisfies:
> $$f_s \geq 2f_m$$
> The minimum theoretical sampling rate $f_s = 2f_m$ is called the **Nyquist Rate**. The maximum permissible sampling interval is $T_s = 1/(2f_m)$.

### 1.1.3 Aliasing Effect — Formal Definition

> [!WARNING]
> **Aliasing Effect**
> When a continuous-time signal is sampled at a rate **below the Nyquist rate** ($f_s < 2f_m$), the high-frequency spectral components fold back into the baseband and become indistinguishable from legitimate low-frequency content. This phenomenon is called **aliasing**, and it results in **irrecoverable information loss**.

### 1.1.4 Zero-Order Hold (ZOH) and First-Order Hold (FOH)

> [!NOTE]
> - **Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)**: A reconstruction device that holds each sample value $x(nT_s)$ **constant** for the entire sampling interval $T_s$ until the next sample arrives. The output is a **staircase waveform**.
> - **First-Order Hold (FOH)**: A reconstruction device that **linearly interpolates** between consecutive samples, producing a piecewise-linear (polygonal) approximation of the original signal.

### 1.1.5 Conceptual Analogy — The "Movie Reel" Interpretation

Think of a continuous signal as a smoothly flowing river. To record it, you take snapshots of the river's surface every $T_s$ seconds using a high-speed camera:

- **Impulse Train Sampling** = The shutter clicks at precise instants, capturing the water's height as a single "dot" of information.
- **Nyquist Theorem** = The camera must click at least **twice as fast** as the fastest ripple (frequency $f_m$) on the water surface; otherwise, slow motion playback will merge the ripples and show a "fake" wave pattern — this is **aliasing**.
- **ZOH** = You display each captured snapshot as a still photo held flat for $T_s$ seconds, producing a step-like movie.
- **FOH** = You draw straight lines between consecutive snapshots, producing a smoother (though still angular) reconstruction.

### 1.1.6 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Nyquist Rate** $= 2f_m$ Hz — the lowest sampling rate that avoids aliasing.
- **Nyquist Frequency** $= f_s/2$ Hz — the highest frequency that can be unambiguously represented.
- **Angular Sampling Frequency** $\omega_s = 2\pi f_s$ rad/s.
- **Anti-Aliasing Filter Cutoff** typically chosen at $f_s/2$ Hz to pre-filter the input.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Frequency-Domain Representation of Impulse Train Sampling

When $x_s(t) = x(t)\,\delta_{T_s}(t)$, the Fourier transform becomes a **periodic replication** of the original spectrum:

$$X_s(\omega) = \frac{1}{T_s} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(\omega - k\omega_s)$$

**Interpretation:** Sampling in the time domain causes **periodic spectral replication** in the frequency domain, centered at integer multiples of $\omega_s = 2\pi/T_s$.

### Step-by-Step Logical Flow

1. **Input Stage:** Original signal $x(t)$ has spectrum $X(\omega)$ bandlimited to $\vert\omega\vert \leq \omega_m$.
2. **Sampling Stage:** Multiplying by $\delta_{T_s}(t)$ convolves $X(\omega)$ with the impulse train spectrum.
3. **Replication Stage:** The baseband spectrum is replicated at $\omega = k\omega_s$ for every integer $k$.
4. **Reconstruction Stage:** A lowpass filter with cutoff $\omega_c = \omega_m$ recovers $X(\omega)$ provided replicas do **not overlap**.

## 2.2 Condition for No Aliasing (Non-Overlap Criterion)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The spectral replicas do **not overlap** if and only if:
> $$\omega_s \geq 2\omega_m \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad f_s \geq 2f_m$$
> Violation of this condition causes adjacent replicas to merge, producing **aliasing distortion**.

## 2.3 Reconstruction — Ideal Lowpass Filter

To perfectly recover $x(t)$ from $x_s(t)$:

$$x(t) = T_s \cdot \mathcal{F}^{-1}\bigl\{X_s(\omega) \cdot H_{\text{LP}}(\omega)\bigr\}$$

where the ideal lowpass filter has:
$$H_{\text{LP}}(\omega) = \begin{cases} T_s, & \vert\omega\vert \leq \omega_m \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

The impulse response of this ideal interpolator is the **sinc function**:
$$h(t) = \text{sinc}\left(\frac{t}{T_s}\right) = \frac{\sin(\pi t / T_s)}{\pi t / T_s}$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **S.No** | **Quantity** | **Formula** | **Units / Remarks** |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Sampled signal (time domain) | $x_s(t) = \sum_n x(nT_s)\delta(t - nT_s)$ | Dimensionless weighted impulses |
| 2 | Sampled signal (frequency domain) | $X_s(\omega) = \frac{1}{T_s}\sum_k X(\omega - k\omega_s)$ | Periodic spectrum |
| 3 | Sampling frequency | $f_s = 1/T_s$ | Hz |
| 4 | Angular sampling frequency | $\omega_s = 2\pi f_s$ | rad/s |
| 5 | Nyquist rate | $f_N = 2f_m$ | Hz |
| 6 | Maximum allowed interval | $T_s^{\max} = 1/(2f_m)$ | seconds |
| 7 | Ideal reconstruction filter | $H(\omega) = T_s$ for $\vert\omega\vert \leq \omega_m$ | Gain factor |
| 8 | ZOH transfer function | $H_{\text{ZOH}}(s) = \dfrac{1 - e^{-sT_s}}{s}$ | Laplace domain |
| 9 | ZOH frequency response | $H_{\text{ZOH}}(j\omega) = T_s \cdot \dfrac{\sin(\omega T_s/2)}{\omega T_s/2} \cdot e^{-j\omega T_s/2}$ | Magnitude rolls off as sinc |
| 10 | FOH impulse response | $h_{\text{FOH}}(t) = \dfrac{t}{T_s}$ for $0 \leq t \leq T_s$ | Linear ramp |
| 11 | Aliasing frequency | $f_{\text{alias}} = \vert f - f_s \vert$ for $f > f_s/2$ | Folded frequency |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Digital Audio (CD Standard):** Audio signals are bandlimited to **$f_m = 20$ kHz** and sampled at $f_s = 44.1$ kHz (slightly above the Nyquist rate of 40 kHz).
- **Medical Imaging (MRI/CT):** Bandlimited k-space data is sampled at rates governed by the Nyquist criterion to avoid spatial aliasing artifacts.
- **Telecommunications:** Anti-aliasing filters (lowpass with cutoff $f_s/2$) precede every ADC in receivers.
- **Control Systems:** ZOH is the standard model for the **actuator** that converts a discrete-time control signal to a continuous-time plant input.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Frequency Spectrum of Impulse Train Sampled Signal

**Given:** $x_s(t) = x(t)\cdot \delta_{T_s}(t)$, where $\delta_{T_s}(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty}\delta(t - nT_s)$.

**Step 1:** Express the impulse train in its Fourier series form (since it is periodic with period $T_s$):

$$\delta_{T_s}(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty}\delta(t - nT_s) = \frac{1}{T_s}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{jk\omega_s t}$$

where $\omega_s = 2\pi/T_s$. The Fourier series coefficients are all equal to $1/T_s$.

**Step 2:** Substitute this expansion into the sampled signal expression:

$$x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot \frac{1}{T_s}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} e^{jk\omega_s t}$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange to isolate the summation:

$$x_s(t) = \frac{1}{T_s}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x(t)\,e^{jk\omega_s t}$$

**Step 4:** Take the Fourier transform of both sides. Using the **frequency shift property** $\mathcal{F}\{x(t)e^{jk\omega_s t}\} = X(\omega - k\omega_s)$:

$$X_s(\omega) = \frac{1}{T_s}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X(\omega - k\omega_s)$$

**Conclusion:** The spectrum of the sampled signal is a **periodic replication** of $X(\omega)$ scaled by $1/T_s$ and centered at $\omega = k\omega_s$ for all integers $k$.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Non-Overlap Condition and the Nyquist Rate

**Step 1:** The original spectrum $X(\omega)$ is assumed bandlimited: $X(\omega) = 0$ for $\vert\omega\vert > \omega_m$.

**Step 2:** The first replica (centered at $\omega_s$) extends from $\omega_s - \omega_m$ to $\omega_s + \omega_m$.

**Step 3:** The baseband replica extends from $-\omega_m$ to $+\omega_m$.

**Step 4:** For **no overlap** between the baseband and the first positive replica:

$$\omega_s - \omega_m \geq \omega_m$$

**Step 5:** Solve the inequality:

$$\omega_s \geq 2\omega_m$$

**Step 6:** Convert to ordinary frequency by dividing by $2\pi$:

$$f_s \geq 2f_m$$

**Conclusion:** The **Nyquist rate** is $f_s^{\min} = 2f_m$. The maximum allowed sampling period is $T_s^{\max} = 1/(2f_m)$.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Zero-Order Hold Frequency Response

The ZOH output is a staircase waveform. Its impulse response is a rectangular pulse of width $T_s$ and height 1:

$$h_{\text{ZOH}}(t) = u(t) - u(t - T_s)$$

**Step 1:** Take the Fourier transform of the rectangular pulse:

$$H_{\text{ZOH}}(\omega) = \int_0^{T_s} e^{-j\omega t}\,dt$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate the integral:

$$H_{\text{ZOH}}(\omega) = \left[\frac{e^{-j\omega t}}{-j\omega}\right]_0^{T_s} = \frac{1 - e^{-j\omega T_s}}{j\omega}$$

**Step 3:** Factor out $e^{-j\omega T_s/2}$ from the numerator:

$$H_{\text{ZOH}}(\omega) = \frac{e^{-j\omega T_s/2}(e^{j\omega T_s/2} - e^{-j\omega T_s/2})}{j\omega}$$

**Step 4:** Apply Euler's identity $e^{j\theta} - e^{-j\theta} = 2j\sin(\theta)$:

$$H_{\text{ZOH}}(\omega) = e^{-j\omega T_s/2} \cdot \frac{2\sin(\omega T_s/2)}{\omega}$$

**Step 5:** Rewrite in sinc form:

$$H_{\text{ZOH}}(\omega) = T_s \cdot \frac{\sin(\omega T_s/2)}{\omega T_s/2} \cdot e^{-j\omega T_s/2}$$

**Conclusion:** The ZOH has a **sinc-shaped magnitude response** with linear phase delay $T_s/2$. The first null occurs at $\omega = 2\pi/T_s = \omega_s$, providing roughly $20$ dB/decade attenuation beyond the Nyquist frequency.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Aliasing — Frequency Folding Formula

**Given:** Signal contains a frequency $f_1 > f_s/2$. Find the apparent (aliased) frequency $f_a$ observed after sampling.

**Step 1:** Compute the integer $k$ such that $\vert f_1 - kf_s \vert \leq f_s/2$:

$$k = \left\lfloor \frac{f_1 + f_s/2}{f_s} \right\rfloor$$

**Step 2:** The aliased frequency is:

$$f_a = \vert f_1 - kf_s \vert$$

**Worked Example:** Let $f_1 = 7$ kHz and $f_s = 6$ kHz. Then $f_s/2 = 3$ kHz. Since $7$ kHz $> 3$ kHz, aliasing occurs:

$$k = \left\lfloor \frac{7 + 3}{6} \right\rfloor = \left\lfloor 1.667 \right\rfloor = 1$$

$$f_a = \vert 7 - 1 \cdot 6 \vert = 1 \text{ kHz}$$

The 7 kHz tone **appears** as a 1 kHz tone after sampling — a classic case of frequency folding.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: First-Order Hold Impulse Response and Frequency Response

The FOH linearly interpolates between samples. Its impulse response is a triangular pulse:

$$h_{\text{FOH}}(t) = \begin{cases} 1 + \dfrac{t}{T_s}, & -T_s \leq t \leq 0 \\[6pt] 1 - \dfrac{t}{T_s}, & 0 \leq t \leq T_s \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 1:** The FOH can be expressed as the **convolution of two ZOH** responses:

$$h_{\text{FOH}}(t) = h_{\text{ZOH}}(t) * h_{\text{ZOH}}(t)$$

**Step 2:** In the frequency domain, this means:

$$H_{\text{FOH}}(\omega) = \bigl[H_{\text{ZOH}}(\omega)\bigr]^2 = T_s^2 \cdot \left[\frac{\sin(\omega T_s/2)}{\omega T_s/2}\right]^2 \cdot e^{-j\omega T_s}$$

**Step 3:** The FOH has a **sinc-squared magnitude** with greater high-frequency attenuation than the ZOH, making it a better lowpass approximation.

**Conclusion:** FOH is essentially a **second-order lowpass filter** that is more accurate than ZOH for bandlimited signals.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| **Property** | **Ideal Reconstruction** | **Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)** | **First-Order Hold (FOH)** |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| Impulse response | $\text{sinc}(t/T_s)$ | Rectangular pulse $T_s$ wide | Triangular pulse $2T_s$ wide |
| Magnitude response | Flat brick-wall | $\vert\text{sinc}(\omega T_s/2)\vert$ | $\bigl[\text{sinc}(\omega T_s/2)\bigr]^2$ |
| Phase response | Linear | Linear with delay $T_s/2$ | Linear with delay $T_s$ |
| Implementation complexity | Not realizable | Simple (capacitor + switch) | Moderate (integrator + switch) |
| High-frequency roll-off | Infinite | $-20$ dB/decade | $-40$ dB/decade |
| Reconstruction error | Zero (for $f_s \geq 2f_m$) | Step discontinuities visible | Piecewise-linear approximation |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End Sampling and Reconstruction Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    B --> C[Multiplier with Impulse Train]

    E --> F[Reconstruction Filter ZOH or FOH]
    F --> G[Reconstructed Signal xr of t]
    G --> H[Comparison with Original]
```

```mermaid

    A1[Original Spectrum X omega centered at 0] --> B1[Replicate at omega equals k omega s]
    B1 --> C1[Replica at k equals minus 1 centered at minus omega s]

    B1 --> E1[Replica at k equals plus 1 centered at plus omega s]

    C1 --> G1{Check: Do replicas overlap?}
    D1 --> G1
    E1 --> G1
    G1 -- No overlap: fs greater than 2fm --> H1[Apply LPF to recover X omega]
    G1 -- Overlap: fs less than 2fm --> I1[Aliasing occurs: irreversible distortion]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📝 PART A QUESTIONS (2 × 3 = 6 Marks)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State and explain the Nyquist sampling theorem. What is the significance of the Nyquist rate?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> The **Nyquist sampling theorem** states that a bandlimited signal $x(t)$ with maximum frequency $f_m$ Hz can be completely reconstructed from its samples $x(nT_s)$ if the sampling frequency satisfies $f_s \geq 2f_m$.

> The **Nyquist rate** is the minimum sampling frequency $f_N = 2f_m$ required to avoid aliasing. Its significance is that it sets the theoretical lower bound for the sampling rate in any digital system, ensuring that spectral replicas do not overlap and the original signal can be recovered by a lowpass filter.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is aliasing? How is it prevented in practical systems?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Aliasing** is the irreversible distortion that occurs when a continuous-time signal is sampled below the Nyquist rate ($f_s < 2f_m$). High-frequency components fold back into the baseband, appearing as false low-frequency content.

> **Prevention methods:**
> 1. Use an **anti-aliasing lowpass filter** with cutoff $f_c = f_s/2$ before sampling.
> 2. Choose a sampling frequency **strictly greater than** $2f_m$.
> 3. Ensure the input signal is **bandlimited** before sampling (in practice, all real signals are lowpass-filtered first).

---

## 📝 PART B QUESTIONS (Internal Choice — 14 Marks Each)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**(a)** Derive the expression for the spectrum of an impulse-train sampled signal. Show clearly the conditions required to avoid aliasing. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A signal $x(t)$ has a maximum frequency component of **$f_m = 10$ kHz**. Determine: (i) the Nyquist rate, (ii) the Nyquist interval, and (iii) the spectrum replication spacing in rad/s. If the signal is sampled at $f_s = 15$ kHz, will aliasing occur? Justify. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question A**

#### **Solution to A(a): Spectrum Derivation** — 7 marks

**Step 1:** Define the impulse train. Let $\delta_{T_s}(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty}\delta(t - nT_s)$ be a periodic impulse train with period $T_s$.

**Step 2:** Express the sampled signal:

$$x_s(t) = x(t)\cdot\delta_{T_s}(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty}x(nT_s)\delta(t - nT_s)$$

**Step 3:** Use the complex exponential Fourier series representation of the impulse train:

$$\delta_{T_s}(t) = \frac{1}{T_s}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty}e^{jk\omega_s t}, \quad \omega_s = \frac{2\pi}{T_s}$$

**Step 4:** Substitute into the sampling equation:

$$x_s(t) = \frac{1}{T_s}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty}x(t)e^{jk\omega_s t}$$

**Step 5:** Apply the Fourier transform and use the frequency-shift property $\mathcal{F}\{x(t)e^{jk\omega_s t}\} = X(\omega - k\omega_s)$:

$$\boxed{X_s(\omega) = \frac{1}{T_s}\sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty}X(\omega - k\omega_s)}$$

**Step 6:** State the no-aliasing condition. For spectral replicas not to overlap:

$$\omega_s - \omega_m \geq \omega_m \quad\Rightarrow\quad \omega_s \geq 2\omega_m \quad\Rightarrow\quad f_s \geq 2f_m$$

**Step 7:** Conclusion: The spectrum is a periodic replication of $X(\omega)$ centered at $\omega = k\omega_s$, and non-overlap requires the sampling rate to be at least twice the highest signal frequency.

---

#### **Solution to A(b): Numerical Problem** — 7 marks

**Given:** $f_m = 10$ kHz.

**Part (i): Nyquist rate**

$$f_N = 2f_m = 2 \times 10 = 20 \text{ kHz}$$

**Part (ii): Nyquist interval**

$$T_N = \frac{1}{f_N} = \frac{1}{20{,}000} = 50\,\mu s$$

**Part (iii): Spectral replication spacing in rad/s**

$$\omega_s = 2\pi f_s$$

For Nyquist rate sampling, $\omega_s = 2\pi(20{,}000) = 4\pi \times 10^4 = 1.2566 \times 10^5$ rad/s.

**Aliasing check at $f_s = 15$ kHz:**

Since $f_s = 15$ kHz $< 2f_m = 20$ kHz, the Nyquist criterion is violated.

$$\text{Replica extent} = \omega_s - \omega_m = 2\pi(15{,}000) - 2\pi(10{,}000) = 2\pi(5{,}000) > \omega_m$$

The replicas overlap, and **aliasing WILL occur**. ✗

**Counter-Example verification using folding formula:** A 12 kHz tone samples at $f_s = 15$ kHz:
$$f_a = \vert 12 - 15 \vert = 3 \text{ kHz}$$ (a clear false-low-frequency artifact).

---

### **Question B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**(a)** Explain the working of a Zero-Order Hold (ZOH) circuit. Derive its frequency response and explain why it approximates a lowpass filter. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare ZOH and FOH reconstruction methods. A signal with $f_m = 4$ kHz is sampled at $f_s = 10$ kHz and reconstructed using a ZOH. Find the percentage amplitude distortion at the highest signal frequency due to the sinc roll-off. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B**

#### **Solution to B(a): ZOH Working and Frequency Response** — 7 marks

**Step 1:** Define ZOH operation. The ZOH holds each sampled value $x(nT_s)$ constant for one sampling period, producing a staircase approximation.

**Step 2:** Impulse response of ZOH:

$$h_{\text{ZOH}}(t) = u(t) - u(t - T_s) = \begin{cases} 1, & 0 \leq t < T_s \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

**Step 3:** Take the Fourier transform:

$$H_{\text{ZOH}}(\omega) = \int_0^{T_s}e^{-j\omega t}\,dt = \frac{1 - e^{-j\omega T_s}}{j\omega}$$

**Step 4:** Factor $e^{-j\omega T_s/2}$:

$$H_{\text{ZOH}}(\omega) = e^{-j\omega T_s/2}\cdot\frac{2\sin(\omega T_s/2)}{\omega} = T_s\cdot\text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{\omega T_s}{2\pi}\right)\cdot e^{-j\omega T_s/2}$$

**Step 5:** Magnitude response:

$$\vert H_{\text{ZOH}}(\omega)\vert = T_s\left\vert\frac{\sin(\omega T_s/2)}{\omega T_s/2}\right\vert$$

**Step 6:** Lowpass interpretation:
- At $\omega = 0$: $\vert H\vert = T_s$ (DC gain).
- First null at $\omega = 2\pi/T_s = \omega_s$.
- The response rolls off as $\text{sinc}$, providing $-20$ dB/decade attenuation beyond $\omega_s/2$ — the **Nyquist frequency**.

**Step 7:** Conclusion: The ZOH acts as a crude **lowpass interpolator** with linear phase delay $T_s/2$, attenuating spectral replicas while approximately preserving the baseband signal.

---

#### **Solution to B(b): ZOH vs FOH and Distortion Calculation** — 7 marks

**Comparison Table:**

| **Aspect** | **ZOH** | **FOH** |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Interpolation | Constant (zero-order) | Linear (first-order) |
| Impulse response | Rectangular | Triangular |
| Frequency response | Sinc | Sinc-squared |
| Roll-off rate | $-20$ dB/dec | $-40$ dB/dec |
| Reconstruction quality | Step artifacts | Smoother, polygonal |
| Implementation | Simple D/A + capacitor | Integrator + switch |

**Distortion calculation:**

Given: $f_m = 4$ kHz, $f_s = 10$ kHz.

$$\omega_m T_s = 2\pi f_m / f_s = 2\pi(4/10) = 0.8\pi \text{ rad}$$

$$\left\vert H_{\text{ZOH}}(\omega_m)\right\vert = T_s\left\vert\frac{\sin(0.4\pi)}{0.4\pi}\right\vert$$

Compute numerically:
$$\sin(0.4\pi) = \sin(72°) = 0.9511$$
$$0.4\pi = 1.2566$$
$$\frac{0.9511}{1.2566} = 0.7568$$

So $\vert H_{\text{ZOH}}(f_m)\vert = 0.7568 \cdot T_s$ compared to the DC gain $T_s$.

**Percentage amplitude distortion:**
$$\text{Distortion} = (1 - 0.7568) \times 100\% = 24.32\%$$

A noticeable **24.32%** amplitude droop occurs at the highest signal frequency — a key reason why practical systems apply **post-filter equalization** (the **$\sin(x)/x$ corrector**) to flatten the response.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the $1/T_s$ scaling factor** in $X_s(\omega)$ derivation — costs 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing Nyquist rate with sampling rate**: The Nyquist rate is $2f_m$, not $f_m$.
> 3. **Sign errors in aliasing folding**: Always use $f_a = \vert f - kf_s \vert$ with the correct integer $k$.
> 4. **Ignoring the linear-phase term** $e^{-j\omega T_s/2}$ in ZOH response — this represents a **time delay** and must be mentioned.
> 5. **Not stating the no-aliasing condition** explicitly as $\omega_s \geq 2\omega_m$ before solving numerical problems.
> 6. **Confusing natural sampling (impulse train) with flat-top sampling (ZOH)** — they have different spectral expressions.

---

## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> ✅ **Quick-Revision Checklist**
> - **Impulse train sampling**: $x_s(t) = x(t)\cdot\delta_{T_s}(t)$ — produces weighted impulse train in time and periodic spectrum in frequency.
> - **Spectrum formula**: $X_s(\omega) = \frac{1}{T_s}\sum_k X(\omega - k\omega_s)$ — periodic with spacing $\omega_s$.
> - **Nyquist theorem**: $f_s \geq 2f_m$ for perfect reconstruction.
> - **Nyquist rate** $= 2f_m$; **Nyquist interval** $T_s = 1/(2f_m)$.
> - **Aliasing**: Spectral overlap when $f_s < 2f_m$ — **irreversible** distortion.
> - **Aliasing frequency**: $f_a = \vert f - kf_s \vert$ where $k$ is chosen so that $f_a \leq f_s/2$.
> - **Anti-aliasing filter**: Analog LPF with cutoff $f_s/2$ placed **before** the sampler.
> - **ZOH**: Holds sample constant for $T_s$ → staircase output → sinc magnitude response → $-20$ dB/dec roll-off → linear phase $T_s/2$ delay.
> - **ZOH transfer function**: $H_{\text{ZOH}}(s) = (1 - e^{-sT_s})/s$.
> - **FOH**: Linear interpolation between samples → triangular impulse response → sinc-squared magnitude → $-40$ dB/dec roll-off → better lowpass approximation.
> - **FOH is mathematically $h_{\text{ZOH}} * h_{\text{ZOH}}$** — equivalent to applying two cascaded ZOHs.
> - **Practical correction**: Use a digital $\sin(x)/x$ equalizer to compensate for the ZOH droop at high frequencies.
> - **Audio CD standard**: $f_m = 20$ kHz, $f_s = 44.1$ kHz, gives $\sim 10\%$ safety margin above the Nyquist rate.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Signal reconstruction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT522 - INTRODUCTION TO SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Signal reconstruction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Signal Reconstruction** is the process of recovering a continuous-time (analog) signal $x(t)$ from its discrete-time samples $x(nT)$, where $T$ is the sampling period, using an appropriate lowpass interpolation filter.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, signal reconstruction completes the cycle that begins with the **Sampling Theorem**. Mathematically, given a bandlimited signal $x(t)$ with maximum frequency component $f_m$ (in Hz) or $\omega_m$ (in rad/s), if it is sampled at or above the **Nyquist Rate** $f_s \geq 2f_m$, the original signal can be perfectly reconstructed using the **Whittaker–Shannon Interpolation Formula**:

$$
x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT) \, \frac{\sin\left(\pi(t - nT)/T\right)}{\pi(t - nT)/T} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT) \, \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t - nT}{T}\right)
$$

This reconstruction is realized in hardware by a **lowpass filter** (often called a **reconstruction filter** or **anti-imaging filter**) with cutoff frequency $\omega_c = \omega_s/2$, where $\omega_s = 2\pi/T$ is the sampling angular frequency.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Dotted Picture" Metaphor

Imagine you have a black-and-white photograph made of dots (a *halftone print*). Each dot is just a sample of the original image. Now, if the dots are small and dense enough, your eye naturally "fills in" the gaps and reconstructs the smooth picture. **Signal reconstruction works the exact same way**:

- **Samples** $x(nT)$ → the discrete dots
- **Sinc interpolation** → the eye's "filling-in" process (performed mathematically by a lowpass filter)
- **Original signal** $x(t)$ → the smooth, continuous picture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight**: Reconstruction is only *perfect* when the Nyquist criterion is met. If samples are too sparse (sampling rate too low), the picture breaks apart — this is the phenomenon of **aliasing**, and no filter can fully recover the original afterwards.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Constants

- **Nyquist Rate**: $f_N = 2f_m$ (minimum sampling rate to avoid aliasing)
- **Sampling Frequency**: $f_s = 1/T$
- **Reconstruction Filter Cutoff**: $\omega_c = \pi/T$ rad/s or $f_c = 1/(2T)$ Hz
- **Sinc Function Normalization**: The normalized form $\text{sinc}(x) = \frac{\sin(\pi x)}{\pi x}$ is the standard in DSP.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Reconstruction Process — Step-by-Step Logic

Let us trace the signal through the complete sampling–reconstruction pipeline:

### Step 1: Sampling in Time Domain
The continuous signal $x(t)$ is multiplied by an impulse train $\delta_T(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \delta(t - nT)$, producing an impulse-sampled signal $x_s(t)$:

$$
x_s(t) = x(t) \cdot \delta_T(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT)\, \delta(t - nT)
$$

### Step 2: Frequency Domain Picture
By the multiplication property of the Fourier Transform, convolution in frequency occurs:

$$
X_s(j\omega) = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} X\!\left(j(\omega - k\omega_s)\right)
$$

This creates **periodic replicas** of $X(j\omega)$ centered at multiples of $\omega_s = 2\pi/T$, each scaled by $1/T$.

### Step 3: Why a Lowpass Filter Works
If $\omega_s \geq 2\omega_m$ (no aliasing), the spectral replicas are **non-overlapping**. Passing $X_s(j\omega)$ through an **ideal lowpass filter** $H_r(j\omega)$ isolates the central replica centered at $\omega = 0$:

$$
H_r(j\omega) = \begin{cases} T, & \vert \omega \vert \leq \omega_c \\ 0, & \vert \omega \vert > \omega_c \end{cases}
$$

> The gain $T$ is critical — it compensates for the $1/T$ scaling introduced during sampling.

### Step 4: Time-Domain Output
The impulse response of this ideal filter is the **sinc function**:

$$
h_r(t) = \frac{T}{2\pi} \int_{-\omega_c}^{\omega_c} e^{j\omega t}\, d\omega = \frac{\sin(\omega_c t)}{\omega_c t} = \frac{\sin(\pi t / T)}{\pi t / T}
$$

The output is the convolution of $x_s(t)$ with $h_r(t)$:

$$
x_r(t) = x_s(t) * h_r(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT)\, \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t - nT}{T}\right)
$$

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

| Step | Reason |
|------|--------|
| Impulse train multiplication | Converts continuous signal into weighted impulses carrying amplitude information |
| Frequency-domain replication | Convolution with impulse train shifts spectrum by $k\omega_s$ |
| Lowpass filtering | Extracts baseband copy of original spectrum (since replicas are non-overlapping) |
| Sinc interpolation | Each shifted sinc "lights up" exactly at sample points, summing to give $x(t)$ |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table compiles every high-yield formula for Module 3 reconstruction problems. Memorize these — they appear in **every** KTU End Semester Examination.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|
| 1 | Sampling period | $T = 1/f_s$ | seconds |
| 2 | Nyquist rate | $f_s \geq 2f_m$ | Hz |
| 3 | Sampled signal | $x_s(t) = \sum x(nT)\delta(t-nT)$ | impulses |
| 4 | Spectrum of sampled signal | $X_s(j\omega) = \frac{1}{T}\sum X(j(\omega-k\omega_s))$ | rad/s |
| 5 | Reconstruct filter gain | $H_r(j\omega) = T$ for $\vert\omega\vert \leq \omega_c$ | magnitude |
| 6 | Cutoff frequency | $\omega_c = \pi/T$ | rad/s |
| 7 | Impulse response of filter | $h_r(t) = \text{sinc}(t/T)$ | dimensionless |
| 8 | Interpolation formula | $x(t) = \sum x(nT)\,\text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t-nT}{T}\right)$ | exact recovery |
| 9 | Aliasing condition | $f_s < 2f_m$ | spectrum overlap |
| 10 | Zero-order hold output | $x_0(t) = \sum x(nT)\,\text{rect}\!\left(\frac{t-nT-T/2}{T}\right)$ | staircase approx |

> Use `\vert` for absolute value notation in your own handwritten answer sheets to avoid notation confusion.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Signal reconstruction is the backbone of:
- **Digital Audio (CDs, MP3, FLAC)**: DAC chips contain reconstruction filters
- **Digital Video Playback**: Frame interpolation
- **Communication Receivers**: Demodulation of pulse-modulated signals
- **Medical Imaging**: MRI/CT scan reconstruction from k-space samples
- **Software-Defined Radio**: Bridging digital samples back to RF analog signals

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: The Whittaker–Shannon Interpolation Formula

**Starting point**: An impulse-sampled signal $x_s(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT)\,\delta(t - nT)$.

**Step 1 — Apply Fourier Transform to the sampled signal**.

Using the frequency-shifting property of impulses, the Fourier Transform of $x_s(t)$ is:

$$
X_s(j\omega) = \mathcal{F}\{x_s(t)\} = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT)\, e^{-j\omega nT}
$$

This is a **periodic function** of $\omega$ with period $\omega_s = 2\pi/T$.

**Step 2 — Pass through the ideal lowpass reconstruction filter** $H_r(j\omega)$.

$$
H_r(j\omega) = \begin{cases} T, & \vert\omega\vert \leq \omega_c = \pi/T \\ 0, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

The output spectrum is:

$$
X_r(j\omega) = X_s(j\omega) \cdot H_r(j\omega) = T \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT)\, e^{-j\omega nT}, \quad \vert\omega\vert \leq \pi/T
$$

**Step 3 — Inverse Fourier Transform to obtain $x_r(t)$**.

$$
x_r(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi/T}^{\pi/T} X_r(j\omega)\, e^{j\omega t}\, d\omega
$$

Substituting:

$$
x_r(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi} \int_{-\pi/T}^{\pi/T} T \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT)\, e^{-j\omega nT}\, e^{j\omega t}\, d\omega
$$

**Step 4 — Interchange sum and integral** (justified by uniform convergence):

$$
x_r(t) = \frac{T}{2\pi} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT) \int_{-\pi/T}^{\pi/T} e^{j\omega(t - nT)}\, d\omega
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the integral**.

$$
\int_{-\pi/T}^{\pi/T} e^{j\omega(t - nT)}\, d\omega = \left[\frac{e^{j\omega(t - nT)}}{j(t - nT)}\right]_{-\pi/T}^{\pi/T} = \frac{2\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi}{T}(t - nT)\right)}{t - nT}
$$

**Step 6 — Substitute back and simplify**.

$$
x_r(t) = \frac{T}{2\pi} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT) \cdot \frac{2\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi(t - nT)}{T}\right)}{t - nT}
$$

Cancel $2$'s:

$$
x_r(t) = \frac{T}{\pi} \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT) \cdot \frac{\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi(t - nT)}{T}\right)}{t - nT}
$$

Rearrange by pulling $1/T$ inside the sine (using $\sin(\pi u) = \pi \sin(\pi u)/\pi$ ):

$$
x_r(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT) \cdot \frac{\sin\!\left(\frac{\pi(t - nT)}{T}\right)}{\frac{\pi(t - nT)}{T}}
$$

**Final Result**:

$$
\boxed{\,x_r(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT)\, \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t - nT}{T}\right)\,}
$$

Since we assumed the signal is properly bandlimited ($f_s \geq 2f_m$), $x_r(t) = x(t)$ exactly.

## 3.2 Worked Analytical Example (KTU Board Style)

**Problem**: A signal $x(t) = 5\cos(100\pi t)$ is sampled at $f_s = 400$ Hz. Determine whether the original signal can be reconstructed perfectly, and find the reconstruction.

**Step 1 — Identify signal frequency**.

The angular frequency is $\omega_0 = 100\pi$ rad/s, so $f_0 = 50$ Hz.

**Step 2 — Check Nyquist criterion**.

Nyquist rate: $f_N = 2f_0 = 100$ Hz. Given $f_s = 400$ Hz $\geq 100$ Hz. ✓ Reconstruction is possible.

**Step 3 — Compute sampling period**.

$$
T = \frac{1}{f_s} = \frac{1}{400} = 2.5 \text{ ms}
$$

**Step 4 — Write the sample values**.

$$
x(nT) = 5\cos(100\pi nT) = 5\cos\!\left(100\pi \cdot \frac{n}{400}\right) = 5\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi n}{4}\right)
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the interpolation formula**.

$$
x(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} 5\cos\!\left(\frac{\pi n}{4}\right) \text{sinc}\!\left(400(t - 0.0025n)\right)
$$

In practice, the original sinusoid is recovered perfectly because the sample values $5\cos(\pi n/4)$ correspond exactly to $5\cos(100\pi t)$ at the sampling instants, and the sinc basis functions sum to produce the original sinusoid everywhere.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key takeaway**: For a single sinusoid sampled well above Nyquist, the samples are sufficient to define the original sinusoid completely. The sinc interpolation sum converges back to the original continuous waveform.

## 3.3 Practical Reconstruction — Zero-Order Hold (ZOH)

In real systems, the ideal lowpass filter is **not realizable** (it is non-causal and infinite-duration). Engineers use the **Zero-Order Hold**, which holds each sample value constant for one sampling interval:

$$
h_{ZOH}(t) = \frac{1}{T}\left[u(t) - u(t - T)\right]
$$

The reconstructed output is a **staircase approximation**. The ZOH transfer function is:

$$
H_{ZOH}(j\omega) = \frac{1 - e^{-j\omega T}}{j\omega T} = e^{-j\omega T/2} \cdot \text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{\omega T}{2\pi}\right)
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The ZOH introduces a **sinc-shaped droop** in the frequency response. To compensate, a practical reconstruction chain uses a ZOH followed by an **equalization filter** that flattens the response up to $\omega_c$.

## 3.4 The Aliasing Problem (Anti-Reconstruction Condition)

If $f_s < 2f_m$, spectral replicas overlap. After lowpass filtering, the output is **not** $x(t)$ but a corrupted, lower-frequency version. This is **aliasing**.

> [!WARNING]
> Aliasing is **irreversible** in the analog domain. The only cure is **prevention** — sample at or above $2f_m$, and use an **anti-aliasing filter** (analog lowpass) *before* sampling to ensure the input is bandlimited.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Sampling–Reconstruction System Block Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram shows the complete signal path from analog input to reconstructed analog output, including the critical anti-aliasing and reconstruction filters.

```mermaid

    A[Analog Input x of t] --> B[Anti Aliasing Lowpass Filter]
    B --> C[Sampler with Impulse Train delta of t]
    C --> D[Sampled Signal x sub s of t]
    D --> E[Reconstruction Lowpass Filter H sub r]
    E --> F[Reconstructed Output x hat of t]
    G[Impulse Train Generator] -->|period T| C
    H[Reference Clock T] --> G
    H --> C
    style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px

    style F fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style G fill:#e8daef,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

```mermaid

    S2[Sampled Spectrum X sub s of j omega replicas at k omega sub s] --> M2

```

```mermaid
    P4[Sample at t = 3T amplitude x 3] --> S
    P5[Sample at t = minus T amplitude x minus 1] --> S

```

## 4.4 Aliasing vs. No-Aliasing Decision Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Given signal with max freq f sub m and sampling rate f sub s] --> B{Is f sub s greater than or equal to 2 f sub m ?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Replicas do NOT overlap]
    B -- No --> D[Replicas overlap ALIASING OCCURS]
    C --> E[Apply Ideal LPF cutoff at pi over T]
    E --> F[PERFECT RECONSTRUCTION x hat of t equals x of t]
    D --> G[LPF output is CORRUPTED sum of overlapped spectra]
    G --> H[Cannot recover original NO PERFECT RECONSTRUCTION]
```
