```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the Nyquist criterion for perfect signal reconstruction.**

**Model Answer**:
A bandlimited signal $x(t)$ with maximum frequency $f_m$ Hz can be perfectly reconstructed from its samples if and only if the sampling frequency satisfies $f_s \geq 2f_m$. The minimum allowed sampling rate, $f_N = 2f_m$, is called the **Nyquist Rate**. When $f_s < 2f_m$, aliasing occurs and perfect reconstruction is impossible regardless of the filter used.

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**What is the role of a reconstruction filter in signal reconstruction?**

**Model Answer**:
The reconstruction filter is an **ideal lowpass filter** with gain $T$ and cutoff frequency $\omega_c = \pi/T$. Its role is to extract the baseband replica of the original signal's spectrum from the periodic, replicated spectrum produced by sampling. In the time domain, its impulse response is a sinc function, and convolving it with the sampled signal yields the continuous reconstructed output $x(t) = \sum x(nT)\text{sinc}((t-nT)/T)$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for reconstructing the original signal $x(t)$ from its samples $x(nT)$ using an ideal lowpass filter. Show all intermediate steps. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A signal $x(t) = 3\cos(200\pi t) + 2\sin(400\pi t)$ is sampled at $f_s = 500$ Hz. Determine:
   (i) The Nyquist rate.
   (ii) Whether perfect reconstruction is possible.
   (iii) The values of the first four samples $x(0), x(T), x(2T), x(3T)$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

Starting with the impulse-sampled signal:

$$
x_s(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT)\,\delta(t - nT)
$$

Taking the Fourier Transform:

$$
X_s(j\omega) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT)\, e^{-j\omega nT}
$$

Passing through ideal LPF $H_r(j\omega) = T$ for $\vert\omega\vert \leq \pi/T$:

$$
X_r(j\omega) = T \sum_{n} x(nT)\, e^{-j\omega nT}, \quad \vert\omega\vert \leq \pi/T
$$

Inverse Fourier Transform:

$$
x_r(t) = \frac{1}{2\pi}\int_{-\pi/T}^{\pi/T} T \sum_n x(nT)\, e^{j\omega(t-nT)}\, d\omega
$$

Interchange sum and integral:

$$
x_r(t) = \frac{T}{2\pi}\sum_n x(nT) \int_{-\pi/T}^{\pi/T} e^{j\omega(t-nT)}\, d\omega
$$

Evaluating the integral yields $2\sin(\pi(t-nT)/T)/(t-nT)$. Substituting and simplifying:

$$
\boxed{x_r(t) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x(nT)\,\text{sinc}\!\left(\frac{t-nT}{T}\right)}
$$

For a bandlimited signal with $f_s \geq 2f_m$, $x_r(t) = x(t)$ exactly.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**(i)** Maximum frequency: from the second term, $\omega = 400\pi$ rad/s $\Rightarrow f_2 = 200$ Hz. From the first term, $f_1 = 100$ Hz. So $f_m = 200$ Hz.

**Nyquist rate**: $f_N = 2f_m = 400$ Hz.

**(ii)** Given $f_s = 500$ Hz $> 400$ Hz. ✓ **Perfect reconstruction IS possible.**

**(iii)** Sampling period: $T = 1/500 = 0.002$ s. Substitute $t = nT$:

$$
x(nT) = 3\cos(200\pi \cdot n \cdot 0.002) + 2\sin(400\pi \cdot n \cdot 0.002)
$$

$$
x(nT) = 3\cos(0.4\pi n) + 2\sin(0.8\pi n)
$$

| $n$ | $x(nT)$ |
|-----|---------|
| 0 | $3\cos(0) + 2\sin(0) = 3 + 0 = 3$ |
| 1 | $3\cos(0.4\pi) + 2\sin(0.8\pi) = 3(0.309) + 2(0.951) = 0.927 + 1.902 = 2.829$ |
| 2 | $3\cos(0.8\pi) + 2\sin(1.6\pi) = 3(-0.809) + 2(-0.951) = -2.427 - 1.902 = -4.329$ |
| 3 | $3\cos(1.2\pi) + 2\sin(2.4\pi) = 3(-0.809) + 2(0.588) = -2.427 + 1.176 = -1.251$ |

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of aliasing. A signal $x(t) = \cos(500\pi t)$ is sampled at $f_s = 300$ Hz. What frequency will be observed in the reconstructed signal? Show your work. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat block diagram and frequency spectrum, explain the complete process of sampling and reconstruction of a bandlimited signal. Mention the role of anti-aliasing and reconstruction filters. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

Aliasing occurs when the sampling rate is below the Nyquist rate ($f_s < 2f_m$), causing spectral replicas to overlap and making perfect reconstruction impossible.

For $x(t) = \cos(500\pi t)$: $f_0 = 250$ Hz, so $f_m = 250$ Hz and $f_N = 500$ Hz.

Given $f_s = 300$ Hz $< 500$ Hz → **aliasing will occur**.

The aliased frequency is computed as:

$$
f_{\text{alias}} = \vert f_0 - f_s \vert = \vert 250 - 300 \vert = 50 \text{ Hz}
$$

So the reconstructed signal will appear as $\cos(2\pi \cdot 50 \cdot t) = \cos(100\pi t)$ — a much lower frequency that was **not** in the original signal.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

Refer to the block diagrams in **Section 4.1** and **Section 4.2** of these notes.

**Pipeline Summary**:
1. **Anti-aliasing filter** (analog LPF, cutoff $f_m$): Ensures input is strictly bandlimited before sampling.
2. **Sampler**: Multiplies by impulse train $\delta_T(t)$, producing $x_s(t)$ with periodic spectrum.
3. **Reconstruction filter** (LPF, cutoff $f_s/2$): Extracts baseband replica, recovering $x(t)$ via sinc interpolation.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls**:
> 1. **Forgetting the gain factor $T$** in the reconstruction filter — this is a 2-mark deduction. The filter magnitude is $T$, not $1$.
> 2. **Confusing the sinc normalization** — some students write $\sin(x)/x$ instead of $\sin(\pi x)/(\pi x)$. KTU expects the **normalized** form $\text{sinc}(x) = \sin(\pi x)/(\pi x)$.
> 3. **Forgetting the anti-aliasing filter** in block diagrams — examiners deduct at least 1 mark.
> 4. **Stating the Nyquist rate as $f_s \geq f_m$** — this is wrong. It is $f_s \geq 2f_m$.
> 5. **Missing the aliasing direction check**: when $f_s < 2f_m$, students must compute the *folded* frequency, not just declare "aliasing exists."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Signal Reconstruction (Module 3)**

- ✅ Reconstruction requires the signal to be **bandlimited** AND sampled at **$f_s \geq 2f_m$**.
- ✅ The reconstruction filter is an **ideal LPF** with gain $T$ and cutoff $\omega_c = \pi/T$.
- ✅ The interpolation formula is $x(t) = \sum x(nT)\text{sinc}((t-nT)/T)$ — this is the **Whittaker–Shannon** formula.
- ✅ Use the **normalized** sinc: $\text{sinc}(x) = \sin(\pi x)/(\pi x)$, with $\text{sinc}(0) = 1$ and $\text{sinc}(\text{integer}) = 0$.
- ✅ **Sampled spectrum** $X_s(j\omega)$ is periodic with period $\omega_s = 2\pi/T$ and amplitude scaled by $1/T$.
- ✅ **Aliasing** is irreversible — prevent it with an **anti-aliasing filter** *before* sampling.
- ✅ The **ZOH** is a practical (non-ideal) reconstruction method producing a staircase output with sinc droop.
- ✅ Compute **folded/aliased frequency** as $f_{\text{alias}} = \vert f - kf_s \vert$ where $k = \text{round}(f/f_s)$.
- ✅ For a signal with components at $f_1, f_2, \ldots$, the overall $f_m = \max(f_i)$ determines the Nyquist rate.
- ✅ Ideal LPF is **non-causal** — practical systems use ZOH + equalizer, or causal approximations (Butterworth, etc.).
- ✅ For multiple sinusoids, **sum the samples** of each component — linearity applies throughout.
- ✅ **Always draw spectra** in KTU answers — block diagrams + frequency sketches fetch easy full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Z-transform: stability and causality conditions using ROC; characterization of difference equations using Z-transform
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEOT522 - INTRODUCTION TO SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Z-transform: stability and causality conditions using ROC; characterization of difference equations using Z-transform # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Region of Convergence (ROC): Formal Definition The **Region of Convergence (ROC)** of the Z-transform of a sequence $x[n]$ is defined as the set of all complex values $z = re^{j\omega}$ (or equivalently in the $z$-plane) for which the Z-transform summation **converges to a finite value**. Mathematically, the Z-transform is defined as: $$X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] \, z^{-n}$$ The ROC is the set: $$\text{ROC} = \{ z \in \mathbb{C} : \left\vert \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] z^{-n} \right\vert < \infty \}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Two different sequences can have the **same algebraic expression** for $X(z)$ but **different ROCs**. Therefore, the ROC is an inseparable part of the complete specification of the Z-transform. Always quote ROC alongside $X(z)$ in KTU exams. ## 1.2 Stability and Causality via ROC — Formal Definitions **Stability (BIBO — Bounded-Input Bounded-Output):** A discrete-time LTI system is **BIBO stable** if and only if its impulse response $h[n]$ is absolutely summable: $$\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[n] \vert < \infty$$ In terms of ROC: A discrete-time LTI system is stable **if and only if the ROC of its system function $H(z)$ includes the unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$**. **Causality:** A discrete-time LTI system is **causal** if its impulse response $h[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$ (i.e., the output depends only on present and past inputs). In terms of ROC: A causal LTI system is one whose ROC is the **exterior of a circle**, including the point $z = \infty$ (i.e., $\vert z \vert > r_{max}$). > [!NOTE] > **Equivalent Pole Condition for Stability:** Since the ROC is bounded by poles, $H(z)$ is stable **iff all its poles lie strictly inside the unit circle**, i.e., $\vert p_k \vert < 1$ for every pole $p_k$. Poles exactly on the unit circle (e.g., $\vert z \vert = 1$) make the system **marginally unstable / critically stable**. ## 1.3 Characterization of Difference Equations Using Z-Transform A general **Linear Constant-Coefficient Difference Equation (LCCDE)** of order $N$ relating input $x[n]$ and output $y[n]$ is: $$\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \, y[n-k] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, x[n-k]$$ Applying the Z-transform (assuming zero initial conditions) to both sides yields: $$\left( \sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \, z^{-k} \right) Y(z) = \left( \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, z^{-k} \right) X(z)$$ Therefore, the **Transfer Function** (or system function) is: $$H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k z^{-k}}{\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k z^{-k}}$$ ## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — Plain-English Explanation > [!TIP] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Water Tank and Filter System":** > Think of the Z-transform as a description of a **water filtration plant**. > - **Poles** are like "blockages" in the pipe — water pressure (energy) builds up there. > - **Zeros** are like "leaks" — they drain energy away. > - **The Unit Circle** $\vert z \vert = 1$ is like the **operating pressure** of the system. > - **Stability:** If all blockages (poles) are *inside* the operating pressure ring, the system never explodes — it's stable. If a blockage sits *on* the ring, the system oscillates forever (marginally stable). > - **Causality:** A causal system is one where water flow depends only on what came *before* — the ROC is the "outside region" (you only need to look at future pressures cautiously, but the system reacts to past inputs). ## 1.5 KTU Board Examination Pointers > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mandatory Board Requirements:** > - Always specify ROC **with the inequality form** (e.g., $\vert z \vert > 2$) — partial answers lose marks. > - State clearly whether the system is **stable**, **causal**, **both**, or **neither**. > - For difference equations, **always assume zero initial conditions** unless stated otherwise (KTU standard). > - The unit step response, impulse response, and system behavior must be tied back to pole-zero locations. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 General Properties of the ROC The ROC has the following fundamental properties (KTU frequently tests these): 1. **The ROC is a ring or annulus in the $z$-plane:** $r_2 < \vert z \vert < r_1$, where $r_2$ and $r_1$ are the magnitudes of the poles nearest and farthest from the origin that bound the ROC. 2. **ROC cannot include any pole** of $X(z)$ (since $X(z)$ does not converge at poles). 3. **If $x[n]$ is of finite duration (and right-sided or left-sided only),** the ROC is the entire $z$-plane except possibly $z=0$ or $z=\infty$. 4. **If $x[n]$ is right-sided**, the ROC extends outward from the outermost finite pole to infinity: $\vert z \vert > r_{max}$. 5. **If $x[n]$ is left-sided**, the ROC extends inward from the innermost nonzero pole to the origin: $\vert z \vert < r_{min}$. 6. **If $x[n]$ is two-sided**, the ROC is a ring (annulus): $r_2 < \vert z \vert < r_1$. 7. **A causal sequence** has ROC of the form $\vert z \vert > r_{max}$ (exterior of a circle, includes $\infty$). ## 2.2 Stability and Causality — The ROC Logic Map The relationship between ROC, causality, and stability can be summarized in three rules (memorize these): | Property | Condition on ROC | Condition on Poles | |----------|------------------|---------------------| | **Causality** | ROC is exterior of circle: $\vert z \vert > r_{max}$ | $H(z)$ is a **proper rational function** (no more zeros than poles) | | **Stability** | ROC **includes** the unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$ | All poles strictly inside unit circle: $\vert p_k \vert < 1$ | | **Causal + Stable** | ROC: $\vert z \vert > r_{max}$ with $r_{max} < 1$ | All poles strictly inside unit circle AND ROC includes $\infty$ | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Combined Condition:** A system is **stable AND causal** if and only if **all poles lie strictly inside the unit circle** ($\vert p_k \vert < 1$). This is the most commonly tested KTU condition. ## 2.3 Characterization of LCCDE — Step-by-Step Logic **Step 1 — Write the LCCDE** in standard form: $$\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k y[n-k] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k x[n-k]$$ **Step 2 — Apply Z-transform** term-by-term using the **Time-Shift Property**: $$\mathcal{Z}\{x[n-k]\} = z^{-k} X(z) \quad \text{(for causal shifts, zero initial conditions)}$$ **Step 3 — Rearrange** to isolate $H(z) = Y(z)/X(z)$: $$H(z) = \frac{\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k z^{-k}}{\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k z^{-k}}$$ **Step 4 — Factorize numerator and denominator** into pole-zero form: $$H(z) = \frac{b_0}{a_0} \cdot \frac{\prod_{i=1}^{M}(1 - q_i z^{-1})}{\prod_{j=1}^{N}(1 - p_j z^{-1})}$$ where $q_i$ are zeros and $p_j$ are poles. **Step 5 — Determine ROC** based on pole magnitudes, and decide causality/stability. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Master Reference Table > [!NOTE] > **The following table consolidates every high-yield formula for this KTU topic. Use \mid or \vert for absolute values — never \vert inside vertical pipe table separators.** | # | Concept | Mathematical Expression | Notes / Conditions | |---|---------|------------------------|--------------------| | 1 | Bilateral Z-transform | $X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] z^{-n}$ | Convergence required | | 2 | Unilateral (right-sided) Z-transform | $X(z) = \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} x[n] z^{-n}$ | ROC: $\mid z \mid > r_{max}$ | | 3 | Time-shift property | $\mathcal{Z}\{x[n-k]\} = z^{-k} X(z)$ | Zero initial conditions | | 4 | Linearity | $\mathcal{Z}\{a x[n] + b y[n]\} = a X(z) + b Y(z)$ | Always valid | | 5 | Multiplication by $a^n$ | $\mathcal{Z}\{a^n x[n]\} = X(z/a)$ | Scaling in $z$ | | 6 | Time reversal | $\mathcal{Z}\{x[-n]\} = X(1/z)$ | ROC flips | | 7 | Convolution | $\mathcal{Z}\{x[n] * h[n]\} = X(z) H(z)$ | ROC: intersection | | 8 | Initial value theorem | $x[0] = \lim_{z \to \infty} X(z)$ | Valid only for causal $x[n]$ | | 9 | Final value theorem | $\lim_{n \to \infty} x[n] = \lim_{z \to 1} (z-1) X(z)$ | Poles of $(z-1)X(z)$ inside unit circle | | 10 | Transfer function from LCCDE | $H(z) = \dfrac{\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k z^{-k}}{\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k z^{-k}}$ | Zero ICs assumed | | 11 | Causal LTI ROC | $\text{ROC}: \mid z \mid > \max \mid p_k \mid$ | Includes $z = \infty$ | | 12 | Stable LTI ROC | $\text{ROC} \supseteq \{\mid z \mid = 1\}$ | Unit circle inside ROC | | 13 | Causal + Stable condition | All poles satisfy $\mid p_k \mid < 1$ | Equivalent to $H(z)$ stable-causal | | 14 | Frequency response from $H(z)$ | $H(e^{j\omega}) = H(z) \big\vert_{z = e^{j\omega}}$ | Valid only if unit circle in ROC | | 15 | Impulse response from $H(z)$ | $h[n] = \mathcal{Z}^{-1}\{H(z)\}$ | Partial fraction expansion | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications > [!TIP] > **Why does this matter in industry?** > - **Digital Filter Design:** IIR and FIR filters are designed by placing poles and zeros inside/outside/around the unit circle. > - **Audio Processing:** Equalizers use pole-zero patterns to shape frequency response. > - **Control Systems:** Stability analysis via pole locations inside the unit circle (analogous to the left-half $s$-plane for continuous systems). > - **Biomedical Signal Processing:** ECG/EEG filtering requires causal + stable systems to be implementable in real-time. > - **Communication Receivers:** Matched filters and adaptive filters rely heavily on $H(z)$ characterization. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Transfer Function of a General LCCDE **Starting Point:** A general $N$-th order Linear Constant-Coefficient Difference Equation: $$\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k y[n-k] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k x[n-k]$$ **Derivation Step 1 — Apply the Z-transform** to both sides. Using the linearity property and the time-shift property $\mathcal{Z}\{f[n-k]\} = z^{-k} F(z)$ (valid for zero initial conditions): $$\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k \, z^{-k} Y(z) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k \, z^{-k} X(z)$$ **Derivation Step 2 — Group the $Y(z)$ and $X(z)$ terms:** $$\left( a_0 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2} + \cdots + a_N z^{-N} \right) Y(z) = \left( b_0 + b_1 z^{-1} + b_2 z^{-2} + \cdots + b_M z^{-M} \right) X(z)$$ **Derivation Step 3 — Solve for the ratio $Y(z)/X(z)$ — this is the transfer function $H(z)$:** $$H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \frac{b_0 + b_1 z^{-1} + b_2 z^{-2} + \cdots + b_M z^{-M}}{a_0 + a_1 z^{-1} + a_2 z^{-2} + \cdots + a_N z^{-N}}$$ **Derivation Step 4 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $z^{\max(M,N)}$** to obtain the polynomial-in-$z$ form (KTU sometimes prefers this): $$H(z) = \frac{b_0 z^{N} + b_1 z^{N-1} + \cdots + b_M z^{N-M}}{a_0 z^{N} + a_1 z^{N-1} + \cdots + a_N}$$ ## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Stability & Causality from $H(z)$ **Problem:** Consider the system with transfer function: $$H(z) = \frac{1}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}} + \frac{1}{1 - 2 z^{-1}}$$ Determine whether each term corresponds to a causal, stable, or causal+stable system. **Step 1 — Identify the poles** of each term: $$\text{Term 1: pole at } p_1 = 0.5 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mid p_1 \mid = 0.5 < 1$$ $$\text{Term 2: pole at } p_2 = 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mid p_2 \mid = 2 > 1$$ **Step 2 — Determine the ROC for each term (causal case):** $$\text{Term 1 ROC: } \mid z \mid > 0.5 \quad \text{(includes unit circle, so causal+stable)}$$ $$\text{Term 2 ROC: } \mid z \mid > 2 \quad \text{(excludes unit circle, so causal but UNSTABLE)}$$ **Step 3 — Combined system:** If the overall ROC is the intersection: $\mid z \mid > 2$ (the more restrictive region). **Conclusion:** The combined system is **causal but NOT stable** (because the ROC does not include $\mid z \vert = 1$). ## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Characterizing an LCCDE **Problem:** A discrete system is described by the difference equation: $$y[n] - \frac{3}{4} y[n-1] + \frac{1}{8} y[n-2] = 2 x[n]$$ Find $H(z)$, its ROC, pole-zero plot, and determine stability and causality. **Step 1 — Apply Z-transform** with zero initial conditions: $$Y(z) - \frac{3}{4} z^{-1} Y(z) + \frac{1}{8} z^{-2} Y(z) = 2 X(z)$$ **Step 2 — Factor out $Y(z)$:** $$Y(z) \left( 1 - \frac{3}{4} z^{-1} + \frac{1}{8} z^{-2} \right) = 2 X(z)$$ **Step 3 — Solve for $H(z) = Y(z)/X(z)$:** $$H(z) = \frac{2}{1 - \frac{3}{4} z^{-1} + \frac{1}{8} z^{-2}}$$ **Step 4 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $z^2$** to get polynomial form: $$H(z) = \frac{2 z^2}{z^2 - \frac{3}{4} z + \frac{1}{8}}$$ **Step 5 — Find the poles** by solving the denominator polynomial: $$z^2 - \frac{3}{4} z + \frac{1}{8} = 0$$ $$z = \frac{\frac{3}{4} \pm \sqrt{\frac{9}{16} - \frac{4}{8}}}{2} = \frac{\frac{3}{4} \pm \sqrt{\frac{9}{16} - \frac{8}{16}}}{2}$$ $$z = \frac{\frac{3}{4} \pm \sqrt{\frac{1}{16}}}{2} = \frac{\frac{3}{4} \pm \frac{1}{4}}{2}$$ $$p_1 = \frac{1}{2}, \quad p_2 = \frac{1}{4}$$ **Step 6 — Determine the ROC for the causal system** (taking the outermost pole): $$\text{ROC: } \mid z \mid > \frac{1}{2}$$ **Step 7 — Check stability:** $$\mid p_1 \mid = \frac{1}{2} < 1 \quad \text{and} \quad \mid p_2 \mid = \frac{1}{4} < 1$$ Both poles are strictly inside the unit circle. Since the ROC ($\mid z \mid > 1/2$) **includes the unit circle**, the system is **stable**. **Step 8 — Check causality:** Since the ROC is $\mid z \mid > 1/2$ (exterior of a circle, includes $\infty$), the system is **causal**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Verdict:** This system is **BOTH causal and stable**. ## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Impulse Response from Difference Equation **Problem:** Find $h[n]$ for the system described by: $$y[n] - \frac{1}{2} y[n-1] = x[n]$$ **Step 1 — Z-transform with zero ICs:** $$Y(z) \left(1 - \frac{1}{2} z^{-1}\right) = X(z) \quad \Rightarrow \quad H(z) = \frac{1}{1 - \frac{1}{2} z^{-1}}$$ **Step 2 — Use the standard pair** $\mathcal{Z}\{a^n u[n]\} = \dfrac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}, \quad \text{ROC}: \mid z \mid > \mid a \mid$: $$h[n] = \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^n u[n], \quad \text{ROC: } \mid z \mid > \frac{1}{2}$$ **Step 3 — Verification of causality and stability:** - Causal: ROC is $\mid z \mid > 1/2$, includes $\infty$. ✓ - Stable: $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^n = \dfrac{1}{1-1/2} = 2 < \infty$. ✓ ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Stability & Causality Decision Flow | $H(z)$ Behavior | ROC | Poles Location | Causal? | Stable? | |-----------------|-----|----------------|---------|---------| | Right-sided, exterior ROC | $\mid z \mid > 1$ | All $\mid p_k \mid < 1$ | ✓ | ✓ | | Right-sided, exterior ROC | $\mid z \mid > 2$ | Includes $p = 2$ | ✓ | ✗ | | Left-sided, interior ROC | $\mid z \mid < 1$ | All $\mid p_k \mid > 1$ | ✗ | ✓ | | Two-sided ROC | $\frac{1}{2} < \mid z \mid < 2$ | Poles span range | ✗ | ✓ | | Pole on unit circle | $p = e^{j\pi/4}$ | $\mid p \mid = 1$ | ✓ | ✗ (marginally unstable) | | Multiple poles at origin | $1/z^2$ | $\mid p \mid = 0$ | ✓ | ✓ (degenerate) | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 ROC Logic Flow Diagram for Stability & Causality Classification ```mermaid flowchart TD startA([Start with H z and ROC]) --> identifyPoles{Identify All Poles
of H z} identifyPoles --> checkCausal{Causal Question
Is ROC of form
abs z greater than r max} checkCausal -->|Yes| causalYes[System is CAUSAL] checkCausal -->|No| causalNo[System is NON CAUSAL] checkCausal -->|Yes| checkStable{Stable Question
Does ROC include
unit circle abs z equals 1} checkCausal -->|Yes| checkPoles{Are all poles
strictly inside
unit circle} checkStable -->|Yes| stableYes[System is STABLE] checkStable -->|No| stableNo[System is UNSTABLE] checkPoles -->|Yes| bothYes[BOTH Causal AND Stable] checkPoles -->|No| onlyCausal[Only Causal
NOT Stable] causalYes --> combineAll([Combine Verdict
Stable Causal BIBO Check]) stableYes --> combineAll stableNo --> combineAll causalNo --> checkStable ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram: LCCDE Realization Using Direct Form II ```mermaid flowchart LR inputNode[X z] --> sumNodeA((Sum Node)) fb1[Feedback Path
a1 z power negative 1] --> sumNodeA fb2[Feedback Path
a2 z power negative 2] --> sumNodeA sumNodeA --> delayNodeA[Delay Element z power negative 1] delayNodeA --> delayNodeB[Delay Element z power negative 2] delayNodeA --> fw1[Feedforward b1] delayNodeB --> fw2[Feedforward b2] fw1 --> sumNodeB((Sum Node)) fw2 --> sumNodeB sumNodeA --> fw0[Feedforward b0] fw0 --> sumNodeB sumNodeB --> outputNode([Y z H z times X z]) ``` ## 4.3 Pole-Zero Plot — Schematic Interpretation ```mermaid flowchart LR regionTitle([Pole Zero Plot for H z equals 1 over 1 minus 0.5 z power negative 1]) zPlane((z Plane
Im Axis vs Re Axis)) unitCircle((Unit Circle
abs z equals 1)) pole1((Pole at z equals 0.5)) rocRegion[ROC Region
Outside abs z equals 0.5
Includes Unit Circle] zPlane --- unitCircle zPlane --- pole1 zPlane --- rocRegion unitCircle --> stableLabel[STABLE: Unit Circle Inside ROC] pole1 --> poleLabel[Pole inside unit circle] rocRegion --> causalLabel[CAUSAL: ROC exterior of circle] ``` ## 4.4 Transformation Pathway Diagram: Difference Equation to Transfer Function ```mermaid flowchart TD startNode([LCCDE Given
sum a k y n minus k equals sum b k x n minus k]) --> applyZ[Apply Z Transform
Use Time Shift Property
Zero Initial Conditions] applyZ --> formA[Form Sum a k z power negative k times Y z] applyZ --> formB[Form Sum b k z power negative k times X z] formA --> equate[Equate and Rearrange] formB --> equate equate --> isolate[Isolate Y z over X z] isolate --> transferFn[H z equals Y z over X z] transferFn --> factorStep[Factorize into
Pole Zero Form] factorStep --> plotPZ[Plot Poles and Zeros
in z Plane] plotPZ --> determineROC[Determine ROC
based on Pole Magnitudes] determineROC --> classifyNode{Final Classification} classifyNode --> stableCausal[BOTH Stable and Causal
if all poles inside unit circle
and ROC is exterior] classifyNode --> onlyCausal2[Only Causal
if ROC exterior but unit circle excluded] classifyNode --> onlyStable2[Only Stable
if ROC includes unit circle but not exterior] classifyNode --> neither[Neither
if ROC does not include unit circle
and is not exterior] ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Q:** Define the Region of Convergence (ROC) of the Z-transform. State any **three properties** of the ROC. **Model Answer:** The **Region of Convergence (ROC)** of the Z-transform $X(z) = \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} x[n] z^{-n}$ is the set of all complex values of $z$ for which the summation converges to a finite value. **Three Properties of ROC:** 1. The ROC is a **ring (annulus)** in the $z$-plane of the form $r_2 < \mid z \mid < r_1$, where $r_2$ and $r_1$ are determined by the poles. 2. The ROC **does not contain any pole** of $X(z)$. 3. A **causal sequence** has its ROC as the **exterior of a circle**, i.e., $\mid z \mid > r_{max}$, including $z = \infty$. --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Q:** A causal LTI system has the system function $H(z) = \dfrac{z}{z - 0.5}$. Is the system stable? Justify using ROC. **Model Answer:** The pole of $H(z)$ is at $z = 0.5$. Since the system is causal, its ROC must be the **exterior of the outermost pole**: $$\text{ROC: } \mid z \mid > 0.5$$ The unit circle $\mid z \mid = 1$ satisfies $1 > 0.5$, so **the unit circle lies inside the ROC**. Equivalently, the pole magnitude $\mid 0.5 \mid = 0.5 < 1$, i.e., the pole is **inside the unit circle**. **Therefore, the system is STABLE.** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part B question has two sub-parts of 7 marks each, OR an internal choice between two full 14-mark questions. We provide both alternatives. --- ### **Part B — Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` (14 Marks) **(a)** Define the **Region of Convergence** for the Z-transform. Explain the conditions under which a discrete-time LTI system is **stable** and **causal** in terms of its ROC. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Consider the discrete-time system characterized by the difference equation: $$y[n] = 0.5 y[n-1] + x[n] + 0.5 x[n-1]$$ Determine the transfer function $H(z)$, find the impulse response $h[n]$, and check whether the system is **stable** and **causal**. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Solution to Question A (a):** **Definition of ROC:** The ROC of $X(z)$ is the set of all complex numbers $z$ for which $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \mid x[n] z^{-n} \mid < \infty$ converges. **Stability Condition via ROC:** An LTI system is **stable** if and only if its ROC **includes the unit circle** $\mid z \mid = 1$. Equivalently, **all poles must lie strictly inside the unit circle**, i.e., $\mid p_k \mid < 1$. **Causality Condition via ROC:** An LTI system is **causal** if and only if its ROC is the **exterior of a circle**, i.e., $\mid z \mid > r_{max}$ for some radius $r_{max}$, and this region includes $z = \infty$. **Combined Condition:** A system is **both causal and stable** iff all poles satisfy $\mid p_k \mid < 1$, and the ROC is $\mid z \mid > \max \mid p_k \mid$. --- #### **Solution to Question A (b):** **Step 1 — Apply Z-transform** with zero initial conditions: $$Y(z) = 0.5 z^{-1} Y(z) + X(z) + 0.5 z^{-1} X(z)$$ **Step 2 — Rearrange** to isolate $Y(z)$: $$Y(z) \left( 1 - 0.5 z^{-1} \right) = X(z) \left( 1 + 0.5 z^{-1} \right)$$ **Step 3 — Transfer function:** $$H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \frac{1 + 0.5 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}}$$ **Step 4 — Impulse response via partial fractions** (rewriting in standard form): $$H(z) = \frac{1 + 0.5 z^{-1}}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}} = 1 + \frac{1}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}}$$ (since $(1 + 0.5 z^{-1}) = (1 - 0.5 z^{-1}) + z^{-1}$, hence $(1 + 0.5 z^{-1})/(1 - 0.5 z^{-1}) = 1 + z^{-1}/(1 - 0.5 z^{-1})$ — using long division): $$H(z) = 1 + \frac{z^{-1}}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}}$$ **Step 5 — Inverse Z-transform** using $\mathcal{Z}^{-1}\{\dfrac{1}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}}\} = (0.5)^n u[n]$ and $\mathcal{Z}^{-1}\{z^{-1} \cdot F(z)\} = f[n-1]u[n-1]$: $$h[n] = \delta[n] + (0.5)^{n-1} u[n-1]$$ **Step 6 — Check Stability and Causality:** - **Causality:** $h[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$ (since both terms are right-sided). ✓ **Causal** - **Stability:** $\sum_{n=0}^{\infty} \mid h[n] \mid = 1 + \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} (0.5)^{n-1} = 1 + \dfrac{1}{1-0.5} = 3 < \infty$. ✓ **Stable** - **Pole location:** Single pole at $z = 0.5$, with $\mid 0.5 \mid < 1$. ✓ Confirms stability. - **ROC:** $\mid z \mid > 0.5$ (includes unit circle and $z = \infty$). ✓ Confirms causal+stable. **Final Verdict:** The system is **BOTH causal and stable**. --- ### **Part B — Question B (Alternative)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` (14 Marks) **(a)** With a suitable example, explain how the **Z-transform characterizes a linear constant-coefficient difference equation (LCCDE)**. Derive the general expression for the transfer function. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A causal LTI system has the impulse response: $$h[n] = \left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^n u[n] + \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^n u[n-3]$$ Find the system function $H(z)$, its ROC, and verify stability and causality. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Solution to Question B (a):** **Definition:** An LCCDE has the form: $$\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k y[n-k] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k x[n-k]$$ **Example:** $y[n] - \frac{3}{4} y[n-1] + \frac{1}{8} y[n-2] = 2 x[n]$ **Step 1:** Applying the Z-transform with zero initial conditions and using $\mathcal{Z}\{x[n-k]\} = z^{-k} X(z)$: $$Y(z) - \frac{3}{4} z^{-1} Y(z) + \frac{1}{8} z^{-2} Y(z) = 2 X(z)$$ **Step 2:** Factoring: $$Y(z) \left( 1 - \frac{3}{4} z^{-1} + \frac{1}{8} z^{-2} \right) = 2 X(z)$$ **Step 3:** Solving: $$H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \frac{2}{1 - \frac{3}{4} z^{-1} + \frac{1}{8} z^{-2}}$$ **General Transfer Function Expression:** $$H(z) = \frac{\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k z^{-k}}{\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k z^{-k}}$$ The zeros are roots of the numerator, and the poles are roots of the denominator. The ROC depends on the pole magnitudes. --- #### **Solution to Question B (b):** **Step 1 — Take Z-transform of each term:** $$\mathcal{Z}\left\{ \left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^n u[n] \right\} = \frac{1}{1 - \frac{1}{3} z^{-1}}, \quad \text{ROC}: \mid z \mid > \frac{1}{3}$$ For the second term, use the time-shift property $\mathcal{Z}\{x[n-3]\} = z^{-3} X(z)$: $$\mathcal{Z}\left\{ \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^n u[n-3] \right\} = z^{-3} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - \frac{1}{2} z^{-1}}, \quad \text{ROC}: \mid z \mid > \frac{1}{2}$$ **Step 2 — Combine:** $$H(z) = \frac{1}{1 - \frac{1}{3} z^{-1}} + \frac{z^{-3}}{1 - \frac{1}{2} z^{-1}}$$ **Step 3 — Determine ROC:** The overall ROC is the intersection: $\mid z \mid > \dfrac{1}{2}$ (the more restrictive region). **Step 4 — Identify Poles:** Poles at $z = \frac{1}{3}$ and $z = \frac{1}{2}$. **Step 5 — Check Causality:** ROC is $\mid z \mid > \dfrac{1}{2}$, which is exterior of a circle and includes $z = \infty$. ✓ **Causal** **Step 6 — Check Stability:** - Unit circle test: $\mid z \mid = 1 > 1/2$, so unit circle lies inside ROC. ✓ - Pole test: $\mid 1/3 \mid = 0.33 < 1$ and $\mid 1/2 \mid = 0.5 < 1$. ✓ **Final Verdict:** The system is **BOTH causal and stable**. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Marks-Deduction Mistakes in KTU Valuation:** > > 1. **Forgetting to specify ROC:** Writing only $H(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 - 0.5 z^{-1}}$ without the ROC. **Loss: 2 marks.** > 2. **Wrong causality criterion:** Saying a system is causal just because $h[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$, without connecting it to ROC ($\mid z \mid > r_{max}$). **Loss: 1–2 marks.** > 3. **Confusing interior and exterior ROCs:** Writing ROC as $\mid z \mid < 1/2$ for a causal right-sided sequence. Always remember: **causal $\Rightarrow$ exterior ROC**. > 4. **Mixing up poles and zeros:** Denominator roots = poles; numerator roots = zeros. Reversing them leads to wrong stability answers. > 5. **Ignoring the time-shift in LCCDE:** When applying $\mathcal{Z}\{y[n-k]\}$, students often write $z^{+k} Y(z)$ instead of $z^{-k} Y(z)$. **Major deduction.** > 6. **Not checking pole magnitudes numerically:** Always substitute and verify $\mid p_k \mid < 1$ explicitly for full marks. > 7. **Skipping zero initial conditions statement:** Always write "Assuming zero initial conditions" before applying the Z-transform to an LCCDE. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:** - **ROC Definition:** Set of $z$ values where Z-transform converges to a finite value. - **ROC is bounded by poles** and never includes a pole. - **Causal system** $\Rightarrow$ ROC is **exterior of a circle** ($\mid z \mid > r_{max}$), includes $z = \infty$. - **Stable system** $\Rightarrow$ ROC **includes the unit circle** $\mid z \mid = 1$. - **Causal + Stable** $\Leftrightarrow$ **All poles strictly inside the unit circle** ($\mid p_k \mid < 1$). - **Left-sided sequence** $\Rightarrow$ ROC is **interior of a circle** ($\mid z \mid < r_{min}$). - **Two-sided sequence** $\Rightarrow$ ROC is an **annulus** ($r_2 < \mid z \mid < r_1$). - **Pole on unit circle** $\Rightarrow$ system is **marginally unstable** (not BIBO stable). - **Pole outside unit circle** $\Rightarrow$ system is **unstable**. - **General transfer function:** $H(z) = \dfrac{\sum b_k z^{-k}}{\sum a_k z^{-k}}$ (from LCCDE under zero ICs). - **Time-shift property** is the most-used tool: $\mathcal{Z}\{f[n-k]\} = z^{-k} F(z)$. - **Impulse response** is the inverse Z-transform of $H(z)$: $h[n] = \mathcal{Z}^{-1}\{H(z)\}$. - **Frequency response** is obtained by $H(e^{j\omega}) = H(z)\big\vert_{z = e^{j\omega}}$ — only valid if unit circle is in ROC. - **Same algebraic $X(z)$ can describe different signals** — ROC disambiguates. - **Always mention ROC, pole locations, and final verdict (stable/causal/neither)** in every KTU answer.

topics.title: Pulse transfer function and impulse response of discrete-time systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEOT522 - INTRODUCTION TO SIGNALS AND SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pulse transfer function and impulse response of discrete-time systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the framework of **Linear Shift-Invariant (LSI)** discrete-time systems governed by the **Z-transform**, the analysis of system behavior is fundamentally anchored by two mathematically equivalent representations:

**(a) Impulse Response — `h[n]`**
The impulse response of a discrete-time LSI system is defined as the output of the system when the input is a unit sample sequence, $\delta[n]$, with all initial conditions set to zero.

$$h[n] \triangleq \mathcal{T}\{\delta[n]\}$$

It is a **time-domain** characterization of the system and completely determines the system because any arbitrary input $x[n]$ produces an output given by the **linear convolution sum**:

$$y[n] = x[n] * h[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k]\, h[n-k]$$

**(b) Pulse Transfer Function — `H(z)`**
The Pulse Transfer Function (PTF) is the **Z-transform** of the impulse response. It is a **Z-domain (frequency-domain equivalent)** representation that algebraically relates the Z-transform of the output to the Z-transform of the input under zero initial conditions:

$$H(z) \triangleq \sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} h[n]\, z^{-n} = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)}\bigg|_{\text{IC} = 0}$$

The two are linked by the elegant duality:

$$\boxed{\,h[n] \;\xleftrightarrow{\;\mathcal{Z}\;}\; H(z)\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Under the **2024 NEP-aligned scheme**, students are expected to:
> - Derive $H(z)$ directly from a Linear Constant-Coefficient Difference Equation (LCCDE).
> - Recover $h[n]$ from $H(z)$ via **partial fraction expansion** and inverse Z-transform lookup tables.
> - Use $h[n]$ and the **Region of Convergence (ROC)** to comment on **causality and stability (BIBO)** of discrete-time systems.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Plain-English Intuition

Imagine a **gymnasium with a single spring-loaded wall** at one end. You throw a ball (a single sharp tap, i.e., $\delta[n]$) at the wall. The wall does not just bounce the ball back instantly — it has **inertia, elasticity, and memory**. The way the wall "rings" after a single tap — decaying oscillations that eventually die out — is the **impulse response** $h[n]$.

Now, the **Pulse Transfer Function $H(z)$** is the engineer's "**signature**" of that wall, written not in time, but in a compressed algebraic form (a ratio of polynomials in $z^{-1}$). Once you have that signature, you can predict — without re-running the experiment — how the wall will respond to **any** sequence of taps (any input $x[n]$) simply by multiplying their "signatures" in the Z-domain and converting back.

| Concept | Real-World Counterpart |
|---|---|
| $\delta[n]$ (unit impulse) | A single sharp tap on the wall |
| $h[n]$ (impulse response) | The "ringing" sound pattern after one tap |
| $H(z)$ (pulse transfer function) | The compressed "fingerprint" / mathematical signature of the wall |
| $x[n] \rightarrow y[n]$ | A sequence of taps produces a complex output |
| ROC of $H(z)$ | The "valid operating envelope" of the system |

> [!NOTE]
> The phrase "**Pulse Transfer Function**" is KTU's preferred terminology for what is often called the "**discrete-time transfer function**" or "**system function**" in international textbooks (Oppenheim, Proakis, Lathi). All three refer to the same quantity $H(z) = Y(z)/X(z)$.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The LCCDE — Starting Point of Analysis

A wide class of causal LSI discrete-time systems is described by a **Linear Constant-Coefficient Difference Equation (LCCDE)** of order $N$:

$$\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k\, y[n-k] = \sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k\, x[n-k]$$

with the convention $a_0 = 1$ (the coefficient of $y[n]$ is normalized to unity). This equation captures how the present output sample $y[n]$ depends linearly on past outputs and on present/past inputs.

### 2.1.1 From LCCDE to Pulse Transfer Function $H(z)$

Taking the Z-transform of both sides of the LCCDE, applying the **time-shift property** $\mathcal{Z}\{y[n-k]\} = z^{-k} Y(z)$ (under zero initial conditions), we get:

$$Y(z)\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k z^{-k} = X(z)\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k z^{-k}$$

Rearranging for the ratio $Y(z)/X(z)$:

$$\boxed{\,H(z) = \frac{Y(z)}{X(z)} = \frac{\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k z^{-k}}{\displaystyle\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k z^{-k}}\,}$$

The numerator polynomial roots are called **zeros**; the denominator polynomial roots are called **poles**.

### 2.1.2 Why Poles & Zeros Matter

- **Poles** dictate the natural modes ($a^n u[n]$, $n a^n u[n]$, $r^n \cos(\omega_0 n)u[n]$, etc.) appearing in $h[n]$.
- **Zeros** modify the relative amplitudes of these modes but do not introduce new ones.
- The **ROC** of $H(z)$ is determined by the pole locations and the causality/stability of the system.

## 2.2 From $H(z)$ to $h[n]$ — Partial Fraction Expansion (PFE)

For **causal LSI systems** (the standard KTU assumption), $h[n]$ is a right-sided sequence. The procedure is:

1. Express $H(z)$ as a proper rational function: $\deg(\text{num}) < \deg(\text{den})$. If not, perform polynomial long division first.
2. **Factor** the denominator to find the poles $p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_N$ (real or complex conjugate pairs).
3. Write $H(z)$ in standard PFE form:

$$H(z) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{A_i}{1 - p_i z^{-1}} \quad \text{(for distinct real poles)}$$

4. Solve for the residues $A_i$:

$$A_i = \left.(1 - p_i z^{-1})\, H(z)\right|_{z = p_i}$$

5. Use the standard Z-transform pair $\mathcal{Z}\{p_i^n u[n]\} = \dfrac{1}{1 - p_i z^{-1}}, \text{ROC: } \vert z \vert > \vert p_i \vert$ to obtain:

$$h[n] = \sum_{i=1}^{N} A_i\, p_i^{\,n}\, u[n]$$

For **repeated poles** of order $r$ at $z = p$, the partial fraction term becomes:

$$\frac{A_{r-1}}{(1-p z^{-1})^{r-1}} + \frac{A_{r-2}}{(1-p z^{-1})^{r-2}} + \cdots + \frac{A_0}{1-p z^{-1}}$$

with inverse transform pairs:

$$\mathcal{Z}\left\{\frac{n!}{(n-m)!}\, p^{n-m}\, u[n]\right\} = \frac{m!}{(1 - p z^{-1})^{m+1}}$$

## 2.3 Stability & Causality via Impulse Response

A discrete LSI system is:

- **Causal** $\iff$ $h[n] = 0$ for $n < 0$ $\iff$ ROC of $H(z)$ is the **exterior** of the outermost pole ($\vert z \vert > r_{\max}$).
- **BIBO Stable** $\iff$ $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[n] \vert < \infty$ $\iff$ **ROC must include the unit circle** $\vert z \vert = 1$.
- **Causal AND Stable** $\iff$ All poles of $H(z)$ lie strictly **inside** the unit circle ($\vert p_i \vert < 1$ for every pole $p_i$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favorite Question Type:** "Given $H(z)$, comment on the stability and causality of the system." The valuation key always awards full marks only if the student explicitly mentions **both** the ROC condition **and** the pole-magnitude test.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Condition |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Convolution sum (time-domain input-output relation) | $y[n] = \sum_{k=-\infty}^{\infty} x[k]\, h[n-k]$ |
| 2 | Pulse Transfer Function definition | $H(z) = Y(z)/X(z)$ under zero IC |
| 3 | LCCDE → $H(z)$ conversion | $H(z) = \dfrac{\sum_{k=0}^{M} b_k z^{-k}}{\sum_{k=0}^{N} a_k z^{-k}}$ |
| 4 | Z-transform ↔ Impulse response | $h[n] \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{Z}} H(z)$ |
| 5 | Causality condition (in time domain) | $h[n] = 0,\; n < 0$ |
| 6 | Causality condition (in Z-domain) | ROC is $\vert z \vert > r_{\max}$ (exterior of outermost pole) |
| 7 | BIBO Stability (time domain) | $\sum_{n=-\infty}^{\infty} \vert h[n] \vert < \infty$ |
| 8 | BIBO Stability (Z-domain) | ROC includes the unit circle $\vert z \vert = 1$ |
| 9 | Causal + Stable condition | All poles satisfy $\vert p_i \vert < 1$ |
| 10 | PFE for distinct real poles | $H(z) = \sum_i \dfrac{A_i}{1 - p_i z^{-1}}$ |
| 11 | Residue formula | $A_i = (1 - p_i z^{-1}) H(z) \big\vert_{z=p_i}$ |
| 12 | Basic Z-transform pair | $a^n u[n] \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{Z}} \dfrac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}$ |
| 13 | Repeated-pole pair | $n\, a^{n-1} u[n] \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{Z}} \dfrac{z^{-1}}{(1 - a z^{-1})^{2}}$ |
| 14 | Complex-pole pair | $r^n \cos(\omega_0 n) u[n] \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{Z}} \dfrac{1 - r\cos\omega_0\, z^{-1}}{1 - 2r\cos\omega_0\, z^{-1} + r^{2} z^{-2}}$ |
| 15 | ROC of $\delta[n]$ | Entire Z-plane |
| 16 | ROC of $a^n u[n]$ | $\vert z \vert > \vert a \vert$ |
| 17 | ROC of $-a^n u[-n-1]$ | $\vert z \vert < \vert a \vert$ |
| 18 | Step response | $s[n] = \sum_{k=0}^{n} h[k] = (h * u)[n]$ |

> **Caution on table syntax:** All set-builder / magnitude conditions above use `\vert` rather than the raw vertical bar `|` to preserve markdown table integrity — a common KTU LaTeX pitfall.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of $H(z)$ and $h[n]$ |
|---|---|
| **Digital Audio / Speech Processing** | Equalizers and noise-cancelling headphones are designed by shaping $H(z)$ to suppress unwanted frequency bands. |
| **Biomedical Signal Processing** | ECG/EEG denoising filters use $h[n]$ of FIR/IIR filters characterized by $H(z)$. |
| **Communication Receivers** | Matched filters for symbol detection use $h[n]$ matched to the pulse shape. |
| **Control Systems (Digital)** | $H(z)$ is the discrete equivalent of $H(s)$ in digital controllers (PLCs, embedded controllers). |
| **Image Processing** | 2-D extensions of $H(z)$ are used in image sharpening and deblurring. |
| **Radar / Sonar** | Matched filtering and clutter rejection rely on impulse response engineering. |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: From LCCDE to $H(z)$

**Statement:** A causal LSI system is described by the second-order LCCDE

$$y[n] - \tfrac{1}{4} y[n-1] - \tfrac{1}{8} y[n-2] = 2 x[n] + 3 x[n-1]$$

Find $H(z)$.

### Step-by-Step Deduction

**Step 1.** Take the unilateral Z-transform of both sides. Apply the linearity property and the time-shift property $\mathcal{Z}\{y[n-k]\} = z^{-k}Y(z) + (\text{initial-condition terms})$. Since all initial conditions are assumed zero, IC terms vanish.

$$Y(z) - \tfrac{1}{4} z^{-1} Y(z) - \tfrac{1}{8} z^{-2} Y(z) = 2 X(z) + 3 z^{-1} X(z)$$

**Step 2.** Group the $Y(z)$ terms on the left and $X(z)$ terms on the right:

$$Y(z) \left[ 1 - \tfrac{1}{4} z^{-1} - \tfrac{1}{8} z^{-2} \right] = X(z) \left[ 2 + 3 z^{-1} \right]$$

**Step 3.** Form the ratio $H(z) = Y(z)/X(z)$:

$$H(z) = \frac{2 + 3 z^{-1}}{1 - \tfrac{1}{4} z^{-1} - \tfrac{1}{8} z^{-2}}$$

**Step 4.** (Optional) Multiply numerator and denominator by $z^{2}$ to obtain positive powers of $z$:

$$H(z) = \frac{2 z^{2} + 3 z}{z^{2} - \tfrac{1}{4} z - \tfrac{1}{8}}$$

**Step 5.** (Optional) Factor the denominator to find poles. Multiply through by 8:

$$8 z^{2} - 2 z - 1 = (4z + 1)(2z - 1) \quad \Longrightarrow \quad z = -\tfrac{1}{4},\; z = \tfrac{1}{2}$$

So $H(z)$ has poles at $z = -1/4$ and $z = 1/2$, and a zero at $z = -3/2$ (from $2z + 3 = 0$).

**Conclusion:** $H(z) = \dfrac{2 + 3 z^{-1}}{(1 + \tfrac{1}{4} z^{-1})(1 - \tfrac{1}{2} z^{-1})}$, with poles strictly inside the unit circle. Therefore the system is **causal and stable**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: From $H(z)$ to $h[n]$ via Partial Fractions

**Statement:** Given the causal LTI system

$$H(z) = \frac{1}{(1 - \tfrac{1}{2} z^{-1})(1 - \tfrac{1}{4} z^{-1})}$$

find the impulse response $h[n]$.

### Step-by-Step Deduction

**Step 1.** Verify that the rational function is **proper** (numerator degree $\leq$ denominator degree). Here, the numerator is the constant 1, the denominator is degree 2, so it is proper. No polynomial division is needed.

**Step 2.** Identify the poles. The denominator vanishes when:

$$1 - \tfrac{1}{2} z^{-1} = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; z = \tfrac{1}{2}, \qquad 1 - \tfrac{1}{4} z^{-1} = 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; z = \tfrac{1}{4}$$

Both poles are real, distinct, and lie inside the unit circle.

**Step 3.** Write the partial-fraction expansion. Since there are two simple real poles:

$$H(z) = \frac{A_1}{1 - \tfrac{1}{2} z^{-1}} + \frac{A_2}{1 - \tfrac{1}{4} z^{-1}}$$

**Step 4.** Compute the residue $A_1$. Multiply both sides by $(1 - \tfrac{1}{2} z^{-1})$ and evaluate at $z = 1/2$:

$$A_1 = \left. \frac{1}{1 - \tfrac{1}{4} z^{-1}} \right|_{z = 1/2} = \frac{1}{1 - \tfrac{1}{4} \cdot 2} = \frac{1}{1 - \tfrac{1}{2}} = \frac{1}{1/2} = 2$$

**Step 5.** Compute the residue $A_2$. Multiply both sides by $(1 - \tfrac{1}{4} z^{-1})$ and evaluate at $z = 1/4$:

$$A_2 = \left. \frac{1}{1 - \tfrac{1}{2} z^{-1}} \right|_{z = 1/4} = \frac{1}{1 - \tfrac{1}{2} \cdot 4} = \frac{1}{1 - 2} = \frac{1}{-1} = -1$$

**Step 6.** Substitute back into the PFE form:

$$H(z) = \frac{2}{1 - \tfrac{1}{2} z^{-1}} - \frac{1}{1 - \tfrac{1}{4} z^{-1}}$$

**Step 7.** Apply the standard Z-transform pair $\mathcal{Z}\{a^n u[n]\} = \dfrac{1}{1 - a z^{-1}}$ to each term (causality is assumed, so ROC is $\vert z \vert > 1/2$):

$$h[n] = 2 \left(\tfrac{1}{2}\right)^{n} u[n] - \left(\tfrac{1}{4}\right)^{n} u[n]$$

**Step 8.** Quick sanity check for $n = 0$: $h[0] = 2(1) - 1(1) = 1$. Verify directly from $H(z)$: the inverse-Z sum-of-residues method or the recursive LCCDE both give $h[0] = 1$. ✓

**Conclusion:** $\boxed{\,h[n] = \left[2 (1/2)^{n} - (1/4)^{n}\right] u[n]\,}$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Worked Example with Complex Poles

**Statement:** Determine $h[n]$ of the causal system

$$H(z) = \frac{1}{1 - z^{-1} + \tfrac{1}{2} z^{-2}}$$

and comment on stability.

### Step-by-Step Deduction

**Step 1.** Find the poles by solving $1 - z^{-1} + \tfrac{1}{2} z^{-2} = 0$. Multiply by $z^{2}$:

$$z^{2} - z + \tfrac{1}{2} = 0$$

**Step 2.** Apply the quadratic formula:

$$z = \frac{1 \pm \sqrt{1 - 2}}{2} = \frac{1 \pm j}{2}$$

**Step 3.** Convert to polar form. The magnitude is:

$$\vert z \vert = \frac{\sqrt{1^{2} + 1^{2}}}{2} = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.7071$$

The argument is $\pm \pi/4$.

**Step 4.** Stability check: both poles have magnitude $1/\sqrt{2} < 1$, so the system is **causal and stable**.

**Step 5.** Write the denominator in the standard second-order form $1 - 2r\cos\omega_0\, z^{-1} + r^{2} z^{-2}$ where $r = 1/\sqrt{2}$ and $\omega_0 = \pi/4$. Verify:

$$2r\cos\omega_0 = 2 \cdot \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = 1 \quad\checkmark \qquad r^{2} = \tfrac{1}{2}\quad\checkmark$$

**Step 6.** Use the standard Z-transform pair for causal damped sinusoids:

$$r^{n} \cos(\omega_0 n) u[n] \xleftrightarrow{\mathcal{Z}} \frac{1 - r\cos\omega_0\, z^{-1}}{1 - 2r\cos\omega_0\, z^{-1} + r^{2} z^{-2}}$$

Since the numerator of $H(z)$ is exactly $1 - r\cos\omega_0\, z^{-1} = 1 - (1/\sqrt{2})(\sqrt{2}/2) z^{-1} = 1 - (1/2) z^{-1}$? Wait — re-check.

Actually, $r\cos\omega_0 = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2} = \tfrac{1}{2}$. So $1 - r\cos\omega_0\, z^{-1} = 1 - \tfrac{1}{2} z^{-1}$. This does **not** match our numerator (which is 1). So we must do a full PFE.

**Step 7.** Full PFE with complex poles. By symmetry (real system), residues are complex conjugates:

$$H(z) = \frac{A}{1 - \tfrac{1+j}{2} z^{-1}} + \frac{A^{*}}{1 - \tfrac{1-j}{2} z^{-1}}$$

**Step 8.** Compute $A$:

$$A = \left.\frac{1}{1 - \tfrac{1-j}{2} z^{-1}}\right|_{z = (1+j)/2} = \frac{1}{1 - \tfrac{1-j}{2}\cdot \tfrac{2}{1+j}} = \frac{1}{1 - \tfrac{1-j}{1+j}} = \frac{1}{1 - \tfrac{(1-j)(1-j)}{(1+j)(1-j)}} = \frac{1}{1 - \tfrac{(1-j)^{2}}{2}}$$

Compute $(1-j)^{2} = 1 - 2j + j^{2} = -2j$. So:

$$A = \frac{1}{1 - \tfrac{-2j}{2}} = \frac{1}{1 + j} = \frac{1-j}{2}$$

**Step 9.** Write $A$ in polar form. Magnitude: $\vert A \vert = \tfrac{\sqrt{2}}{2} = 1/\sqrt{2}$. Argument: $\arg(1-j) = -\pi/4$.

So $A = \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} e^{-j\pi/4}$.

**Step 10.** Use the damped-sinusoid identity: for $H(z) = \dfrac{A}{1 - p z^{-1}} + \dfrac{A^{*}}{1 - p^{*} z^{-1}}$ with $p = re^{j\omega_0}$, the inverse Z-transform is:

$$h[n] = 2 \vert A \vert\, r^{n} \cos(\omega_0 n + \arg A) u[n]$$

**Step 11.** Substitute values: $\vert A \vert = 1/\sqrt{2}$, $r = 1/\sqrt{2}$, $\omega_0 = \pi/4$, $\arg A = -\pi/4$.

$$h[n] = 2 \cdot \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \cdot \left(\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{n} \cos\!\left(\tfrac{\pi}{4} n - \tfrac{\pi}{4}\right) u[n] = \sqrt{2} \left(\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\right)^{n} \cos\!\left(\tfrac{\pi}{4}(n-1)\right) u[n]$$

**Conclusion:** $h[n] = \sqrt{2} \cdot 2^{-n/2} \cos\!\left(\tfrac{\pi}{4}(n-1)\right) u[n]$, an exponentially decaying oscillation. Since $\vert p \vert < 1$, the system is **stable**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — System Behavior Summary

| Pole Location | Mode in $h[n]$ | Magnitude Behavior | System Implication |
|---|---|---|---|
| Real, $0 < p < 1$ | $p^{n} u[n]$ | Decays to **0** | Stable if all poles satisfy this |
| Real, $p > 1$ | $p^{n} u[n]$ | Grows without bound | **Unstable** |
| Real, $-1 < p < 0$ | $p^{n} u[n]$ | Alternating sign, decays | Stable |
| Real, $p < -1$ | $p^{n} u[n]$ | Alternating sign, grows | **Unstable** |
| Complex, $\vert p \vert < 1$ | $r^{n} \cos(\omega_0 n + \phi) u[n]$ | Decaying oscillation | **Stable** |
| Complex, $\vert p \vert = 1$ | $\cos(\omega_0 n + \phi) u[n]$ | Sustained oscillation | **Marginally stable** |
| Complex, $\vert p \vert > 1$ | $r^{n} \cos(\omega_0 n + \phi) u[n]$ | Growing oscillation | **Unstable** |
| Repeated pole, $\vert p \vert < 1$ | $n p^{n} u[n]$ | Still decays (polynomial × exponential) | Stable |
| Repeated pole, $\vert p \vert = 1$ | $n u[n]$ | Grows linearly | **Unstable** |

flowchart TB

    FindPoles --> CheckCausality{System causal?}

    CheckCausality -->|No, ROC interior| ComputeROC2[ROC: mod z less than r min]
    ComputeROC --> CheckUC[Does ROC include unit circle mod z equals 1]
    ComputeROC2 --> CheckUC
    CheckUC -->|Yes AND causal| Stable[Causal AND BIBO Stable]
    CheckUC -->|No| Unstable[Not BIBO Stable]

```
